Name: Disable Auto-Shrink on Databases to Prevent Fragmentation and Performance Degradation
Description: The auto-shrink feature in SQL Server databases is a widely recognized anti-pattern and should be disabled on all production systems. When enabled, it forces the database engine to repeatedly shrink data files by releasing unused space back to the file system.
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER DATABASE [name] SET AUTO_SHRINK OFF for every user database. Disable any maintenance plan shrink tasks. Verify with SELECT name, is_auto_shrink_on FROM sys.databases.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Disable Auto-Shrink on Databases to Prevent Fragmentation and Performance Degradation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Auto-shrink is the most common self-inflicted performance wound in SQL Server. It fragments indexes globally in exchange for temporary disk space.

Name: Disable auto-close on all databases
Description: The AUTO_CLOSE database option, when enabled, automatically shuts down a database after the last connection is terminated. While this feature might be useful for isolated or temporary desktop databases, it severely degrades performance in production environments and high-traffic applications.
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER DATABASE [name] SET AUTO_CLOSE OFF for every database. This is a one-line change with zero app impact and immediate latency elimination.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Disable auto-close on all databases' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Auto-close forces a cold database start for every new connection. Users experience multi-second delays where milliseconds are expected.

Name: Set PAGE_VERIFY = CHECKSUM on all databases
Description:
This check ensures that the PAGE_VERIFY database option is set to CHECKSUM on all production databases. This setting is a critical data integrity measure designed to detect page-level corruption, often referred to as "silent corruption."

Silent corruption occurs when physical I/O subsystem failures or hardware malfunctions alter data pages without triggering immediate errors. While TORN_PAGE_DETECTION detects specific types of physical damage, CHECKSUM provides the most protection by calculating and verifying checksums over the entire page content during read operations. Failure to enable this setting leaves the database vulnerable to undetected data loss during reads or writes, potentially leading to severe business disruption.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Set PAGE_VERIFY = CHECKSUM on all databases' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Set PAGE_VERIFY = CHECKSUM on all databases' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Optimize TempDB File Count to Prevent Allocation Contention
Description: This check validates the optimal configuration of TempDB data files within SQL Server. TempDB is used for temporary objects, such as sort operations and temp tables, which are critical components of many queries. Configuring multiple, equally sized TempDB data files significantly reduces allocation page contention (specifically PAGELATCH_UP waits), thereby preventing performance bottlenecks on multi-core systems.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Optimize TempDB File Count to Prevent Allocation Contention' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Optimize TempDB File Count to Prevent Allocation Contention' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Set tempdb autogrowth to fixed MB (e.g., 512MB or 1GB), not percentage
Description: The autogrowth settings for the system TempDB database use fixed Megabyte (MB) increments rather than percentage-based growth. Using fixed MB values makes sure predictable Input/Output (I/O) patterns, preventing sudden and unpredictable file expansion which can lead to performance degradation, resource contention, and potential.
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER DATABASE tempdb MODIFY FILE (NAME='tempdev', FILEGROWTH=512MB) and repeat for each TempDB data file. Set log file to 256MB fixed growth.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Set tempdb autogrowth to fixed MB (e.g., 512MB or 1GB), not percentage' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: TempDB contention is the #1 cause of intermittent application timeouts. Fixing this removes a recurring source of user-facing slowness.

Name: Enable Trace Flag 174 to increase plan cache bucket count and reduce SOS_CACHESTORE contention
Description: This check validates the configuration of Trace Flag 174, which increases the number of plan cache buckets in SQL Server. When the default plan cache limit is reached on high-performance systems, low-cost query plans can be evicted to make room for new ones, leading to severe SOS_CACHESTORE spinlock contention and significant CPU usage spikes. Enabling this flag mitigates this performance bottleneck by increasing the available cache size.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable Trace Flag 174 to increase plan cache bucket count and reduce SOS_CACHESTORE contention' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Trace Flag 174 to increase plan cache bucket count and reduce SOS_CACHESTORE contention' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'access check cache bucket count'. Controls hash bucket count for the security descriptor cache (tokenandpermissionshashedbycombination). Default 0 (auto). Non-zero values are rarely justified. Microsoft KB articles have occasionally recommended specific values for high-permission-check scenarios.
FullStoryAction: Access Check Cache Bucket Count. The option controls the number of hash buckets that are used for the access check result cache.

Name: Monitor Query Store Cleanup Health (KB3178297) — Prevent Disk Exhaustion
Description: This check validates the operational health of the Query Store automatic data cleanup mechanism. When this feature fails, particularly on certain SQL Server editions (e.g., non-Enterprise/Developer 2016 RTM), the Query Store size can grow unchecked. Uncontrolled growth leads to potential disk space exhaustion and memory dumps filling up error logs. Continuous failure degrades performance monitoring capabilities by potentially forcing the Query Store into a read-only state.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Monitor Query Store Cleanup Health (KB3178297) - Prevent Disk Exhaustion' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Monitor Query Store Cleanup Health (KB3178297) — Prevent Disk Exhaustion' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

ValidationSources.source: Microsoft SQL Server 2016 CU1 Release Notes
Brent Ozar SQL Server First Responder Kit Issue #352
Microsoft Learn - Query Store Best Practices

CheckTriggeredSimplified: Query Store Cleanup Disabled On @ server(s)
NextAction: sys.databases where is_query_store_on = 1 and query_store_state = 'READ_ONLY' because max_storage_size is reached. Query Store stops capturing new data when the size limit is reached. Check: SELECT * FROM sys.database_query_store_options. Fix: increase MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB or set CLEANUP_POLICY (STALE_QUERY_THRESHOLD_DAYS). Run: ALTER DATABASE [db] SET QUERY_STORE CLEAR to flush old data if necessary.
FullStoryAction: SQL 2016 RTM has a bug involving dumps that happen every time Query Store cleanup jobs run. This is fixed in CU1 and later.

Name: Verify SQL Server Memory Configuration - Min/Max Server Memory Settings
Description: This check validates that the minimum and maximum server memory settings (min server memory (MB) and max server memory (MB)) are configured optimally. Proper configuration is vital for allowing SQL Server to dynamically manage memory resources, preventing resource starvation of the operating system or other critical services. Best practices recommend setting min server memory to 0 MB (or a very low value) and max server memory to approximately 85-90% of the physical system's RAM. Setting min equal to max can lock memory allocation, severely limiting performance and stability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Verify SQL Server Memory Configuration - Min/Max Server Memory Settings' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verify SQL Server Memory Configuration - Min/Max Server Memory Settings' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Ensure Fixed Paging File Size is Set for Predictable Performance
Description: This check validates that the SQL Server instance is configured with a fixed, adequately sized paging file rather than allowing automatic management. Allowing the page file to grow automatically can lead to unpredictable I/O latency spikes and performance degradation during critical operations. Setting a fixed size based on physical memory recommendations ensures consistent resource availability and predictable performance for optimal database operation.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure Fixed Paging File Size is Set for Predictable Performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure Fixed Paging File Size is Set for Predictable Performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

ValidationSources.source: Microsoft Learn - Windows Server Failover Clustering
Microsoft Learn - Always On Failover Cluster Instances

CheckTriggeredSimplified: Max File Size Set for @ database files
CheckClearSimplified: No database files have Max File Size Set
NextAction: sys.master_files where max_size > 0 (max file size configured). A maximum file size cap silently stops the database from growing when it reaches the limit , causing 'Could not allocate space' errors that immediately impact the application. Remove max size restrictions: ALTER DATABASE [db] MODIFY FILE (NAME='...', MAXSIZE=UNLIMITED).
FullStoryAction: If this space runs out, the database will stop working even though there may be drive space available.

Name: Configure Windows Power Plan to High Performance for SQL Server Availability
Description: This check validates that the underlying operating system power plan is set to "High Performance" (or equivalent) for the server hosting SQL Server. The default "Balanced" or energy-saving plans can dynamically throttle CPU frequency during periods of perceived low utilization, leading to unpredictable latency spikes and degraded query performance in resource-intensive OLTP workloads. Setting the plan to High Performance ensures consistent, predictable throughput by maintaining maximum CPU clock speeds, which is critical for stable SQL Server availability and optimal transaction processing.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Configure Windows Power Plan to High Performance for SQL Server Availability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Configure Windows Power Plan to High Performance for SQL Server Availability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some SQL server(s) are not running in High Performance mode
CheckClearSimplified: All Servers are in High Performance mode
NextAction: Reads Windows power scheme via registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings or WMI. Balanced and Power Saver plans use CPU frequency scaling (C-states and P-states), capping clock speed and adding latency to CPU state transitions. SQL Server requires High Performance power plan to allow maximum CPU frequency at all times. This is a commonly missed configuration on VMs where the hypervisor power policy also matters.
FullStoryAction: The current count of servers not running in High-Performance power scheme

Name: Verify Applied State of Critical SQL Server Configurations
Description: This check validates that critical SQL Server configuration options, which may have been modified using sp_configure, are fully applied and reflect the intended state. Configuration drift occurs when a setting is changed but not properly activated (e.g., forgetting to run RECONFIGURE). Failure to validate this can lead to severe security gaps, operational instability, or non-compliance with established hardening baselines.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Verify Applied State of Critical SQL Server Configurations' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verify Applied State of Critical SQL Server Configurations' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Restrict or Disable the Default sa Login Account
Description: Verifies the sa account is disabled and renamed. Default-named sa is the #1 brute-force target in every breach report and every audit framework requires it to be locked down.
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER LOGIN sa DISABLE. Then rename it: ALTER LOGIN sa WITH NAME='sql_sa_disabled_do_not_use'. Verify with SELECT name, is_disabled FROM sys.server_principals WHERE principal_id=1.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Restrict or Disable the Default sa Login Account' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Required by PCI-DSS, CIS, NIST, and every security framework. An enabled sa account is an automatic audit finding with a 30-day remediation deadline.

Name: Detect special characters (spaces, brackets, dashes, semicolons, quotes)
Description: This check validates that database objects (tables, views, stored procedures, etc.) adhere to strict naming conventions by avoiding special characters such as spaces, brackets, dashes, dots, commas, and semicolons. Using non-alphanumeric identifiers forces the use of delimiters (e.g., `[]`) in T-SQL code, which significantly increases code complexity, reduces readability, and introduces potential syntax error risks during development and maintenance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect special characters (spaces, brackets, dashes, semicolons, quotes)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect special characters (spaces, brackets, dashes, semicolons, quotes)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect non-standard characters in database object names (columns/tables)
Description: This check validates that database object names (especially column names) adhere to strict naming conventions, avoiding special characters like spaces, dashes, or semicolons. Using non-alphanumeric characters requires complex bracket delimitation (`[ ]`) in all T-SQL code, significantly increasing the risk of syntax errors and reducing developer productivity. Adopting a standard convention using only alphanumeric characters and underscores improves readability, simplifies ORM compatibility, and strengthens overall database maintainability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect non-standard characters in database object names (columns/tables)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect non-standard characters in database object names (columns/tables)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Identify Use of Reserved Keywords as Object Names for Code Clarity and Compatibility
Description: This check validates that database objects (tables, columns, views, etc.) do not use SQL Server reserved keywords as their names. While T-SQL syntax allows using reserved words by wrapping them in delimited identifiers (square brackets), this practice severely degrades code readability and maintainability. Adhering to naming conventions that avoid reserved keywords is a critical best practice for ensuring long-term stability, reducing the risk of syntax errors, and improving developer onboarding efficiency across complex applications.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Identify Use of Reserved Keywords as Object Names for Code Clarity and Compatibility' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Identify Use of Reserved Keywords as Object Names for Code Clarity and Compatibility' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect tables with zero indexes — pure heaps that severely degrade performance
Description: This check validates that user tables are properly indexed, ensuring they do not function as pure heaps (tables without a clustered index). Heaps force SQL Server to perform full table scans for data retrieval operations, which drastically degrades performance and increases resource consumption on large datasets. Implementing appropriate indexes is crucial for optimizing query execution plans, improving join efficiency, and maintaining system stability in production environments, aligning with PCI-DSS requirements for secure configuration.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect tables with zero indexes - pure heaps that severely degrade performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect tables with zero indexes — pure heaps that severely degrade performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) — waste storage, memory, and write performance
Description: Duplicate indexes are defined as having identical key column sets within a single table. Maintaining redundant indexes significantly degrades write performance because every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operation must update all duplicate index structures, leading to unnecessary I/O overhead. They waste storage space and can confuse the query optimizer, potentially causing plan variability or forcing suboptimal execution plans. Best practice mandates consolidating these into unique constraints or carefully designed covering indexes to maintain data integrity while optimizing performance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) - waste storage, memory, and write performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) — waste storage, memory, and write performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Ensure Clustered Indexes are Unique for Optimal Performance
Description: This check validates that clustered indexes are defined on unique columns. When a clustered index is built on non-unique columns, SQL Server automatically appends a 4-byte integer uniqueifier to every duplicate key value. While this mechanism ensures data integrity, the resulting overhead significantly increases the physical storage size of both the clustered and non-clustered indexes. This practice can lead to substantial performance degradation, including increased I/O operations, poor query execution times, and accelerated index fragmentation (Simple Talk). For optimal database efficiency, minimizing uniqueifier usage is a critical configuration best practice.|
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure Clustered Indexes are Unique for Optimal Performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure Clustered Indexes are Unique for Optimal Performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: 19 Use Windows Authentication over SQL Authentication
Description: Detection check extracted from check_19_Use_Windows_Authentication_over_SQL_Authentication.yaml.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding '19 Use Windows Authentication over SQL Authentication' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of '19 Use Windows Authentication over SQL Authentication' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Set max server memory to 85% of total RAM (min 0%) to prevent OS resource exhaustion
Description: The maximum server memory setting must be explicitly configured to prevent the operating system (OS) from experiencing memory pressure. Leaving this value at its default, effectively unlimited state, can cause SQL Server to consume excessive physical RAM. This leads to OS instability, increased paging activity, and potential performance degradation for all services running on the host machine.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Set max server memory to 85% of total RAM (min 0%) to prevent OS resource exhaustion' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Set max server memory to 85% of total RAM (min 0%) to prevent OS resource exhaustion' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'min server memory (MB)' > 0. When min memory is set, SQL Server will not release memory below this floor even under OS memory pressure. Setting min = max is a best practice to prevent buffer pool eviction by other processes. Confirm the value is appropriate for the server's dedicated SQL role and available physical memory.
FullStoryAction: min server memory (MB). Guarantees a minimum amount of memory available to the SQL Server Memory Manager

Name: Detect rarely used or redundant indexes to reduce write overhead and improve performance
Description: Unused or redundant indexes significantly increase write I/O overhead during INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations (write amplification), consume disk space, and slow down backup times. While monitoring views like sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats can identify low-read indexes, care must be taken as these statistics can reset after server restarts or plan changes. Best practice involves evaluating the read/write ratio against the index's selectivity to determine if its maintenance cost justifies its query benefit. Before dropping any index, ensure it is not referenced by critical queries, views, or foreign keys.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect rarely used or redundant indexes to reduce write overhead and improve performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect rarely used or redundant indexes to reduce write overhead and improve performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect Untrusted Constraints (WITH NOCHECK) and Performance Risks
Description: This check identifies SQL Server constraints (FOREIGN KEY, CHECK) that have been enabled using WITH NOCHECK. When a constraint is created or altered with this option, the database engine does not validate existing data against the rule and marks the constraint as untrusted. This has two major risks: first, the query optimizer may ignore these constraints for cardinality estimation, potentially leading to suboptimal execution plans; second, it compromises data integrity by allowing invalid data that violates defined business rules at insert or update time. Maintaining trusted constraints is crucial for both performance optimization and regulatory compliance (e.g., GDPR, SOX).
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Untrusted Constraints (WITH NOCHECK) and Performance Risks' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Untrusted Constraints (WITH NOCHECK) and Performance Risks' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Review PARAMETRIZATION database option for optimal performance and stability
Description: The PARAMETRIZATION database option controls how SQL Server handles literal values in queries. When set to FORCED, all queries are treated as parameterized, which can improve plan reuse and reduce compilation overhead but risks suboptimal plans due to parameter sniffing or cardinality estimate mismatches for ad-hoc queries (Plan Cache Bloat). Best practice generally recommends using SIMPLE, allowing the optimizer flexibility while maintaining performance benefits.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Review PARAMETRIZATION database option for optimal performance and stability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Review PARAMETRIZATION database option for optimal performance and stability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect user-defined functions using reserved prefixes (e.g., fn_) to prevent naming conflicts with system functions
Description: This check validates that user-defined functions (UDFs) do not use reserved prefixes, such as 'fn_', which are designated for system functions in SQL Server. Using reserved or system-like prefixes can lead to naming conflicts, ambiguity, and potential instability if Microsoft modifies or deprecates the underlying system function names. Best practices recommend using distinct, non-reserved prefixes (e.g., 'udf_' or a custom schema prefix) to ensure long-term stability and adherence to established coding conventions.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect user-defined functions using reserved prefixes (e.g., fn_) to prevent naming conflicts with system functions' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect user-defined functions using reserved prefixes (e.g., fn_) to prevent naming conflicts with system functions' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enforce Strong Password Policies and Modern Authentication for SQL Logins
Description:
This check validates that SQL Server logins are protected by strong, complex passwords and that authentication policies are enforced. Weak or blank passwords significantly increase the risk of brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and unauthorized access to sensitive data.

Best practice dictates prioritizing Windows Authentication or Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) authentication methods over traditional SQL logins whenever possible, as these integrate with centralized identity providers and eliminate local password management risks. Policies must enforce minimum length, complexity, and regular rotation of passwords at the time of login creation.
SqlQuery: SELECT 'SKIP' AS result, 'Password policy check escalates to OPUS_REVIEW.md — use xp_logininfo, CHECK_POLICY, and CHECK_EXPIRATION instead.' AS message;
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enforce Strong Password Policies and Modern Authentication for SQL Logins' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enforce Strong Password Policies and Modern Authentication for SQL Logins' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect Unauthorized or Excessive Use of DBCC FREEPROCCACHE
Description: This check monitors for the unauthorized or unnecessary execution of the DBCC FREEPROCCACHE command. This command clears the entire procedure (plan) cache, forcing SQL Server to recompile all cached queries and plans. While this can be useful for specific troubleshooting scenarios like parameter sniffing testing, frequent or indiscriminate use causes a significant performance hit due to high CPU usage and plan recompilation overhead across multiple databases.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Unauthorized or Excessive Use of DBCC FREEPROCCACHE' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Unauthorized or Excessive Use of DBCC FREEPROCCACHE' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Ensure Incremental Statistics Creation for Partitioned Tables
Description: This check validates that the database is configured to maintain auto-created column statistics incrementally on partitioned tables. When this setting is enabled, updating statistics does not require scanning the entire table, which significantly reduces I/O overhead and resource consumption. Maintaining optimal statistics is a critical performance best practice, as poor statistics can lead to inefficient query plans, potentially causing service degradation or denial of service in large-scale environments.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure Incremental Statistics Creation for Partitioned Tables' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure Incremental Statistics Creation for Partitioned Tables' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: 20 Apply Principle of Least Privilege
Description: Detection check extracted from check_20_Apply_Principle_of_Least_Privilege.yaml.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding '20 Apply Principle of Least Privilege' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of '20 Apply Principle of Least Privilege' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect stored procedures configured to run at SQL Server startup — Review for Security and Persistence Risks
Description: Stored procedures configured to execute automatically when SQL Server starts up (via the 'scan for startup procs' configuration option) pose a significant security risk. These procedures run with elevated permissions, often before full authentication and authorization contexts are established. If undocumented or unnecessary code is present, it can create hidden persistence mechanisms, enabling unauthorized actions or backdoors even if other security layers are intact. Reviewing these procedures is critical for maintaining the integrity of the SQL Server instance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect stored procedures configured to run at SQL Server startup - Review for Security and Persistence Risks' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect stored procedures configured to run at SQL Server startup — Review for Security and Persistence Risks' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Stored Procedures Run at SQL Start-up
CheckClearSimplified: No Stored Procedure Run at SQL Start-up
NextAction: sys.objects with startup extended stored procedure (XP) configuration. Startup stored procedures execute automatically at instance start with sysadmin privileges. A malicious actor who can create/modify startup procs gains persistent code execution. Audit: EXEC sp_procoption @ProcName=N'<proc>', @OptionName='startup', @OptionValue='true' , remove any undocumented startup procs.
FullStoryAction: These procedures could pose a security risk.

Name: Detect enabled server-level triggers (logon triggers) — reduce attack surface and overhead
Description: Server-level triggers, especially `logon` triggers, execute for every new connection established to the instance. If these triggers are poorly written, inefficient, or unnecessary, they can introduce significant performance overhead, degrade overall system responsiveness, or even block all connections (leading to a Denial of Service condition). Best practice dictates that non-system server triggers should be disabled unless strictly required for critical security monitoring; in such cases, Microsoft recommends using dedicated auditing mechanisms like Extended Events instead. Reviewing and disabling unused or unnecessary server triggers is crucial for maintaining system stability and reducing the overall attack surface.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect enabled server-level triggers (logon triggers) - reduce attack surface and overhead' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect enabled server-level triggers (logon triggers) — reduce attack surface and overhead' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect DATE_CORRELATION_OPTIMIZATION Enabled (Potential Performance Overhead)
Description: This check validates the status of DATE_CORRELATION_OPTIMIZATION, a database option that maintains statistics showing relationships between date fields in two tables. Enabling this feature can introduce significant performance overhead and may cause the Query Optimizer to make incorrect assumptions about data correlation, potentially leading to suboptimal query plans and degraded application performance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect DATE_CORRELATION_OPTIMIZATION Enabled (Potential Performance Overhead)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect DATE_CORRELATION_OPTIMIZATION Enabled (Potential Performance Overhead)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Validate MSDB integrity and detect unmanaged transactions for robust recovery
Description: This check validates two critical SQL Server configuration areas. First, it identifies user-created tables within the MSDB system database that are not automatically included in standard backup routines, posing a significant risk of data loss during disaster recovery or upgrades. Second, it monitors for active implicit transactions (where @@TRANCOUNT is non-zero), which can lead to severe blocking issues, increased log growth, and deadlocks, compromising overall database stability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Validate MSDB integrity and detect unmanaged transactions for recovery' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Validate MSDB integrity and detect unmanaged transactions for robust recovery' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Maintain Cleanliness of the Model Database to Prevent Schema Drift and Attack Surface Expansion
Description: The Model Database acts as a template for all newly created databases on an instance. If unauthorized or non-standard user-created objects (like custom stored procedures, tables, or functions) reside in this model database, they are automatically copied to every new database. This practice can lead to schema drift, unexpected security vulnerabilities, and unnecessary attack surface expansion across the entire environment.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Maintain Cleanliness of the Model Database to Prevent Schema Drift and Attack Surface Expansion' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Maintain Cleanliness of the Model Database to Prevent Schema Drift and Attack Surface Expansion' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Tables in the Model Database
NextAction: sys.objects in model database. Objects in model are copied to every new database created. Unintended objects in model propagate to all future databases, creating unexpected schema and potentially sensitive data in new databases. Audit and clean model regularly.
FullStoryAction: Tables in the model database are automatically copied into all new databases

Name: Detect DML Triggers on User Tables — Hidden Complexity, Performance Risk, and Security Surface Expansion
Description: DML triggers execute automatically upon INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operations on user tables. While they are useful for enforcing complex business logic, unmanaged or poorly written triggers introduce significant hidden complexity and performance overhead. From a security perspective, they can expand the attack surface by executing code under specific caller permissions, potentially leading to unauthorized data modification or privilege escalation if not strictly controlled.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect DML Triggers on User Tables - Hidden Complexity, Performance Risk, and Security Surface Expansion' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect DML Triggers on User Tables — Hidden Complexity, Performance Risk, and Security Surface Expansion' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.triggers joined to sys.objects. Triggers add hidden overhead to DML operations , they fire within the transaction, increasing lock hold duration. Multiple nested triggers can cause deadlocks. Auditing triggers (logging every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) are a common source of blocking on high-throughput tables. Review trigger logic for set-based vs cursor-based patterns.
FullStoryAction: It is important to note triggers for performance considerations

Name: Detect Enterprise Edition Feature Usage for Compatibility and Licensing Compliance
Description:
This check validates the usage of advanced, Enterprise Edition-specific features (such as Columnstore Indexes, Table Partitioning, or In-Memory OLTP) within databases. It is critical because utilizing these features on a lower edition (like Standard) can lead to operational failures, data loss during restore operations, or severe licensing non-compliance if the database environment is downgraded.
Monitoring this usage ensures that architectural decisions are documented and maintained across planned version upgrades or edition changes, protecting data integrity and business continuity.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Enterprise Edition Feature Usage for Compatibility and Licensing Compliance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Enterprise Edition Feature Usage for Compatibility and Licensing Compliance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) for Data-at-Rest Protection
Description:
This check validates that Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) is enabled on user databases. TDE encrypts the physical database files (.mdf/.ldf) and transaction logs at rest using a Database Encryption Key (DEK). This ensures that stolen backup media or detached database files cannot be read without the certificate and private key used to protect the DEK. TDE provides the highest-impact data-at-rest encryption with near-zero application changes.

TDE does not protect data in transit (use TLS) or data accessible to users with explicit permissions on the running instance. It also does not encrypt data in memory or in the buffer pool. However, it is a foundational compliance requirement for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, and SOC2 for protecting stored data. Microsoft recommends enabling TDE on all databases containing sensitive data, with backups of the certificate and key stored in a secure, off-site location.

This check queries sys.dm_database_encryption_keys joined to sys.databases to count user databases with encryption_state = 3 (encrypted). It reports PASS if one or more have TDE, FAIL if none use TDE.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) for Data-at-Rest Protection' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) for Data-at-Rest Protection' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Ensure Transaction Log Files are Separated from Data/Other Logs for Optimal I/O Performance
Description: This check validates that a database's transaction log files are physically separated from data files and other logs. SQL Server writes to the transaction log sequentially, meaning placing multiple log files on the same physical drive does not provide I/O parallelism benefits and significantly increases Virtual Log File (VLF) complexity. Best practice dictates separating these files onto dedicated, high-speed storage volumes to ensure optimal performance, efficient backup/restore operations, and simplified maintenance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure Transaction Log Files are Separated from Data/Other Logs for Optimal I/O Performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure Transaction Log Files are Separated from Data/Other Logs for Optimal I/O Performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Verify Consistent Auto-Growth Settings Across Filegroup Data Files
Description: This check validates that all data files within a single filegroup use consistent auto-growth settings (e.g., same growth amount or mixed MB/percent). When disparate growth strategies are used, SQL Server's proportional fill algorithm struggles to distribute I/O evenly across the underlying physical storage. This imbalance can lead to one file growing disproportionately larger, creating an I/O hotspot and causing significant performance degradation and potential fragmentation. Maintaining consistent settings is a critical configuration best practice for ensuring optimal database throughput and stability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Verify Consistent Auto-Growth Settings Across Filegroup Data Files' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verify Consistent Auto-Growth Settings Across Filegroup Data Files' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect and Clean Up Hypothetical Indexes Left Behind by Tuning Tools
Description: Hypothetical indexes (where is_hypothetical = 1) are artifacts generated by performance tuning tools, such as the Database Tuning Advisor or Index Tuning Wizard. While these indexes help analyze potential improvements, they do not contain real data and are ignored by the query optimizer during production execution. Leaving them behind contributes to database clutter, increases metadata overhead, and complicates maintenance efforts. Best practice dictates that these artifacts must be explicitly dropped after tuning analysis is complete to maintain a clean and optimized database schema.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect and Clean Up Hypothetical Indexes Left Behind by Tuning Tools' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect and Clean Up Hypothetical Indexes Left Behind by Tuning Tools' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect SQL Server Agent jobs configured to run at startup — Security and Performance Risk
Description: The check validates that no unnecessary or overly privileged SQL Server Agent jobs are scheduled to execute automatically upon service startup (freq_type = 64). Such jobs can significantly delay instance recovery time, impacting the Recovery Time Objective (RTO), consume excessive CPU/memory resources during critical startup phases, and introduce potential security vulnerabilities if they run with elevated permissions. Regular review is required to ensure only essential, non-disruptive maintenance tasks are configured, adhering strictly to the principle of least privilege for service accounts.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect SQL Server Agent jobs configured to run at startup - Security and Performance Risk' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect SQL Server Agent jobs configured to run at startup — Security and Performance Risk' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
CheckClearSimplified: No SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysjobs where enabled = 1 and start_step_id = 1 with schedule_id matching 'startup'. SQL Agent jobs configured to run at SQL Server startup. These run before any login-based access and with Agent's service account permissions. Review all startup jobs for legitimacy and document their purpose.
FullStoryAction: Make sure you know exactly what this job is doing, because it could pose a security risk.

Name: Validate SQL Server Agent Alert Notification Configuration
Description: All active SQL Server Agent alerts are properly configured with associated notification methods (e.g., email, pager) or job response steps. Alerts without defined follow-up actions result in silent failures, meaning critical system events, security breaches, or performance degradation may go unnoticed by operational staff.
RecommendedAction: Configure SQL Agent alerts for severity 16-25 and error 823-825. Enable Database Mail. Add operators with email/pager. Test by raising a test error.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Validate SQL Server Agent Alert Notification Configuration' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: SQL Server Agent is running under an NT Service account
CheckClearSimplified: SQL Server Agent is running under an domain Service account
NextAction: sys.dm_server_services WHERE servicename LIKE '%Agent%' and service_account LIKE 'NT Service%'. SQL Agent is running as the built-in NT Service account. This account has limited network access , it cannot write to network shares, access Active Directory, or perform remote operations required by some jobs (log shipping, SSIS, linked server jobs). Configure a dedicated domain service account with minimum required permissions.
FullStoryAction: SQL Server Agent services can run under NT Service accounts. It is ideal to run as Active Directory accounts.

Name: Ensure BUILTINAdministrators login is removed — prevents excessive sysadmin rights
Description: By default, SQL Server adds the BUILTINAdministrators Windows group to the sysadmin fixed server role. This configuration grants excessive administrative access to all local Windows administrators on the host machine, severely violating the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP). Compromising a local administrator account can lead to full database control and complete system takeover, significantly increasing the attack surface. Best practice dictates removing this membership and enforcing granular access control using specific domain security groups or dedicated service accounts.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure BUILTINAdministrators login is removed - prevents excessive sysadmin rights' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure BUILTINAdministrators login is removed — prevents excessive sysadmin rights' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use Always Encrypted for client-side protection of sensitive columns (SSN, PII)
Description: Always Encrypted is a critical security feature that protects sensitive column data (e.g., SSN, credit cards, PII) by encrypting it at the client application level before transmission to SQL Server. This mechanism ensures that even database administrators or server processes cannot view the plain text data, providing a layer of protection beyond standard encryption methods like TDE. Implementing this is vital for meeting strict regulatory requirements such as GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS regarding data confidentiality.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use Always Encrypted for client-side protection of sensitive columns (SSN, PII)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use Always Encrypted for client-side protection of sensitive columns (SSN, PII)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Verification of Complete and Current Database Backup Chains
Description: A complete, current, and secure backup chain exists for critical databases. Regular full backups are foundational to data recovery, making sure the Recovery Point Objective (RPO) is met by minimizing data loss.
RecommendedAction: Schedule and verify backups for Verification of Complete and Current Database Backup Chains. Run RESTORE VERIFYONLY monthly on every backup file.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verification of Complete and Current Database Backup Chains' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: Configure Windows Power Plan to High Performance for Stable SQL Server Operation
Description: The underlying operating system power plan is set to "High Performance" (or equivalent) on the SQL Server host machine. The default "Balanced" or energy-saving plans can dynamically throttle CPU resources and adjust performance based on perceived load, which introduces unpredictable latency spikes for database.
SqlQuery:
DECLARE @ver INT; SET @ver=CONVERT(INT,SERVERPROPERTY('ProductMajorVersion')); IF @ver<10 BEGIN SELECT 'SKIP' AS result,CONCAT('Requires 2008+. Current: ',@ver) AS message; RETURN; END;
SELECT 'INFO' AS result,'Windows Power Plan cannot be validated from T-SQL. Verify in Control Panel > Power Options that High Performance plan is selected.' AS message;
RecommendedAction: Set Windows Power Plan to High Performance via powercfg.cpl. This is a 30-second change with measurable impact on CPU-bound queries.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Configure Windows Power Plan to High Performance for Stable SQL Server Operation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: CPU throttling from Balanced power plan adds unpredictable latency to every query. This is a 30-second fix with measurable performance gain.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some SQL server(s) are not running in High Performance mode
CheckClearSimplified: All Servers are in High Performance mode
NextAction: Reads Windows power scheme via registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings or WMI. Balanced and Power Saver plans use CPU frequency scaling (C-states and P-states), capping clock speed and adding latency to CPU state transitions. SQL Server requires High Performance power plan to allow maximum CPU frequency at all times. This is a commonly missed configuration on VMs where the hypervisor power policy also matters.
FullStoryAction: The current count of servers not running in High-Performance power scheme

Name: Detect use of GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key – causes severe fragmentation
Description: Using GUIDs (uniqueidentifier) as clustered index keys is highly discouraged because their random nature leads to frequent page splits and severe fragmentation. This process significantly degrades database performance over time, requiring costly maintenance operations. Best practice dictates using sequential identifiers, such as identity columns or `NEWSEQUENTIALID()`, for clustered indexes whenever global uniqueness is not strictly required across distributed systems.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect use of GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key - causes severe fragmentation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect use of GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key – causes severe fragmentation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Identify Disabled Indexes to Prevent Performance Degradation and Storage Waste
Description: Indexes are critical database structures designed to improve query performance by providing fast lookup paths. When indexes are disabled (often done temporarily during large ETL processes or data loads), they consume storage space but fail to contribute to query optimization until explicitly re-enabled or rebuilt. Failure to audit and restore these indexes after maintenance operations leads to significant, often overlooked, performance degradation and unnecessary storage overhead.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Identify Disabled Indexes to Prevent Performance Degradation and Storage Waste' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Identify Disabled Indexes to Prevent Performance Degradation and Storage Waste' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Agent IO Alerts
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Agent IO Alerts' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Agent IO Alerts' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Agent IO Alerts' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Deprecated XPs
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Deprecated XPs' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Deprecated XPs' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Deprecated XPs' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Unsupported Build
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Unsupported Build' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Unsupported Build' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Unsupported Build' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Offline CPU Schedulers
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Offline CPU Schedulers' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Offline CPU Schedulers' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Offline CPU Schedulers' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ server(s) have CPUs that are Offline
NextAction: Cumulative MB read + written since last SQL restart, extrapolated to daily rate. High daily I/O volume is a leading indicator of future storage capacity issues. Compare against storage rated sequential throughput (MB/s). Also check for write amplification from excessive index fragmentation or log flush patterns.
FullStoryAction: Some CPU cores can become inaccessible to SQL Server due to affinity masking or licensing problems

Name: NUMA Nodes Offline
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'NUMA Nodes Offline' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'NUMA Nodes Offline' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'NUMA Nodes Offline' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect excessive serializable locking waits (LCK_M_R%) indicating concurrency bottlenecks
Description: This check detects excessive wait types (LCK_M_R% waits) that indicate the use of SERIALIZABLE isolation level. Serializable transactions acquire range locks, which severely restrict concurrent operations like inserts and updates across data ranges, leading to significant performance bottlenecks. Best practice dictates using less restrictive isolation levels, such as Read Committed Snapshot Isolation (RCSI), or leveraging modern features like Optimized Locking to minimize lock contention and maximize concurrency while maintaining data integrity.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect excessive serializable locking waits (LCK_M_R%) indicating concurrency bottlenecks' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect excessive serializable locking waits (LCK_M_R%) indicating concurrency bottlenecks' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Internal Monitoring
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Internal Monitoring' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Internal Monitoring' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Internal Monitoring' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.configurations WHERE name = 'default trace enabled' = 0 or related internal monitoring feature disabled. Default trace, DMV retention, or other built-in diagnostic features have been disabled. These provide zero-cost forensic data that is invaluable for incident investigation. Re-enable unless replaced with equivalent coverage.
FullStoryAction: Internal Monitoring can be disabled using certain server startup switches. This is not advisable in production environments.

Name: Enable SQL Server Audit for Compliance-Grade Security Monitoring and Access
Description: SQL Server Audit is the modern, mechanism for tracking and logging security events on both the instance (server level) and individual databases. It provides granular control over what actions are monitored - such as successful/failed logins, schema changes, or specific data access attempts - with minimal performance overhead. Utilizing SQL Server Audit is critical for meeting strict regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS) by establishing a non-repudiable audit trail necessary for forensic analysis and demonstrating accountability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable SQL Server Audit for Compliance-Grade Security Monitoring and Access' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable SQL Server Audit for Compliance-Grade Security Monitoring and Access' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_role_members where role_principal_id = SUSER_ID('securityadmin'). securityadmin members can grant any permission and create logins , effectively elevating to sysadmin capability. Treat securityadmin membership as equivalent to sysadmin for risk purposes. Review all securityadmin members and reduce to those that genuinely require it.
FullStoryAction: These users can give themselves permission to do absolutely anything in SQL Server

Name: Detect Shrink Database Task in SQL Server Maintenance Plans to Prevent Performance Degradation
Description: Maintenance Plans configured with a "Shrink Database Task" are considered outdated and detrimental practices in modern SQL Server environments. This task can lead to severe index fragmentation, waste I/O resources by prematurely releasing space that is quickly re-allocated, and negatively impact overall database performance over time. Best practice dictates avoiding automatic shrinking; instead, administrators should rely on maintenance scripts (like those from Ola Hallengren) for proper index management and file growth monitoring.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Shrink Database Task in SQL Server Maintenance Plans to Prevent Performance Degradation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Shrink Database Task in SQL Server Maintenance Plans to Prevent Performance Degradation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Shrink Database Step In Maintenance Plan
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_query_plan , detects maintenance plan steps with DBCC SHRINKDATABASE or SHRINKFILE. A shrink step in a maintenance plan re-fragments every index immediately after the rebuild step. Remove shrink steps from all maintenance plans. If free space is a concern, reclaim it by archiving data or adding storage.
FullStoryAction: When it comes to Maintenance Plans the Database shrink jobs can cause performance degradation on Production systems

Name: Detect Query Store Cleanup Instability on SQL Server 2016 RTM; Verify Patch Level
Description:
This check validates that the SQL Server instance is patched to a version (at least CU1 for 2016 RTM) where Query Store cleanup jobs do not cause instability or dumps on Standard/Developer editions. Prior versions of SQL Server 2016 RTM had known bugs related to automated Query Store maintenance, which could lead to service disruption.
Best practice dictates ensuring all critical components are patched and configured according to vendor recommendations (e.g., Microsoft Learn best practices).
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Query Store Cleanup Instability on SQL Server 2016 RTM; Verify Patch Level' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Query Store Cleanup Instability on SQL Server 2016 RTM; Verify Patch Level' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect single point of failure in WSFC/Always On Availability Groups (No Passive Nodes)
Description: The redundancy of a Windows Server Failover Cluster (WSFC) hosting SQL Server. If there are no available passive nodes, the cluster cannot perform an automatic failover if the current active node fails or loses quorum.
RecommendedAction: Add a second node to the WSFC/AG cluster. A single-node AG provides no automatic failover. This is an availability group that is not actually available.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect single point of failure in WSFC/Always On Availability Groups (No Passive Nodes)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: Ensure HA Endpoints (AG/Mirroring) are Owned by Service Accounts" \u2014 Prevents Failover Failure
Description: This check validates that endpoints used for high availability (HA) features, such as Always On Availability Groups (AG) and Database Mirroring, are owned by the dedicated SQL Server service account. Endpoints owned by user accounts pose a significant operational risk because if the owner login is disabled, deleted, or becomes unavailable due to Active Directory issues, failover mechanisms will fail. Best practice dictates that all HA endpoint owners must be the dedicated service account to ensure reliable cluster communication and uninterrupted failover capabilities, maintaining system integrity during critical events.
RecommendedAction:
Investigate the finding 'Ensure HA Endpoints (AG/Mirroring) are Owned by Service Accounts" \u2014 Prevents Failover Failure
' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult:
Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure HA Endpoints (AG/Mirroring) are Owned by Service Accounts" \u2014 Prevents Failover Failure
' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Data Sync Endpoints are Owned by a User
CheckClearSimplified: Data Sync Endpoints are Owned by the system
NextAction: sys.endpoints where principal_id != sa and the endpoint owner is an unprivileged login. Endpoint ownership allows the owner to change endpoint configuration, potentially enabling unauthenticated connections. Reassign endpoint ownership to sa: ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON ENDPOINT::[name] TO sa.
FullStoryAction: If the endpoint owner login is disabled or not available due to Active Directory problems, the high availability will stop working.

Name: DBCC Events Trace
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'DBCC Events Trace' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'DBCC Events Trace' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'DBCC Events Trace' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Logged High Impact SQL Admin Tasks
CheckClearSimplified: No Logged High Impact SQL Admin Tasks
NextAction: Reads default trace (fn_trace_gettable) for DBCC events (EventClass 116). Filters out CHECKDB and CHECKTABLE. High-impact DBCC commands (SHRINKFILE, SHRINKDATABASE, DBREINDEX, CHECKALLOC) run by users indicate unplanned maintenance or attempts to recover a problem. Investigate who ran the command and why.
FullStoryAction: These are normally high impact DBA DBCC tasks. This does not include CHECKDB and other usually benign DBCC events.

Name: Ensure SQL Server services run under unique, dedicated Windows service accounts
Description:
Each SQL Server service (e.g., MSSQLSERVER, SQLAGENT) must run under a dedicated Windows domain service account rather than generic or shared accounts like "NT SERVICE" or local administrator accounts. Using shared identities violates the principle of least privilege and severely compromises auditability, making it impossible to determine which specific process or user initiated an action (non-repudiation failure).
It is strongly recommended to use Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) where possible, as they automate password management and significantly enhance security posture.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure SQL Server services run under unique, dedicated Windows service accounts' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure SQL Server services run under unique, dedicated Windows service accounts' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.databases where log_reuse_wait_desc = 'LOG_BACKUP' AND state_desc = 'RESTORING'. Log shipping is configured , this database is a warm standby. Monitor log shipping jobs in msdb.dbo.log_shipping_monitor_secondary. Check log_shipped_or_backed_up_date and restore_date , if the gap exceeds the recovery objective, the secondary is falling behind.
FullStoryAction: Version, patch level, CU, edition, AG enabled status.

Name: Unknown DB Owner
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Unknown DB Owner' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Unknown DB Owner' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Unknown DB Owner' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Implicit Transactions
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Implicit Transactions' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Implicit Transactions' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Implicit Transactions' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_sessions or sys.dm_tran_active_transactions with open_transaction_count > 0 and transaction type indicating implicit transaction mode. Implicit transactions (SET IMPLICIT_TRANSACTIONS ON) auto-begin a transaction on certain statements and require explicit COMMIT. Forgotten COMMITs cause long-running open transactions, escalating lock holdings and blocking other sessions. Check via DBCC OPENTRAN.
FullStoryAction: Open implicit transactions can potentially cause severe blocking issues

Name: Pre-size database files and configure fixed auto-growth increments for stability
Description: This check validates that database files are appropriately pre-sized and configured to prevent performance degradation or service disruption caused by unexpected file auto-growth events. Best practice dictates estimating the required size based on current data plus 6-12 months of projected growth. Initial file sizes should be set to a percentage (50-100%) of the estimated final size, and crucially, auto-growth must be configured using fixed increments (e.g., 256MB for data files, 128MB for log files). Percentage-based auto-growth is strongly discouraged on production systems as it can lead to unpredictable I/O spikes and resource contention during peak workloads.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Pre-size database files and configure fixed auto-growth increments for stability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Pre-size database files and configure fixed auto-growth increments for stability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Limit Virtual Log File (VLF) Count to Maintain Optimal Performance
Description: The Virtual Log File (VLF) count in the transaction log file should be monitored because excessive VLFs indicate high fragmentation, which can significantly degrade performance. High VLF counts slow down database recovery, backup operations, and restore times due to increased I/O overhead. Best practice dictates avoiding frequent `DBCC SHRINKFILE` operations, as these are the primary cause of VLF proliferation.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Limit Virtual Log File (VLF) Count to Maintain Optimal Performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Limit Virtual Log File (VLF) Count to Maintain Optimal Performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Configure MAXDOP Based on NUMA Topology and Core Count
Description: MAXDOP (Max Degree of Parallelism) controls the maximum number of CPU cores used by a query for parallel execution. Improper configuration is a critical performance risk, as setting it too high can cause excessive resource contention and CXPACKET waits, while setting it too low may underutilize available CPU resources. Best practice dictates configuring MAXDOP based on the server's NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) topology to ensure stable and optimal query execution across all hardware configurations.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Configure MAXDOP Based on NUMA Topology and Core Count' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Configure MAXDOP Based on NUMA Topology and Core Count' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enforce Appropriate SQL Server Recovery Model Based on Business Criticality
Description:
This check validates that the appropriate SQL Server recovery model is selected based on the database's criticality and required Recovery Point Objective (RPO). The choice between FULL, SIMPLE, and BULK_LOGGED models dictates the ability to perform point-in-time recovery (PITR), which is crucial for minimizing data loss.
FULL recovery enables PITR but requires diligent transaction log backups. SIMPLE recovery automatically manages logs but limits recovery scope. Selecting an incorrect model can lead to significant compliance failures and inability to restore data accurately after a failure event.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enforce Appropriate SQL Server Recovery Model Based on Business Criticality' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enforce Appropriate SQL Server Recovery Model Based on Business Criticality' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Optimize Storage and I/O using Data Compression
Description: Data compression is a database optimization technique used to reduce the physical storage footprint of data and improve Input/Output (I/O) performance. It achieves this by encoding data, resulting in significant storage savings (up to 70%). However, this efficiency comes at the cost of increased CPU utilization during both compression and decompression cycles, making it crucial to monitor system resources before implementation.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Optimize Storage and I/O using Data Compression' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Optimize Storage and I/O using Data Compression' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Evaluate SQL Server 2022 Database Ledger for Tamper-Evident Audit Trails
Description: This check validates the implementation of SQL Server Database Ledger, a feature introduced in SQL Server 2022. The ledger provides cryptographic proof of data integrity by recording all changes in an immutable, tamper-evident manner, similar to blockchain technology. This capability is critical for meeting stringent regulatory requirements (e.g., SOX, HIPAA) where auditable and unalterable records are mandatory for financial, legal, or compliance reporting.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Evaluate SQL Server 2022 Database Ledger for Tamper-Evident Audit Trails' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
