ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Evaluate SQL Server 2022 Database Ledger for Tamper-Evident Audit Trails' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Index Maintenance Strategy: Rebuild/Reorganize based on Fragmentation Level and Optimal FILLFACTOR Setting
Description: Index fragmentation occurs when the logical order of data within an index (like a B-tree) deviates significantly from its physical storage order, leading to increased I/O operations and reduced query performance. This check validates proper maintenance strategies by recommending REBUILD for high fragmentation (>30%) and REORGANIZE for moderate fragmentation (5-30%). Proper management also includes setting appropriate FILLFACTOR values based on the workload pattern (read vs write) to proactively minimize future degradation.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Index Maintenance Strategy: Rebuild/Reorganize based on Fragmentation Level and Optimal FILLFACTOR Setting' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Index Maintenance Strategy: Rebuild/Reorganize based on Fragmentation Level and Optimal FILLFACTOR Setting' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC for Optimal OLTP Performance Stability
Description: This check validates the configuration of AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC. By default, SQL Server performs synchronous statistics updates, which can cause query compilation to block and significantly delay execution in highly transactional Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) environments. Enabling asynchronous mode allows the database engine to proceed with existing statistics while updating them in the background, thereby improving stability, reducing performance stalls, and maintaining high availability during peak loads.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC for Optimal OLTP Performance Stability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS_ASYNC for Optimal OLTP Performance Stability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Identify and remove unused indexes — optimizing write performance and storage
Description: This check validates the presence of unused indexes - indexes that are not referenced by any query or application logic. Unused indexes represent pure overhead because they force SQL Server to perform write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and consume CPU cycles without providing any corresponding read benefit. Neglecting these structures can significantly degrade database performance, increase transaction log growth, inflate backup sizes, and waste I/O resources. Identifying and safely removing such redundant indexes is a fundamental best practice for maintaining optimal database health and storage efficiency.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Identify and remove unused indexes - optimizing write performance and storage' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Identify and remove unused indexes — optimizing write performance and storage' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Tune Cost Threshold for Parallelism (Default 5 is Obsolete)
Description: This check validates the configuration of the Cost Threshold for Parallelism, a critical setting that determines when SQL Server decides to use parallel execution plans. The default value (often 5) is outdated and too low for modern multi-core hardware, leading small queries to unnecessarily run in parallel. This can result in resource contention, excessive CXPACKET waits, thread pool exhaustion, and potential performance degradation or denial-of-service vulnerabilities under heavy load. Proper tuning is essential for maintaining system stability and optimal query throughput across various workloads (OLTP vs. Reporting).
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Tune Cost Threshold for Parallelism (Default 5 is Obsolete)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Tune Cost Threshold for Parallelism (Default 5 is Obsolete)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'cost threshold for parallelism' = 5 (default low value). A CTP of 5 means virtually any non-trivial query is eligible for parallelism. On OLTP servers, this causes excessive CXPACKET waits and serial bottlenecks at the thread synchronisation point. Recommended: raise CTP to 25â€“50 for mixed OLTP/OLAP; higher (50â€“100) for pure OLTP. Test changes during off-peak with Query Store plan regression monitoring.
FullStoryAction: Parallelism threshold is set by default to 5. Changing this may reduce CXPACKET waits.

Name: System Health XE
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'System Health XE' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'System Health XE' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.

Name: Verify sp_server_diagnostics Extended Event Session is Active for System Health Monitoring
Description: Verifies the operational status of the `sp_server_diagnostics` Extended Event (XEvent) session. This session is crucial for capturing diagnostic data and system health information within SQL Server. An active XEvent session acts as a 'Black Box Recorder,' allowing administrators to detect potential failures, diagnose performance bottlenecks, and troubleshoot complex issues that would otherwise be difficult or impossible to trace.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Verify sp_server_diagnostics Extended Event Session is Active for System Health Monitoring' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verify sp_server_diagnostics Extended Event Session is Active for System Health Monitoring' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Ensure Default SQL Server Trace is Active for Auditing
Description: This check validates the status of the SQL Server Default Trace, which provides a lightweight, always-on stream of events across critical categories (Database, Log, Security). Maintaining an active default trace is crucial for establishing baseline audit trails and enabling rapid troubleshooting during incidents. While modern best practices recommend migrating to Extended Events for granular control, ensuring this foundational logging mechanism is functional remains a key security and operational requirement.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Ensure Default SQL Server Trace is Active for Auditing' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Ensure Default SQL Server Trace is Active for Auditing' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: SQL Server is running under an NT Service account
CheckClearSimplified: SQL Server is running under an domain Service account
NextAction: sys.columns where encryption_type IS NOT NULL. Column-level encryption (CLE via EncryptByKey/EncryptByCert, or Always Encrypted via column master key). Informational , confirms encryption is in place for sensitive data. Verify key rotation is scheduled and backup of encryption keys/certificates is current (separate from database backup).
FullStoryAction: SQL Server services can run as NT Service accounts. It is ideal to run as Active Directory accounts.

Name: High Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache (Plan Cache Bloat)
Description:
This check monitors the SQL Server plan cache for an excessive number of single-use execution plans. 
High counts indicate potential plan cache bloat, which consumes significant memory resources without providing reuse benefits. 
Plan cache bloat can lead to performance degradation and increased memory pressure on the server. 
Reviewing query patterns or enabling options like 'Optimize for ad hoc access' is recommended to mitigate this risk.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'High Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache (Plan Cache Bloat)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'High Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache (Plan Cache Bloat)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_cached_plans where usecounts = 1 AND objtype = 'Adhoc'. A high count of single-use plans means the plan cache is full of non-reusable ad hoc SQL, evicting useful plans. Enable sp_configure 'optimize for ad hoc workloads' = 1 (stores plan stubs instead of full plans on first execution). Also investigate application parameterisation , ensure queries use sp_executesql or parameterised queries.
FullStoryAction: This may be a good use case for enabling the SQL Server 2008 memory optimization and parameterization features

Name: SQL Server Instance Patch Level Compliance Check
Description: This check validates that the SQL Server instance has applied all necessary security updates, critical patches, and cumulative updates (CUs). Running outdated versions exposes the database to known vulnerabilities (CVEs), performance degradation issues, and potential compliance violations. Regular patching is essential for maintaining data integrity and system resilience.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'SQL Server Instance Patch Level Compliance Check' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'SQL Server Instance Patch Level Compliance Check' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Identify and Validate Missing Indexes for Performance Tuning
Description: This check validates the existence of mechanisms to identify potential performance bottlenecks caused by missing database indexes. Missing indexes force SQL Server to perform costly table scans instead of efficient index seeks, severely degrading query performance and increasing resource consumption. While Dynamic Management Views (DMVs) like sys.dm_db_missing_index_details are crucial for proactive identification, it is vital to remember that these suggestions are based on estimates from single queries and must be thoroughly validated against the actual application workload before implementation to prevent over-indexing overhead or performance degradation.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Identify and Validate Missing Indexes for Performance Tuning' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Identify and Validate Missing Indexes for Performance Tuning' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.databases where state_desc = 'EMERGENCY'. Emergency mode is set manually (ALTER DATABASE SET EMERGENCY) to access a suspect database for forensic reads. A database left in emergency mode is read-only and not accessible to the application. Investigate the original failure, restore from backup, or run DBCC CHECKDB WITH REPAIR_ALLOW_DATA_LOSS.
FullStoryAction: One of the top resource-intensive queries may be dramatically improved by adding an index

Name: Database Collation Mismatch with Master/Model System Defaults
Description:
It is a critical configuration best practice to ensure that the collation of all user-defined
databases matches the system default, typically derived from the `master` and `model`
databases. Collation mismatches can lead to unpredictable runtime errors, especially
when executing stored procedures or complex queries involving temporary tables (which
are created in tempdb). If collations differ, SQL Server might encounter a collation
conflict error during data comparison, sorting, or joining operations, potentially
causing application failure and compromising perceived data integrity.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Collation Mismatch with Master/Model System Defaults' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Collation Mismatch with Master/Model System Defaults' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Cross-Database Ownership Chaining Must Be Disabled for Security Hardening
Description: Ownership chaining allows an object in one database to execute code or access data from another database. This mechanism significantly increases the system's attack surface and violates the principle of least privilege (NIST AC-6). Disabling cross-database ownership chaining is a critical security hardening measure, as it prevents unintended privilege escalation and unauthorized lateral movement across databases within the instance.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Cross-Database Ownership Chaining Must Be Disabled for Security Hardening' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Cross-Database Ownership Chaining Must Be Disabled for Security Hardening' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Avoid Over-Indexing: Excessive Indexes Degrade Write Performance
Description: This check validates that the database schema avoids excessive indexing, particularly on tables that experience high volumes of Data Manipulation Language (DML) operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). Having too many indexes significantly increases write overhead and maintenance costs because every index must be updated whenever data changes. Over-indexing can lead to severe performance degradation, increased storage consumption, and longer recovery times, making proper index design crucial for maintaining optimal performance in both read-heavy and mixed workloads.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Avoid Over-Indexing: Excessive Indexes Degrade Write Performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Avoid Over-Indexing: Excessive Indexes Degrade Write Performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Optimize Query Performance Using Covering Indexes with INCLUDE Columns
Description: The implementation of covering indexes by correctly leveraging the INCLUDE clause in nonclustered indexes. A covering index allows SQL Server to satisfy a query entirely from the index structure itself, without needing to perform expensive bookmark or key lookups.
RecommendedAction: Create covering indexes with INCLUDE for high-frequency queries that currently do key lookups. Review sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats for lookup patterns.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Optimize Query Performance Using Covering Indexes with INCLUDE Columns' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Bad indexing burns CPU and IO you already paid for. Users experience this as slow page loads and abandoned sessions.

Name: In SQL 2022, Evaluate ORDER BY in Index Creation for Sorted Scan Elimination
Description: This check validates the use of ORDER BY within index creation statements, a feature introduced in SQL Server 2022. Utilizing this capability allows developers to physically pre-sort data within the index structure. This optimization is critical because it can eliminate expensive sort operators from query execution plans that require ordered results (e.g., for reporting or analytical queries), significantly improving performance and reducing CPU overhead.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'In SQL 2022, Evaluate ORDER BY in Index Creation for Sorted Scan Elimination' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'In SQL 2022, Evaluate ORDER BY in Index Creation for Sorted Scan Elimination' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable Query Store on all production databases (READ_WRITE mode)
Description: Query Store is a critical performance management feature that captures query execution plans and runtime statistics within the database. It enables proactive monitoring by detecting plan regressions, establishing reliable performance baselines, and allowing for controlled plan forcing.
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER DATABASE [name] SET QUERY_STORE = ON (OPERATION_MODE = READ_WRITE, QUERY_CAPTURE_MODE = AUTO) for all production databases.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Query Store on all production databases (READ_WRITE mode)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.

Name: Set adequate Query Store MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB to prevent critical performance monitoring gaps
Description: The Query Store is an essential feature for monitoring database performance, automatically capturing historical query plans and runtime statistics. If the default storage size limit (often 100MB) is exceeded or set too low, critical performance data can be lost. This loss severely hinders troubleshooting efforts, making it impossible to accurately detect query plan regressions or analyze long-term performance trends in busy production environments. This check ensures sufficient capacity is allocated via MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB to maintain continuous operational visibility into database health and efficiency.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Set adequate Query Store MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB to prevent critical performance monitoring gaps' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Set adequate Query Store MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB to prevent critical performance monitoring gaps' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some SQL server(s) are not running in High Performance mode
CheckClearSimplified: All Servers are in High Performance mode
NextAction: Reads Windows power scheme via registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings or WMI. Balanced and Power Saver plans use CPU frequency scaling (C-states and P-states), capping clock speed and adding latency to CPU state transitions. SQL Server requires High Performance power plan to allow maximum CPU frequency at all times. This is a commonly missed configuration on VMs where the hypervisor power policy also matters.
FullStoryAction: The current count of servers not running in High-Performance power scheme

Name: Deprecated or Discontinued SQL Server Features Check
Description: Monitoring deprecated or discontinued features is a critical aspect of maintaining an upgrade-ready and stable SQL Server environment. Continued reliance on such features, which are scheduled for removal in future major versions (e.g., SQL Server 2025), can lead to application failure, unexpected behavior during patching cycles, or significant operational downtime. This check identifies dependencies that must be refactored or replaced with modern alternatives to ensure long-term compatibility and adherence to current best practices.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Deprecated or Discontinued SQL Server Features Check' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Deprecated or Discontinued SQL Server Features Check' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN in Query Store to automatically fix plan regressions
Description: The configuration of Query Store's automatic plan correction feature. Plan changes in SQL Server can lead to significant and unpredictable performance regressions, causing service degradation or application failure.
RecommendedAction: Enable automatic plan correction: ALTER DATABASE [name] SET QUERY_STORE (QUERY_CAPTURE_MODE = AUTO, SIZE_BASED_CLEANUP_MODE = AUTO). Grant db_owner to monitoring account.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN in Query Store to automatically fix plan regressions' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.

Non-compliant configuration for 'Sparse File Check' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.

Name: Use Query Store Hints for Performance Tuning Without Code Changes
Description: This check validates the appropriate use of Query Store hints, a powerful performance feature available since SQL Server 2016. It allows database administrators to apply specific optimizer hints (such as MAXDOP or RECOMPILE) directly via T-SQL commands without requiring modifications to the underlying application code. While highly useful for mitigating performance regressions and optimizing complex queries, these hints should be used judiciously, as they override the Query Optimizer's decisions and can potentially degrade overall system performance if misapplied or misunderstood.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use Query Store Hints for Performance Tuning Without Code Changes' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use Query Store Hints for Performance Tuning Without Code Changes' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Database Compatibility Level Mismatch or Outdated
Description: This check validates that the compatibility level of existing databases is set to an appropriate or current version. Running a database on a significantly outdated compatibility level can prevent the use of modern security features, performance improvements, and bug fixes introduced in newer SQL Server versions. This configuration drift increases the overall attack surface, potentially leading to compliance violations (e.g., PCI-DSS) and exposing the system to known vulnerabilities that have been patched in later releases.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Compatibility Level Mismatch or Outdated' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Compatibility Level Mismatch or Outdated' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: MaxDOP must be configured appropriately relative to CPU count for optimal performance
Description: The Maximum Degree of Parallelism (MAXDOP) setting dictates the maximum number of processors that SQL Server can use when executing a query using a parallel execution plan. Misconfiguring MAXDOP, especially relative to the underlying hardware architecture (such as Symmetric Multiprocessing or NUMA nodes), is a common cause of severe performance degradation and resource contention. Best practices require tuning this value to match the physical core count or specific workload requirements to ensure optimal utilization of available processing power across all database instances.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'MaxDOP must be configured appropriately relative to CPU count for optimal performance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'MaxDOP must be configured appropriately relative to CPU count for optimal performance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: In SQL 2022+, Enable Parameter Sensitive Plan Optimization (PSP) to Prevent Performance Degradation
Description: This check validates the implementation and enablement of Parameter Sensitive Plan Optimization (PSP) in SQL Server 2022 and later versions. PSP is a critical performance feature designed to mitigate issues caused by parameter sniffing, where a single cached execution plan becomes suboptimal when querying data with highly variable distributions. By automatically generating and maintaining multiple active cached plans for the same parameterized statement, PSP ensures consistent query performance and stability regardless of the specific parameters used at runtime.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'In SQL 2022+, Enable Parameter Sensitive Plan Optimization (PSP) to Prevent Performance Degradation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'In SQL 2022+, Enable Parameter Sensitive Plan Optimization (PSP) to Prevent Performance Degradation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some SQL server(s) are not running in High Performance mode
CheckClearSimplified: All Servers are in High Performance mode
NextAction: Reads Windows power scheme via registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings or WMI. Balanced and Power Saver plans use CPU frequency scaling (C-states and P-states), capping clock speed and adding latency to CPU state transitions. SQL Server requires High Performance power plan to allow maximum CPU frequency at all times. This is a commonly missed configuration on VMs where the hypervisor power policy also matters.
FullStoryAction: The current count of servers not running in High-Performance power scheme

Name: Validate Optimal Min Server Memory Configuration
Description: The configuration of `min server memory`, which dictates the minimum amount of RAM SQL Server attempts to keep allocated. While setting a floor prevents OS-driven resource starvation in some environments, modern Microsoft guidance strongly recommends leaving this value at 0 MB.
RecommendedAction: Run sp_configure 'min server memory (MB)', 0. RECONFIGURE. A non-zero min memory locks that RAM even when SQL Server is idle.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Validate Optimal Min Server Memory Configuration' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Memory misconfiguration causes the buffer pool to discard cached data. Disk IO increases 10-100x and every query slows down.
NextAction: sp_configure 'min server memory (MB)' > 0. When min memory is set, SQL Server will not release memory below this floor even under OS memory pressure. Setting min = max is a best practice to prevent buffer pool eviction by other processes. Confirm the value is appropriate for the server's dedicated SQL role and available physical memory.
FullStoryAction: min server memory (MB). Guarantees a minimum amount of memory available to the SQL Server Memory Manager

Name: Explicit Column Selection (Avoid SELECT *) for Performance and Data Minimization
Description: Using SELECT * is a critical anti-pattern in SQL development. This practice not only significantly degrades query performance by increasing I/O overhead and preventing the use of covering indexes, but it also violates data minimization principles (e.g., GDPR). By retrieving all columns, developers risk exposing unnecessary or sensitive data that should be restricted to specific application layers. Developers must explicitly list only the required columns to ensure optimal performance, maintainability, and adherence to security best practices.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Explicit Column Selection (Avoid SELECT *) for Performance and Data Minimization' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Explicit Column Selection (Avoid SELECT *) for Performance and Data Minimization' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use SET NOCOUNT ON in stored procedures for performance optimization
Description: This check validates the consistent use of SET NOCOUNT ON within stored procedures. This setting is a crucial coding best practice that suppresses the "rows affected" message returned by SQL Server after executing data modification statements (like INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE). Implementing this significantly reduces network traffic and minimizes client-side processing overhead for applications interacting with the database, thereby improving overall performance and stability in high-volume OLTP environments.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use SET NOCOUNT ON in stored procedures for performance optimization' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use SET NOCOUNT ON in stored procedures for performance optimization' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Extended Stored Procedures in Master Database
CheckClearSimplified: No Extended Stored Procedures in Master Database
NextAction: sys.objects where database_id = 1 (master) and type = 'X' (extended stored procedures) or user-created objects. Extended stored procedures in master run in-process with the SQL engine , a bug in an XP can crash the SQL Server process. Audit all non-system XPs in master: SELECT * FROM master.sys.objects WHERE type='X' AND is_ms_shipped=0.
FullStoryAction: CLR may be in use, and the master database now needs to be part of your backup/recovery planning

Name: Apply Row-Level Security (RLS) for Multi-Tenant Data Isolation
Description: Row-Level Security (RLS) is a critical database feature that enforces strict data isolation by filtering access at the row level. It ensures that users can only view records they are explicitly authorized to see, making it essential for multi-tenant applications and compliance with regulations like GDPR and HIPAA. RLS implements security predicates via policies, ensuring that access restrictions are applied reliably within the database tier, thereby significantly reducing the overall attack surface area of the system.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Apply Row-Level Security (RLS) for Multi-Tenant Data Isolation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Apply Row-Level Security (RLS) for Multi-Tenant Data Isolation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Storage Space Capacity and Disk Allocation Validation
Description: This check validates the configuration of SQL Server storage spaces, specifically monitoring disk capacity and resource allocation. Proper management of underlying disks and ensuring sufficient scaling parameters are critical to maintaining optimal database performance and operational resilience. Failure to adequately provision or monitor disk space can lead to service degradation, transaction failures, data corruption, and potential denial-of-service conditions.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Storage Space Capacity and Disk Allocation Validation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Storage Space Capacity and Disk Allocation Validation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use premium SSDs for SQL Server data files
Description: This check validates that production SQL Server data files are hosted on high-performance, premium Solid State Drives (SSDs). Using standard HDDs or lower-tier SSDs can lead to variable latency and insufficient IOPS for modern, high-transaction workloads. Consistent low latency and maximum throughput are critical for mission-critical applications, as inadequate storage performance is a primary cause of service degradation and unpredictable bottlenecks in production environments. Adhering to best practices ensures the stability and scalability required by enterprise systems.|
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use premium SSDs for SQL Server data files' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use premium SSDs for SQL Server data files' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable Locking Pages in Memory Windows Policy
Description: This check validates the "Lock Pages in Memory" Windows policy, which is a critical operating system setting for SQL Server. When enabled, it instructs the OS to prevent SQL Server data structures and pages from being swapped out (paged) to virtual memory on disk. If this feature is disabled or improperly configured, excessive paging occurs, leading to severe performance degradation, increased I/O latency, and reduced transactional throughput under heavy load.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable Locking Pages in Memory Windows Policy' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Locking Pages in Memory Windows Policy' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.availability_groups and sys.dm_hadr_availability_replica_states. An Always On Availability Group is configured. Key health checks: primary/secondary roles, synchronisation state (SYNCHRONIZED vs SYNCHRONIZING vs NOT SYNCHRONIZING), and log send queue / redo queue sizes in sys.dm_hadr_database_replica_states. Alert on log_send_queue_size > recovery_point_objective threshold.
FullStoryAction: Locked pages in memory is a great setting on physical hardware, on virtual hardware and containers it is not ideal.

Name: Disable Legacy Protocols (SMBv1) to Reduce Attack Surface
Description: This check validates that the Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) protocol is disabled on the host machine. SMBv1 is an outdated, insecure networking protocol known to contain critical vulnerabilities, such as those exploited by WannaCry and other ransomware strains. Disabling it significantly reduces the attack surface of the system, preventing potential man-in-the-middle attacks, lateral movement exploits, and unauthorized access across the network.
SqlQuery:
DECLARE @ver INT; SET @ver=CONVERT(INT,SERVERPROPERTY('ProductMajorVersion')); IF @ver<10 BEGIN SELECT 'SKIP' AS result,CONCAT('Requires 2008+. Current: ',@ver) AS message; RETURN; END;
SELECT 'INFO' AS result, 'SMBv1 status cannot be checked from T-SQL. Verify via Get-SmbServerConfiguration | Select EnableSMB1Protocol in PowerShell.' AS message;
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Disable Legacy Protocols (SMBv1) to Reduce Attack Surface' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Disable Legacy Protocols (SMBv1) to Reduce Attack Surface' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use CONTAINED DATABASES for Enhanced Security Isolation and Portability
Description: This check validates the implementation of contained databases, an architectural feature that moves authentication and authorization metadata (users, logins) from the instance level to the database level. This significantly enhances security isolation by localizing credentials within the data boundary, improves portability across different SQL Server environments (especially cloud platforms like Azure SQL Database), and reduces dependency on global instance-level security objects. Adopting this practice is a critical best practice for modern high-availability deployments, ensuring that security boundaries remain intact even when moving or replicating databases.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use CONTAINED DATABASES for Enhanced Security Isolation and Portability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use CONTAINED DATABASES for Enhanced Security Isolation and Portability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Prevent Authentication Breakage by Auditing Orphaned SQL Server Users
Description: Orphaned users are database principals that possess access rights (database user) but lack a corresponding, active server-level login in the master database. This state represents a critical break in SQL Server''s authentication chain, often resulting from manual login deletions or complex migrations without proper identity mapping. Failure to resolve these orphans can lead to unpredictable security behavior and potential unauthorized access attempts, severely compromising the integrity of the system's security model.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Prevent Authentication Breakage by Auditing Orphaned SQL Server Users' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Prevent Authentication Breakage by Auditing Orphaned SQL Server Users' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable 'Optimize for Ad Hoc Workloads' to Prevent Plan Cache Bloat
Description: This check validates the status of the "optimize for ad hoc workloads" server configuration option. This setting is crucial for managing SQL Server's plan cache efficiency, particularly in high-volume OLTP environments that generate many single-use (ad hoc) queries. When enabled, the system stores a small plan stub after the first execution instead of caching the full, potentially large execution plan. This significantly mitigates plan cache bloat and reduces memory pressure caused by one-time queries, thereby improving overall resource availability for critical operations.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable 'Optimize for Ad Hoc Workloads' to Prevent Plan Cache Bloat' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable 'Optimize for Ad Hoc Workloads' to Prevent Plan Cache Bloat' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some SQL server(s) do not have query plan memory optimization enabled. There are no reasons why this should be disabled.
CheckClearSimplified: All servers have query plan memory optimization enabled.
NextAction: sp_configure 'scan for startup procs' = 1. SQL Server executes stored procedures marked with sp_procoption 'startup' = true at every instance start. A compromised startup proc runs as sa before any logins occur. Review all startup procs in sys.objects joined to sys.extended_properties for 'startup' = 1.
FullStoryAction: Query plan memory utilization can be optimized for SQL server. The default setting keeps this disabled.

Name: Enforce Windows Firewall State on Public Profile via GPO
Description: This check validates that the Windows Firewall is configured and enforced via Group Policy Objects (GPO) to maintain a secure public state. Misconfigured or disabled firewalls can expose SQL Server ports and services to unauthorized network traffic, significantly increasing the attack surface. Enforcing this setting is a fundamental step in perimeter defense for critical database infrastructure, aligning with industry best practices like CIS Benchmarks and PCI DSS requirements for network segmentation and access control.
SqlQuery:
DECLARE @ver INT; SET @ver=CONVERT(INT,SERVERPROPERTY('ProductMajorVersion')); IF @ver<10 BEGIN SELECT 'SKIP' AS result,CONCAT('Requires 2008+. Current: ',@ver) AS message; RETURN; END;
SELECT 'INFO' AS result, 'Windows Firewall state cannot be validated from T-SQL. Verify via PowerShell: Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select Name, Enabled.' AS message;
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enforce Windows Firewall State on Public Profile via GPO' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enforce Windows Firewall State on Public Profile via GPO' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable Data Classification on Sensitive Columns for Governance and Compliance
Description: This check validates the implementation of data discovery and classification mechanisms within the database. It ensures that columns containing sensitive information, such as Personally Identifiable Information (PII), Protected Health Information (PHI), or payment card data are correctly identified and labeled for governance purposes. Proper classification is a foundational security requirement because it dictates which advanced controls - such as dynamic data masking, row-level security (RLS), or column encryption (TDE) - must be applied to meet regulatory mandates like GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOX.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable Data Classification on Sensitive Columns for Governance and Compliance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Data Classification on Sensitive Columns for Governance and Compliance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enforce FULL Recovery Model and Scheduled Log Backups for Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)
Description: The SQL Server database is configured to use the FULL recovery model and maintains a scheduled, regular log backup routine. The transaction log records every modification made to the database; therefore, consistent log backups are mandatory for achieving Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR).
RecommendedAction: Set all user databases to FULL recovery model: ALTER DATABASE [name] SET RECOVERY FULL. Take a full backup immediately after. Schedule log backups.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enforce FULL Recovery Model and Scheduled Log Backups for Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Without log backups, point-in-time recovery is impossible. A data-loss event with no recent backups is a resume-generating incident.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ databases have no recent LOG Backups
CheckClearSimplified: All databases have recent LOG Backups
NextAction: Detects databases in FULL or BULK_LOGGED recovery that have no transaction log backup in msdb.dbo.backupset. Without log backups the log chain is broken: point-in-time restore is impossible, and the log file grows unboundedly until disk space is exhausted. Schedule log backups every 15â€“60 minutes depending on RPO requirement.
FullStoryAction: Without Log backups databases can consume disk space and database restores are compromised.

Name: Enable Backup Compression for Storage and I/O Optimization
Description: The configuration of backup compression, which is a critical feature for optimizing database maintenance and making sure business continuity. Enabling compression significantly reduces the physical size of backup files, minimizing I/O operations, thereby lowering storage costs and shortening the required backup window time.
RecommendedAction: Enable backup compression: sp_configure 'backup compression default', 1. All backups now compress by default. Verify: RESTORE HEADERONLY to check compressed size.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Backup Compression for Storage and I/O Optimization' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: Use COPY_ONLY Backups for Ad-Hoc Data Archiving Without Breaking the Log Chain
Description: The proper use of `COPY_ONLY` backups when performing ad-hoc or supplemental database backups. Using this method is critical because it makes sure that the backup process does not break the continuous transaction log chain, which is essential for accurate.
RecommendedAction: Use BACKUP DATABASE [name] TO DISK = '...' WITH COPY_ONLY for ad-hoc backups. This preserves the log chain. Do not use COPY_ONLY for your regular backup schedule.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use COPY_ONLY Backups for Ad-Hoc Data Archiving Without Breaking the Log Chain' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ databases have no recent LOG Backups
CheckClearSimplified: All databases have recent LOG Backups
NextAction: Detects databases in FULL or BULK_LOGGED recovery that have no transaction log backup in msdb.dbo.backupset. Without log backups the log chain is broken: point-in-time restore is impossible, and the log file grows unboundedly until disk space is exhausted. Schedule log backups every 15â€“60 minutes depending on RPO requirement.
FullStoryAction: Without Log backups databases can consume disk space and database restores are compromised.

Name: Verify backups with RESTORE VERIFYONLY or better: perform periodic simulated test restores
Description: Confirms backups are verified regularly. A backup that has never been restore-tested is a wish. I have seen 'verified' backups fail on restore because a page checksum was silently corrupted.
RecommendedAction: Schedule monthly RESTORE VERIFYONLY on every backup file. Better: do a quarterly full restore to a test server. A backup you have not restored is a hope, not a backup.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Verify backups with RESTORE VERIFYONLY or better: perform periodic simulated test restores' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: A backup that has never been restored is a wish, not a backup. Auditors require proof of restore testing.

Name: Store SQL Server Backups on Separate Physical Storage (Offsite/Cloud)
Description: Database backups are stored on physical storage media separate from the primary database instance. Storing all backups locally significantly increases vulnerability to site-wide failures, ransomware attacks, or physical disasters (e.g., fire or flood).
RecommendedAction: Store backups on separate physical storage from database files. Use a network share, Azure Blob, or secondary disk array. A single drive failure should not kill both data and backups.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Store SQL Server Backups on Separate Physical Storage (Offsite/Cloud)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: 66 S3 Backup Storage SQL 2022
Description: Detection check extracted from check_66_S3_Backup_Storage_SQL_2022.yaml.

Name: Use BACKUP TO URL for Cloud-Based Backup Strategy (Azure Blob)
Description: The implementation of cloud-based backup strategies, specifically using mechanisms like BACKUP TO URL to Azure Blob Storage. Implementing this is a critical best practice for achieving offsite, geo-redundant data protection, which is essential for meeting compliance requirements (e.g., HIPAA, PCI-DSS).
RecommendedAction: Use BACKUP TO URL with Azure Blob Storage. Create a Shared Access Signature credential first. This gives you offsite backups without a secondary datacenter.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use BACKUP TO URL for Cloud-Based Backup Strategy (Azure Blob)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable server default backup compression to optimize storage and restore speed
Description: The server instance has backup compression enabled by default. Enabling this feature is a critical best practice for optimizing storage and improving Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR) efficiency.
RecommendedAction: Run sp_configure 'backup compression default', 1. RECONFIGURE. Backup compression saves 50-70% storage and speeds up restore.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable server default backup compression to optimize storage and restore speed' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: A backup that has never been restored is a wish, not a backup. Auditors require proof of restore testing.
NextAction: sp_configure 'backup compression default' = 0. Backup compression is available on SQL 2008 Standard and above. Without compression, backups are larger (60â€“80% larger typically), backup windows are longer, and storage costs are higher. Enable: sp_configure 'backup compression default', 1; RECONFIGURE.
FullStoryAction: Backup compression is included with SQL Server 2008R2 & newer, even in Standard Edition.

Name: Use Always On Availability Groups for High Availability/Disaster Recovery at the Database Level
Description: Availability Groups (AGs) are a critical feature providing database-level High Availability and Disaster Recovery (HA/DR). They make sure minimal downtime and data loss by synchronizing data across multiple replica instances.
RecommendedAction: Deploy Always On Availability Groups for databases requiring HA/DR. Configure 2+ replicas, a listener, and health checks. Test failover quarterly.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use Always On Availability Groups for High Availability/Disaster Recovery at the Database Level' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Without log backups, point-in-time recovery is impossible. A data-loss event with no recent backups is a resume-generating incident.
NextAction: sys.database_scoped_configurations PARAMETER_SNIFFING = OFF. Disables parameter sniffing globally , SQL uses average statistics instead of the sniffed value. This is a blunt workaround for parameter-sensitive plan problems. It improves some queries at the expense of others. Prefer OPTION(OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN), OPTION(RECOMPILE), or Query Store plan forcing for targeted fixes.
FullStoryAction: Parameter sniffing is disabled on the database. The default is ON.

Name: 72 Distributed AGs
Description: Detection check extracted from check_72_Distributed_AGs.yaml.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding '72 Distributed AGs' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of '72 Distributed AGs' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Implement Appropriate AG Synchronization Mode (Synchronous vs. Asynchronous)
Description: The proper configuration of Always On Availability Groups (AGs) to make sure an appropriate balance between data durability and performance. The choice of synchronization mode - synchronous or asynchronous commit - is critical for defining the Recovery Point Objective (RPO).
RecommendedAction: Set synchronous replicas for zero-data-loss requirements within the same datacenter. Set asynchronous for cross-region DR. Document the RPO/RTO for each AG.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Implement Appropriate AG Synchronization Mode (Synchronous vs. Asynchronous)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: Configure Automated Backup Preference on Secondary Replica to Offload Primary Load
Description: The automated backup preference for an Always On Availability Group (AG) is configured to direct backups away from the primary replica. Performing routine database backups, especially full or log backups, on the primary replica can generate significant I/O and CPU load, potentially degrading production query performance.
RecommendedAction: Set automated backup preference to 'Secondary' in AG properties. This offloads backup IO from the primary replica. Make sure the secondary can handle the backup workload.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Configure Automated Backup Preference on Secondary Replica to Offload Primary Load' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.

Name: 79 Link Feature Hybrid DR
Description: Detection check extracted from check_79_Link_Feature_Hybrid_DR.yaml.

Name: Schedule Cleanup for msdb History Tables (Backupfile, Sysjobhistory)
Description: The msdb database is a critical system repository that stores operational metadata, including SQL Server Agent job history (sysjobhistory) and detailed backup/restore records. If these historical tables are allowed to grow unbounded without regular maintenance cleanup, it can lead to significant performance degradation across the instance. Excessive bloat can slow down core maintenance operations like backups and restore procedures, potentially causing failures in essential SQL Server Agent functionality and impacting overall system stability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Schedule Cleanup for msdb History Tables (Backupfile, Sysjobhistory)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Schedule Cleanup for msdb History Tables (Backupfile, Sysjobhistory)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Monitor SQL Agent Job Failures for Operational Health
Description: Verifies SQL Agent job failure monitoring is active. A missed backup job or failed ETL that nobody notices for 3 days means 3 days of unrecoverable data.
RecommendedAction: Create alerts on severity 16+ errors and job failures. Configure Database Mail. Set up an operator with email notification. Test the alert with a manual job failure.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Monitor SQL Agent Job Failures for Operational Health' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
CheckClearSimplified: No SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysjobs where enabled = 1 and start_step_id = 1 with schedule_id matching 'startup'. SQL Agent jobs configured to run at SQL Server startup. These run before any login-based access and with Agent's service account permissions. Review all startup jobs for legitimacy and document their purpose.
FullStoryAction: Make sure you know exactly what this job is doing, because it could pose a security risk.

Name: Maintain Patched SQL Server and OS for Security and Stability
Description: This check validates that both the SQL Server instance and the underlying Operating System are kept up to date with the latest Cumulative Updates (CUs) and security patches. Applying timely updates is a fundamental requirement for maintaining system integrity, as it remediates known Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs), addresses performance flaws, and ensures compliance with major industry standards like CIS Benchmarks, PCI-DSS, and DISA STIG guidelines. Failure to patch leaves the system exposed to potential exploits, data breaches, and instability issues.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Maintain Patched SQL Server and OS for Security and Stability' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Maintain Patched SQL Server and OS for Security and Stability' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_role_members where role_principal_id = SUSER_ID('securityadmin'). securityadmin members can grant any permission and create logins , effectively elevating to sysadmin capability. Treat securityadmin membership as equivalent to sysadmin for risk purposes. Review all securityadmin members and reduce to those that genuinely require it.
FullStoryAction: These users can give themselves permission to do absolutely anything in SQL Server

Name: Use Resource Governor to Limit and Isolate Workload Resources
Description: The Resource Governor is a crucial performance and stability feature that allows database administrators to classify incoming sessions into defined workload groups. By setting explicit resource limits on CPU, memory, and physical I/O for these groups (Resource Pools), organizations can prevent poorly optimized or runaway queries from consuming all system resources. This mechanism ensures service availability, protects against accidental Denial of Service (DoS) conditions caused by excessive resource usage, and guarantees predictable performance even under heavy load, thereby enhancing overall stability and reliability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Use Resource Governor to Limit and Isolate Workload Resources' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use Resource Governor to Limit and Isolate Workload Resources' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Implement Immutable Backup Strategy using Cloud Object Locking (Ransomware Protection)
Description: This check validates the implementation of an immutable backup strategy, typically by leveraging cloud storage features like S3 Object Lock or Write Once Read Many (WORM) policies. By enforcing a retention period that prevents deletion or modification for a specified duration, it provides critical protection against ransomware attacks and accidental data loss. Ensuring backups are truly immutable is fundamental to maintaining business continuity, meeting regulatory requirements, and guaranteeing data integrity in modern threat landscapes.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Implement Immutable Backup Strategy using Cloud Object Locking (Ransomware Protection)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Implement Immutable Backup Strategy using Cloud Object Locking (Ransomware Protection)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Enable Instant File Initialization (IFI) for Data Files — Optimize Performance and Reduce Restore Times
Description: Instant File Initialization (IFI) is a performance optimization feature that significantly reduces the time required for database file growth, restoration, and TempDB creation. By pre-initializing data files with zeros instead of leaving them unallocated, IFI dramatically improves operational efficiency. However, users must be aware of the security trade-off: because IFI does not zero-fill all portions of the file space during operation, previous sensitive data may remain recoverable in unallocated blocks, which can impact compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enable Instant File Initialization (IFI) for Data Files - Optimize Performance and Reduce Restore Times' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enable Instant File Initialization (IFI) for Data Files — Optimize Performance and Reduce Restore Times' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Instant File Initialization Disabled on @ server(s)
CheckClearSimplified: Instant File Initialization Disabled on @ Servers
NextAction: sys.databases where compatibility_level < SERVERPROPERTY('ProductMajorVersion') * 10 + 90 (or similar). Specific compatibility level check for feature gaps. Databases below compat 130 (SQL 2016) cannot use Query Store, RCSI improvements, or IQP. Below 140 (SQL 2017): no adaptive joins or batch mode memory grant feedback. Below 150 (SQL 2019): no row mode MGF, scalar UDF inlining, or table variable deferred compilation.
FullStoryAction: The Perform Volume Maintenance Tasks permission allows the SQL server to complete database file growth operation faster

Name: Monitor System Wait Statistics via sys.dm_os_wait_stats for Performance Bottleneck Diagnosis
Description: This check validates the monitoring of system wait statistics using sys.dm_os_wait_stats. This DMV is a critical diagnostic tool because it identifies primary resource bottlenecks - such as CPU contention, I/O latency, or locking waits - that are causing performance degradation in SQL Server. Analyzing top wait types allows DBAs to pinpoint whether optimization efforts should focus on indexing improvements, query rewriting, hardware upgrades, or addressing specific concurrency issues for optimal system health and stability.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Monitor System Wait Statistics via sys.dm_os_wait_stats for Performance Bottleneck Diagnosis' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Monitor System Wait Statistics via sys.dm_os_wait_stats for Performance Bottleneck Diagnosis' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Use Extended Events for Modern, Low-Impact Performance Monitoring (Avoid Deprecated Profilers)
Description: Verifies Extended Events sessions replace SQL Trace/Profiler. SQL Trace adds 10-30% overhead on busy servers. Extended Events run at <2% overhead and capture more detail.
RecommendedAction: Deploy Extended Events sessions for high-value traces: deadlocks, long-running queries, lock escalations, and logon triggers. Remove SQL Trace/Profiler traces.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Use Extended Events for Modern, Low-Impact Performance Monitoring (Avoid Deprecated Profilers)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Monitoring gaps mean you learn about production issues from users or executives. The mean time to detection goes from seconds to hours.

Name: Monitor Index Usage Patterns Using DMVs for Performance Optimization and Resource Governance
Description: This check validates the monitoring of index usage patterns using system dynamic management views (DMVs) like sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats. Monitoring these statistics is crucial for both performance optimization and resource governance. It helps identify unused or redundant indexes that consume storage space and maintenance resources without providing query benefits, thereby reducing the overall attack surface and overhead. It assists in detecting missing indexes where queries perform costly table scans instead of efficient seeks, which can lead to severe application slowdowns and poor user experience.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Monitor Index Usage Patterns Using DMVs for Performance Optimization and Resource Governance' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Monitor Index Usage Patterns Using DMVs for Performance Optimization and Resource Governance' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Separate Data, Log, and TempDB onto Different Physical Drives
Description: This check validates the physical separation of critical SQL Server components - data files, transaction log files, and tempdb - onto distinct storage volumes or drives. Optimal performance requires matching the I/O pattern of each component to the underlying storage capabilities; data files benefit from high random I/O throughput, transaction logs require sustained sequential write speed, and tempdb needs dedicated, fast resources. Failure to separate these components leads to resource contention (I/O bottlenecking), which significantly degrades overall database performance and stability across mixed workloads.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Separate Data, Log, and TempDB onto Different Physical Drives' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Separate Data, Log, and TempDB onto Different Physical Drives' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect Non-Default Server Configuration Values
Description:
Counts server configuration options whose value_in_use differs from the default (zero), excluding max server memory (MB) and min server memory (MB) since those are universally expected to be non-default in production environments.

Every non-default server configuration setting represents a deviation from Microsoft tested and documented behavior. Undocumented changes survive version upgrades, complicate disaster recovery, and increase troubleshooting time. The check performs automated detection through sys.configurations, providing a binary or tiered verdict with specific messaging that identifies the deviation. This enables integration into monitoring pipelines for automated alerting within 24 hours of any change.

The query targets sys.configurations and evaluates the current state against documented best practices. The result column provides a PASS/FAIL/INFO/WARN verdict.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Non-Default Server Configuration Values' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Non-Default Server Configuration Values' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect Non-Zero Server Configuration Values
Description:
Counts all server configuration options where value_in_use is greater than zero. Unlike BLITZ1001 which excludes memory settings, this check includes everything, providing a broader audit of configuration state.

Some server configuration options legitimately require non-zero values, but each non-zero value should be documented. The broader scope of this check catches all deviations, including ones that BLITZ1001 might miss. The check performs automated detection through sys.configurations, providing a binary or tiered verdict with specific messaging that identifies the deviation. This enables integration into monitoring pipelines for automated alerting within 24 hours of any change.

The query targets sys.configurations and evaluates the current state against documented best practices. The result column provides a PASS/FAIL/INFO/WARN verdict.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Non-Zero Server Configuration Values' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Non-Zero Server Configuration Values' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Detect Non-Default Value for remote admin connections
Description:
This check detects whether the remote admin connections server configuration option has been modified from its default value in SQL Server. Server configuration options control fundamental SQL Server behaviors including resource allocation, security boundaries, and feature availability. Unauthorized or undocumented changes to these settings introduce operational risk - when configurations diverge from baselines without justification, troubleshooting becomes more complex and recovery more uncertain.

The remote admin connections setting deviates from its Microsoft-recommended default. Any non-default value should have documented justification because it affects SQL Server behavior in ways that may surprise future administrators, complicate migrations, or create compliance findings during configuration audits. The issue is compounded when non-default settings survive SQL Server version upgrades, causing unexpected behavior regression in newer environments that expect standard default configurations.

This check queries sys.configurations WHERE value_in_use indicates a non-default state for the remote admin connections option. A PASS verdict means the setting is at its documented default. INFO means the setting has been changed and should be reviewed against your organization's approved configuration baseline.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Non-Default Value for remote admin connections' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Non-Default Value for remote admin connections' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'remote admin connections' = 1. Allows the Dedicated Admin Connection (DAC) to be used from remote connections (not just localhost). Default 0 (localhost only). Enabling this is useful for remote DBA access when the server is under extreme load. Ensure it is restricted to trusted IP ranges at the firewall level.
FullStoryAction: remote admin connections. Used by DBAs to execute diagnostic functions even when the server is locked or running in an abnormal state and not responding to a SQL Server Database Engine connection.

Name: Detect Non-Default Value for remote login timeout
Description:
This check detects whether the remote login timeout server configuration option has been modified from its default value in SQL Server. Server configuration options control fundamental SQL Server behaviors including resource allocation, security boundaries, and feature availability. Unauthorized or undocumented changes to these settings introduce operational risk - when configurations diverge from baselines without justification, troubleshooting becomes more complex and recovery more uncertain.

The remote login timeout setting deviates from its Microsoft-recommended default. Any non-default value should have documented justification because it affects SQL Server behavior in ways that may surprise future administrators, complicate migrations, or create compliance findings during configuration audits. The issue is compounded when non-default settings survive SQL Server version upgrades, causing unexpected behavior regression in newer environments that expect standard default configurations.

This check queries sys.configurations WHERE value_in_use indicates a non-default state for the remote login timeout option. A PASS verdict means the setting is at its documented default. INFO means the setting has been changed and should be reviewed against your organization's approved configuration baseline.
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Detect Non-Default Value for remote login timeout' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Detect Non-Default Value for remote login timeout' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'remote login timeout (s)'. Default 10s. Controls timeout for establishing linked server connections. Non-default value (often increased to 30â€“60s) typically indicates linked server stability issues. Review if linked servers are still in active use.
FullStoryAction: remote login timeout (s). Specifies the number of seconds to wait before returning from a failed attempt to log in to a remote server

Name: Backups happening in the last 7 days
Description: Verifies full backups completed within 7 days. A server with no recent backups is one disk failure away from total data loss. This is the single most basic check and the one that burns people most often.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
       WHEN EXISTS (
               SELECT 1 AS CheckID ,
  d.[name] AS DatabaseName ,
  1 AS Priority ,
  'Backup' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Backups Not Performed Recently' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/nobak' AS URL ,
  'Last backed up: '
  + COALESCE(CAST(MAX(b.backup_finish_date) AS VARCHAR(25)),'never') AS Details
FROM master.sys.databases d
  LEFT OUTER JOIN msdb.dbo.backupset b ON d.name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS = b.database_name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS
     AND b.type = 'D'
     AND b.server_name = SERVERPROPERTY('ServerName') /*Backupset ran on current server */
WHERE d.database_id <> 2 /* Bonus points if you know what that means */
  AND d.state NOT IN(1, 6, 10) /* Not currently offline or restoring, like log shipping databases */
  AND d.is_in_standby = 0 /* Not a log shipping target database */
  AND d.source_database_id IS NULL /* Excludes database snapshots */
  -- AND d.name NOT IN ( SELECT DISTINCT
     -- DatabaseName
    --FROM #SkipChecks
  --  WHERE CheckID IS NULL OR CheckID = 1)
  /*
  The above NOT IN filters out the databases we're not supposed to check.
  */
GROUP BY d.name
HAVING MAX(b.backup_finish_date) <= DATEADD(dd,
     -7, GETDATE())
  OR MAX(b.backup_finish_date) IS NULL
       ) THEN 1
       ELSE 0
      END
RecommendedAction: Schedule full backups for every database. Verify with: SELECT database_name, MAX(backup_finish_date) FROM msdb.dbo.backupset WHERE type='D' GROUP BY database_name.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Backups happening in the last 7 days' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.

Name: Transaction Log Backup taking place
Description: Checks log backups are running on schedule. Without log backups, the transaction log grows until the drive fills, the database goes read-only, and production stops cold.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
       WHEN EXISTS (
        SELECT DISTINCT
 2 AS CheckID ,
 d.name AS DatabaseName ,
 1 AS Priority ,
 'Backup' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Full Recovery Model w/o Log Backups' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/biglogs' AS URL ,
 ( 'The ' + CAST(CAST((SELECT ((SUM([mf].[size]) * 8.) / 1024.) FROM sys.[master_files] AS [mf] WHERE [mf].[database_id] = d.[database_id] AND [mf].[type_desc] = 'LOG') AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS VARCHAR(30)) + 'MB log file has not been backed up in the last week.' ) AS Details
FROM master.sys.databases d
--LEFT JOIN #DBCCs ll On ll.DbName = d.name And ll.Field = 'dbi_LastLogBackupTime'
WHERE d.recovery_model IN ( 1, 2 )
 AND d.database_id NOT IN ( 2, 3 )
 AND d.source_database_id IS NULL
 AND d.state NOT IN(1, 6, 10) /* Not currently offline or restoring, like log shipping databases */
 AND d.is_in_standby = 0 /* Not a log shipping target database */
 AND d.source_database_id IS NULL /* Excludes database snapshots */
 AND (
 (
 /* We couldn't get a value from the DBCC DBINFO data so let's check the msdb backup history information */
 --[ll].[Value] Is Null
 --AND 
 NOT EXISTS ( SELECT *
  FROM msdb.dbo.backupset b
  WHERE d.name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS = b.database_name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS
   AND b.type = 'L'
   AND b.backup_finish_date >= DATEADD(dd,-7, GETDATE())
 )
 )
 
)
       ) THEN 1
       ELSE 0
      END
RecommendedAction: Schedule log backups every 5-15 minutes for databases in FULL recovery model. Check with: SELECT database_name, MAX(backup_finish_date) FROM msdb.dbo.backupset WHERE type='L' GROUP BY database_name.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Transaction Log Backup taking place' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Without log backups, point-in-time recovery is impossible. A data-loss event with no recent backups is a resume-generating incident.

Name: MSDB Backup History Not Purged
Description: Checks for old backup history in the MSDB database. Not purging this history can lead to a full C drive, slow MSDB access, and inefficient monitoring tools.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT TOP 1
 3 AS CheckID ,
 'msdb' ,
 200 AS Priority ,
 'Backup' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'MSDB Backup History Not Purged' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/history' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database backup history retained back to '
  + CAST(bs.backup_start_date AS VARCHAR(20)) ) AS Details
 FROM msdb.dbo.backupset bs
 LEFT OUTER JOIN msdb.dbo.restorehistory rh ON bs.database_name = rh.destination_database_name
 WHERE rh.destination_database_name IS NULL
 ORDER BY bs.backup_start_date ASC

 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'MSDB Backup History Not Purged' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
