ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'MSDB Backup History Not Purged' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Backup History is cleared often on all server(s)
CheckClearSimplified: Backup History is cleared often on all servers
NextAction: msdb.dbo.backupset , checks whether the backup history table has grown beyond a threshold (typically > 10,000 rows without purge). Large backup history tables slow down backup/restore commands because SQL reads from this table for every operation. Run EXEC msdb.dbo.sp_delete_backuphistory to purge. Set a retention policy via SQL Agent maintenance plan.
FullStoryAction: Not maintaining this table can negatively impact backup and restore times.

Name: Sysadmins on SQL server
Description: Identifies users with sysadmin privileges in SQL Server, which can pose a security risk if not properly managed.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT 4 AS CheckID ,
 230 AS Priority ,
 'Security' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Sysadmins' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/sa' AS URL ,
 ( 'Login [' + l.name
 + '] is a sysadmin - meaning they can do absolutely anything in SQL Server, including dropping databases or hiding their tracks.' ) AS Details
FROM master.sys.syslogins l
WHERE l.sysadmin = 1
 AND l.name <> SUSER_SNAME(0x01)
 AND l.denylogin = 0
 AND l.name NOT LIKE 'NT SERVICE\%'
 AND l.name <> 'l_certSignSmDetach' /* Added in SQL 2016 */
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Sysadmins on SQL server' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Sysadmins on SQL server' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_principals where is_sysadmin = 1. sysadmin is the most privileged SQL Server role , equivalent to root. Accounts with sysadmin have unrestricted access to all databases, all server objects, and can impersonate any login. List via SELECT name, type_desc FROM sys.server_principals WHERE IS_SRVROLEMEMBER('sysadmin',name)=1. Remove sysadmin from any account that does not absolutely require it; use principle of least privilege.
FullStoryAction: Sysadmins can do absolutely anything in SQL Server. Modifying security settings amongst others.

Name: Security Admins on SQL server
Description: Identifies users with security admin privileges in SQL Server, which can pose a security risk if not properly managed.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT 5 AS CheckID ,
 230 AS Priority ,
 'Security' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Security Admins' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/sa' AS URL ,
 ( 'Login [' + l.name
 + '] is a security admin - meaning they can give themselves permission to do absolutely anything in SQL Server, including dropping databases or hiding their tracks.' ) AS Details
FROM master.sys.syslogins l
WHERE l.securityadmin = 1
 AND l.name <> SUSER_SNAME(0x01)
 AND l.denylogin = 0
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Security Admins on SQL server' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Security Admins on SQL server' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_role_members where role_principal_id = SUSER_ID('securityadmin'). securityadmin members can grant any permission and create logins , effectively elevating to sysadmin capability. Treat securityadmin membership as equivalent to sysadmin for risk purposes. Review all securityadmin members and reduce to those that genuinely require it.
FullStoryAction: These users can give themselves permission to do absolutely anything in SQL Server

Name: Jobs owned by users
Description: Checks for SQL Server Agent jobs owned by user accounts, which can cause jobs to fail if the user account is dropped or disabled.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 6 AS CheckID ,
 230 AS Priority ,
 'Security' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Jobs Owned By Users' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/owners' AS URL ,
 ( 'Job [' + j.name + '] is owned by ['
 + SUSER_SNAME(j.owner_sid)
 + '] - meaning if their login is disabled or not available due to Active Directory problems, the job will stop working.' ) AS Details
FROM msdb.dbo.sysjobs j
WHERE j.enabled = 1
 AND SUSER_SNAME(j.owner_sid) <> SUSER_SNAME(0x01)
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Jobs owned by users' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Jobs owned by users' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ SQL Jobs are Owned by Users
CheckClearSimplified: No SQL Jobs are Owned by Users
NextAction: . msdb.dbo.sysjobs where owner_sid != SYSLOGIN('sa'). Jobs owned by user accounts can fail if the owner's login is disabled or dropped. Additionally, the job runs with the permissions of the owner in certain contexts. Standardise job ownership to SA: EXEC msdb.dbo.sp_update_job @job_name=N'...', @owner_login_name=N'sa'.
FullStoryAction: Disabling these users in AD will cause the job will stop working.

Name: Stored Procedures running at startup
Description: Checks for stored procedures that are configured to run automatically when SQL Server starts. These procedures can be a security risk if their purpose is unclear.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 7 AS CheckID ,
 230 AS Priority ,
 'Security' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Stored Procedure Runs at Startup' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/startup' AS URL ,
 ( 'Stored procedure [master].['
 + r.SPECIFIC_SCHEMA + '].['
 + r.SPECIFIC_NAME
 + '] runs automatically when SQL Server starts up. Make sure you know exactly what this stored procedure is doing, because it could pose a security risk.' ) AS Details
FROM master.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.ROUTINES r
WHERE OBJECTPROPERTY(OBJECT_ID(ROUTINE_NAME),
  'ExecIsStartup') = 1
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Stored Procedures running at startup' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Stored Procedures running at startup' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Stored Procedures Run at SQL Start-up
CheckClearSimplified: No Stored Procedure Run at SQL Start-up
NextAction: sys.objects with startup extended stored procedure (XP) configuration. Startup stored procedures execute automatically at instance start with sysadmin privileges. A malicious actor who can create/modify startup procs gains persistent code execution. Audit: EXEC sp_procoption @ProcName=N'<proc>', @OptionName='startup', @OptionValue='true' , remove any undocumented startup procs.
FullStoryAction: These procedures could pose a security risk.

Name: Server Audits running
Description: Checks if SQL Server Audits are running. Misconfigured audits can impact performance and consume disk space.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
      SELECT 8 AS CheckID,
  230 AS Priority,
  'Security' AS FindingsGroup,
  'Server Audits Running' AS Finding,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/audits' AS URL,
  ('SQL Server built-in audit functionality is being used by server audit: ' + [name]) AS Details 
  FROM sys.dm_server_audit_status --OPTION (RECOMPILE)
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Server Audits running' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Server Audits running' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_audits WHERE is_state_enabled = 1. SQL Server Audit is configured and running. Informational , auditing is a positive security control. Verify: (1) audit destination (file, application log, or security log), (2) audit action groups cover required events (SCHEMA_OBJECT_ACCESS_GROUP for data access, LOGIN_CHANGE_PASSWORD_GROUP for security events), (3) audit file path has sufficient disk space, (4) ON_FAILURE = CONTINUE vs SHUTDOWN SERVER (critical environments should use SHUTDOWN to prevent unaudited access).
FullStoryAction: SQL Server has built-in audit functionality, this can be active on any SQL server.

Name: Resource Governor Enabled
Description: Checks if the SQL Server Resource Governor is enabled. If not configured correctly, it can negatively impact performance.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 10 AS CheckID,
  100 AS Priority,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup,
  'Resource Governor Enabled' AS Finding,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/rg' AS URL,
  ('Resource Governor is enabled. Queries may be throttled. Make sure you understand how the Classifier Function is configured.') AS Details FROM sys.resource_governor_configuration WHERE is_enabled = 1 
  
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Resource Governor Enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Resource Governor Enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Resource Governor Enabled. This could have performance impacts, or be used to mitigate them.
NextAction: sys.resource_governor_configuration where is_enabled = 1. Resource Governor partitions CPU and memory across workload groups. A misconfigured Resource Governor can silently throttle critical workloads below acceptable thresholds. Review sys.dm_resource_governor_resource_pools and sys.dm_resource_governor_workload_groups for current resource limits and actual usage.
FullStoryAction: Resource Governor is enabled.  Queries may be throttled. Make sure you understand how the Classifier Function is configured.

Name: Server triggers enabled
Description: Checks for enabled server-level triggers. These triggers can impact performance and should be reviewed.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 11 AS CheckID,
  100 AS Priority,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup,
  'Server Triggers Enabled' AS Finding,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/logontriggers/' AS URL,
  ('Server Trigger [' + [name] ++ '] is enabled. Make sure you understand what that trigger is doing - the less work it does, the better.') AS Details
  FROM sys.server_triggers
  WHERE is_disabled = 0 AND is_ms_shipped = 0 AND name NOT LIKE 'rds^_%' ESCAPE '^'
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Server triggers enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Server triggers enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.server_triggers where is_disabled = 0. Server-level DDL triggers fire on DDL events across the entire instance (CREATE TABLE, ALTER LOGIN, etc.). They add overhead to every DDL operation. A poorly-written server trigger can cause deadlocks or block administrative operations. Audit each trigger's purpose and performance impact.
FullStoryAction: Triggers on tables bring business logic to the database.  Make sure you understand what triggers do and that triggers do the least amount of work required.

Name: Database Auto-Close enabled
Description: Auto-Close is enabled on one or more user databases. This causes SQL Server to shut down the database and release resources when all connections close. Re-opening the database incurs significant overhead and can cause performance degradation on subsequent connections.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 12 AS CheckID ,
 [name] AS DatabaseName ,
 10 AS Priority ,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Auto-Close Enabled' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/autoclose' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database [' + [name]
 + '] has auto-close enabled. This setting can dramatically decrease performance.' ) AS Details
FROM sys.databases
WHERE is_auto_close_on = 1
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Auto-Close enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Auto-Close enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.databases where is_auto_close_on = 1. AUTO_CLOSE evicts the database from memory when all connections close. On next connection, SQL re-opens the database files, rebuilds the system-table cache, and re-allocates buffer pool pages. This cold-start penalty causes I/O spikes and increased latency on OLTP databases with short connection bursts. Disable: ALTER DATABASE [db] SET AUTO_CLOSE OFF.
FullStoryAction: This feature puts a database in a sleeping state and will impact IO performance

Name: Database Auto-Shrink enabled
Description: Detects AUTO_SHRINK enabled. Shrink operations fragment indexes at the page level. You trade a few GB of free space for 30-60% query slowdown.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 13 AS CheckID ,
 [name] AS DatabaseName ,
 10 AS Priority ,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Auto-Shrink Enabled' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/autoshrink' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database [' + [name]
 + '] has auto-shrink enabled. This setting can dramatically decrease performance.' ) AS Details
FROM sys.databases
WHERE is_auto_shrink_on = 1
 AND state <> 6 /* Offline */
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Run ALTER DATABASE [name] SET AUTO_SHRINK OFF for every database. Disable any maintenance plan shrink tasks.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Auto-Shrink enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Auto-shrink is the most common self-inflicted performance wound in SQL Server. It fragments indexes globally in exchange for temporary disk space.
NextAction: sys.databases where is_auto_shrink_on = 1. AUTO_SHRINK periodically attempts to reduce file size, generating massive random read I/O as it moves pages from the end of the file forward. Immediately after shrink, the released space is reallocated on next load, causing the file to grow again. This creates a perpetual shrink/grow cycle that fragments indexes and stresses I/O. Disable: ALTER DATABASE [db] SET AUTO_SHRINK OFF. Never use SHRINKDATABASE except in extreme emergencies.
FullStoryAction: This feature attempts to reduce overall database size and can generate a high amount of IO, negatively impacting performance

Name: Corruption checks not optimal
Description: One or more databases do not have PAGE_VERIFY set to CHECKSUM. CHECKSUM page verification allows SQL Server to detect I/O corruption and data integrity issues. Databases using TORN_PAGE_DETECTION or NONE provide weaker integrity guarantees.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

 SELECT 14 AS CheckID,
 [name] as DatabaseName,
 50 AS Priority,
 'Reliability' AS FindingsGroup,
 'Page Verification Not Optimal' AS Finding,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/torn' AS URL,
 ('Database [' + [name] + '] has ' + [page_verify_option_desc] + ' for page verification. SQL Server may have a harder time recognizing and recovering from storage corruption. Consider using CHECKSUM instead.') COLLATE database_default AS Details
 FROM sys.databases
 WHERE page_verify_option < 2
 AND name <> 'tempdb'
 AND state NOT IN (1, 6) /* Restoring, Offline */
  
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Corruption checks not optimal' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Corruption checks not optimal' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ databases have automatic corruption checking turned off
CheckClearSimplified: All databases have automatic corruption checking turned on
NextAction: Reads default trace for DBCC events. Cross-checks whether DBCC CHECKDB was run without the WITH PHYSICAL_ONLY option on very large databases (causing excessive runtime) or with DATA_PURITY skipped. Ensure the maintenance script runs WITH DATA_PURITY on databases migrated from older versions.
FullStoryAction: This can lead to undetected corruption on databases. Good luck explaining this to the board.

Name: Auto-Create Statistics Disabled
Description: Auto-Create Statistics is disabled on one or more databases. SQL Server relies on statistics to build efficient query plans. When disabled, the query optimizer lacks accurate data distribution information, leading to poor plan choices and degraded query performance.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  15 AS CheckID ,
          [name] AS DatabaseName ,
          110 AS Priority ,
          'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
          'Auto-Create Stats Disabled' AS Finding ,
          'https://www.brentozar.com/go/acs' AS URL ,
          ( 'Database [' + [name]
            + '] has auto-create-stats disabled.  SQL Server uses statistics to build better execution plans, and without the ability to automatically create more, performance may suffer.' ) AS Details
        FROM    sys.databases
        WHERE   is_auto_create_stats_on = 0
  
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Auto-Create Statistics Disabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Auto-Create Statistics Disabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: Auto-Create Stats enabled on all databases
NextAction: sys.databases where is_auto_create_stats_on = 0. Auto-create statistics allows SQL Server to create single-column statistics on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses. Without it, the optimizer has no cardinality information for unindexed columns and may choose full scans or bad join orders. Disable only if you are manually managing all statistics.
FullStoryAction: SQL server will attempt to automatically create statistics to improve performance

Name: Auto-Update Stats Disabled
Description: Auto-Update Statistics is disabled on one or more databases. When statistics become stale due to data changes, the query optimizer continues using outdated information, which can lead to suboptimal query execution plans and poor performance.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 16 AS CheckID ,
 [name] AS DatabaseName ,
 110 AS Priority ,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Auto-Update Stats Disabled' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/aus' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database [' + [name]
 + '] has auto-update-stats disabled. SQL Server uses statistics to build better execution plans, and without the ability to automatically update them, performance may suffer.' ) AS Details
FROM sys.databases
WHERE is_auto_update_stats_on = 0  
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Auto-Update Stats Disabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Auto-Update Stats Disabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: Auto-Update Stats enabled on all databases
NextAction: sys.databases where is_auto_update_stats_on = 0. Auto-update statistics triggers a statistics update when 20% of rows change (or dynamic threshold on SQL 2016+). Without it, statistics become stale and the optimizer makes increasingly bad decisions as data changes. Never disable without a documented replacement (scheduled manual updates with Ola Hallengren).
FullStoryAction: SQL server will attempt to automatically update statistics to improve performance

Name: Stats Updated Asynchronously
Description: Auto-Update Statistics Asynchronously is enabled on one or more databases. Queries may use stale statistics for the current execution while an update runs in the background, potentially causing poor plan choices until the update completes.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 17 AS CheckID ,
 [name] AS DatabaseName ,
 150 AS Priority ,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Stats Updated Asynchronously' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/asyncstats' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database [' + [name]
 + '] has auto-update-stats-async enabled. When SQL Server gets a query for a table with out-of-date statistics, it will run the query with the stats it has - while updating stats to make later queries better. The initial run of the query may suffer, though.' ) AS Details
FROM sys.databases
WHERE is_auto_update_stats_async_on = 1 
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Stats Updated Asynchronously' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Stats Updated Asynchronously' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Asynchronous Stats Updates enabled on @ databases
CheckClearSimplified: Asynchronous Stats Updates are not enabled on any databases
NextAction: sys.databases where is_auto_update_stats_async_on = 1. Async stats updates allow a query to run with the old (stale) stats while the update runs in the background. The query may get a bad plan this execution, but the next execution will use fresh stats. Good for OLTP where a blocking synchronous stats update would cause query timeouts. Acceptable trade-off , not a problem per se.
FullStoryAction: This may impact performance on queries that trigger the statistic updates.

Name: Replication in use
Description: One or more databases are configured for replication (transactional, merge, or snapshot). Replication adds overhead to DML operations through replication triggers and log reader activity. This is informational to note its presence and associated performance implications.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN
     EXISTS (             
SELECT  19 AS CheckID ,
        [name] AS DatabaseName ,
        200 AS Priority ,
        'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
        'Replication In Use' AS Finding ,
        'https://www.brentozar.com/go/repl' AS URL ,
        ( 'Database [' + [name]
          + '] is a replication publisher, subscriber, or distributor.' ) AS Details
FROM    sys.databases
WHERE    (is_published = 1
        OR is_subscribed = 1
        OR is_merge_published = 1
                  OR is_distributor = 1)            
  ) 
        THEN 1
     ELSE 0
    END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Replication in use' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Replication in use' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.databases where is_published = 1 or is_subscribed = 1 or is_distributor = 1. Replication is active. Monitor distribution latency, agent status, and undistributed commands in the distribution database (msdb..MSdistribution_commands). Replication failures accumulate silently until the distribution database fills or latency breaches SLA thresholds.
FullStoryAction: This check gets triggered where the server is a replication publisher, subscriber, or acts as a distributor.

Name: Date correlation
Description: Date Correlation Optimization is enabled on one or more databases. This feature is rarely needed and can cause the optimizer to make incorrect assumptions about date-correlated tables, potentially leading to poor query plans.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

 SELECT 20 AS CheckID ,
 [name] AS DatabaseName ,
 200 AS Priority ,
 'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Date Correlation On' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/corr' AS URL ,
 ( 'Database [' + [name]
  + '] has date correlation enabled. This is not a default setting, and it has some performance overhead. It tells SQL Server that date fields in two tables are related, and SQL Server maintains statistics showing that relation.' ) AS Details
 FROM sys.databases
 WHERE is_date_correlation_on = 1 
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Date correlation' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Date correlation' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Databases have date correlation enabled.
NextAction: sys.databases where is_date_correlation_on = 1. Date correlation optimization maintains statistics about date column correlations between tables for a small set of designated tables. Rarely used. Adding overhead to datetime column updates. Confirm it is actively used or disable: ALTER DATABASE [db] SET DATE_CORRELATION_OPTIMIZATION OFF.
FullStoryAction: Date correlation allows the SQL Engine to better filter related Date columns and can improve performance.

Name: Database Encrypted
Description: One or more databases have Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) enabled. TDE encrypts data at rest but introduces CPU overhead for encryption/decryption operations. Encrypted databases also require backup encryption key management.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 21 AS CheckID,
  [name] as DatabaseName,
  200 AS Priority,
  'Informational' AS FindingsGroup,
  'Database Encrypted' AS Finding,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/tde' AS URL,
  ('Database [' + [name] + '] has Transparent Data Encryption enabled. Make absolutely sure you have backed up the certificate and private key, or else you will not be able to restore this database.') AS Details
  FROM sys.databases
  WHERE is_encrypted = 1 
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Encrypted' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Encrypted' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: System Database on C Drive
Description: System Database on C Drive - File Configuration (sp_Blitz CheckID 24)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT DISTINCT
  24 AS CheckID ,
  DB_NAME(database_id) AS DatabaseName ,
  170 AS Priority ,
  'File Configuration' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'System Database on C Drive' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/cdrive' AS URL ,
  ( 'The ' + DB_NAME(database_id)
    + ' database has a file on the C drive.  Putting system databases on the C drive runs the risk of crashing the server when it runs out of space.' ) AS Details
FROM    sys.master_files
WHERE   UPPER(LEFT(physical_name, 1)) = 'C'
  AND DB_NAME(database_id) IN ( 'master',
          'model', 'msdb' )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'System Database on C Drive' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'System Database on C Drive' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some System Database on C Drive
CheckClearSimplified: No System Database on C Drive
NextAction: sys.master_files where physical_name LIKE 'C:%' for system databases (master, model, msdb). System databases on C: (OS drive) create I/O contention with the OS. More critically, if C: fills, SQL Server may be unable to write to system databases, causing instance failures. Move system databases to a dedicated drive.
FullStoryAction: Putting databases on the C drive runs the risk of crashing the server when it runs out of space.

Name: TempDB on C Drive
Description: Detects when the tempdb database has data or log files located on the C drive. TempDB frequently grows unpredictably, putting your server at risk of running out of C drive space and crashing hard. The C drive is also often much slower than other drives, so performance may be suffering even if space is not immediately a concern.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(
    SELECT 1
    FROM sys.master_files
    WHERE UPPER(LEFT(physical_name, 1)) = 'C'
      AND DB_NAME(database_id) = 'tempdb'
) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction:
Move tempdb data and log files to a separate fast dedicated drive.
Pre-size tempdb files to avoid autogrowth events and use fixed-size growth increments.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when tempdb has no files on the C drive. Returns FAIL when tempdb files reside on C.
NextAction: sys.master_files where physical_name LIKE 'C:%' for tempdb. TempDB on the C: drive (OS drive) shares I/O with the Windows OS, event logs, and page file. Tempdb is typically the highest-I/O database. Move tempdb data and log files to a dedicated high-performance drive. Also: if C: fills up, the OS can crash.
FullStoryAction: TempDB frequently grows unpredictably, putting your server at risk of running out of C drive space and crashing.

Name: User Database on C Drive
Description: One or more user database data or log files are located on the C: (system) drive. Storing user databases on the OS drive risks filling the system partition, which can crash SQL Server and the operating system.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT DISTINCT
  26 AS CheckID ,
  DB_NAME(database_id) AS DatabaseName ,
  20 AS Priority ,
  'Reliability' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'User Databases on C Drive' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/cdrive' AS URL ,
  ( 'The ' + DB_NAME(database_id)
    + ' database has a file on the C drive.  Putting databases on the C drive runs the risk of crashing the server when it runs out of space.' ) AS Details
FROM    sys.master_files
WHERE   UPPER(LEFT(physical_name, 1)) = 'C'
  AND DB_NAME(database_id) NOT IN ( 'master','model', 'msdb', 'tempdb' )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'User Database on C Drive' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'User Database on C Drive' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some User Databases on C Drive
CheckClearSimplified: No User Databases on C Drive
NextAction: sys.master_files where physical_name LIKE 'C:%' for user databases. User databases on the OS drive create I/O contention and risk the OS drive filling to capacity. Move database files to a dedicated data volume.
FullStoryAction: Putting databases on the C drive runs the risk of crashing the server when it runs out of space.

Name: Tables in the Master Database
Description: User-created tables were found in the master database. Master should only contain system objects. User tables in master are not backed up by default database backup jobs and can interfere with SQL Server upgrades and maintenance.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  27 AS CheckID ,
  'master' AS DatabaseName ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Tables in the Master Database' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/mastuser' AS URL ,
  ( 'The ' + name
    + ' table in the master database was created by end users on '
    + CAST(create_date AS VARCHAR(20))
    + '. Tables in the master database may not be restored in the event of a disaster.' ) AS Details
FROM    master.sys.tables
WHERE   is_ms_shipped = 0
  AND   name NOT IN ('CommandLog','SqlServerVersions','$ndo$srvproperty')
  AND name NOT LIKE 'rds^_%' ESCAPE '^'
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Tables in the Master Database' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Tables in the Master Database' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: There are @ Tables in the Master Database.
CheckClearSimplified: There are no Tables in the Master Database.
NextAction: sys.objects WHERE database_id = db_id('master') AND type IN ('U','V','P'). User-created tables in master complicate upgrades and instance refreshes. Objects in master persist across database backups, making recovery scenarios more complex. Move user objects to a dedicated administration database.
FullStoryAction: Unexpected usage of the Master database can cause data growth which may lead to longer recovery times.

Name: Tables in the MSDB Database
Description: User-created tables were found in the msdb database. MSDB is a system database for SQL Server Agent, backups, and maintenance. User tables in msdb risk data loss during upgrades and may cause maintenance complications.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  28 AS CheckID ,
        'msdb' AS DatabaseName ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Tables in the MSDB Database' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/msdbuser' AS URL ,
  ( 'The ' + name
    + ' table in the msdb database was created by end users on '
    + CAST(create_date AS VARCHAR(20))
    + '. Tables in the msdb database may not be restored in the event of a disaster.' ) AS Details
FROM    msdb.sys.tables
WHERE   is_ms_shipped = 0 AND name NOT LIKE '%DTA_%'
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Tables in the MSDB Database' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Tables in the MSDB Database' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Tables in the MSDB Database
NextAction: sys.objects in msdb database , user-created tables beyond the standard msdb schema. User tables in msdb are at risk of being overwritten during SQL Server upgrades. Move to a dedicated DBA database.
FullStoryAction: Tables in the msdb database may not be restored in the event of a disaster.

Name: Tables in the Model Database
Description: User-created tables were found in the model database. Model is the template for all new databases created on the instance. Objects in model are copied to every new database, which is rarely intended and can cause unexpected schema in new databases.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  29 AS CheckID ,
        'model' AS DatabaseName ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Tables in the Model Database' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/model' AS URL ,
  ( 'The ' + name
    + ' table in the model database was created by end users on '
    + CAST(create_date AS VARCHAR(20))
    + '. Tables in the model database are automatically copied into all new databases.' ) AS Details
FROM    model.sys.tables
WHERE   is_ms_shipped = 0
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Tables in the Model Database' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Tables in the Model Database' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Tables in the Model Database
NextAction: sys.objects in model database. Objects in model are copied to every new database created. Unintended objects in model propagate to all future databases, creating unexpected schema and potentially sensitive data in new databases. Audit and clean model regularly.
FullStoryAction: Tables in the model database are automatically copied into all new databases

Name: Not All Alerts Configured
Description: SQL Server does not have alerts configured for all critical severity levels (17-25) and error numbers (823, 824, 825). Without complete alert coverage, DBAs may not be notified of serious errors including I/O failures and potential corruption events.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  30 AS CheckID ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Not All Alerts Configured' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/alert' AS URL ,
  ( 'Not all SQL Server Agent alerts have been configured.  This is a free, easy way to get notified of corruption, job failures, or major outages even before monitoring systems pick it up.' ) AS Details
  WHERE (
  SELECT COUNT(1) FROM   msdb.dbo.sysalerts
  WHERE  severity BETWEEN 19 AND 25) < 7
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Not All Alerts Configured' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Not All Alerts Configured' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Some other Alerts are missing or not configured
CheckClearSimplified: All Alerts seem to be configured
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysalerts , not all recommended sp_Blitz alerts are configured. sp_Blitz recommends a baseline set of SQL Agent alerts covering: severity 17â€“25, error 823 (I/O error), 824 (consistency error), 825 (read retry), 832 (corrupt page). Run sp_Blitz's alert setup script or Brent Ozar's 'sp_Blitz_Alerts' to configure the complete recommended alert set.
FullStoryAction: Not all SQL Server Agent alerts have been configured

Name: No Operators Configured/Enabled
Description: No Operators Configured/Enabled - Monitoring (sp_Blitz CheckID 31)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  31 AS CheckID ,
 200 AS Priority ,
 'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'No Operators Configured/Enabled' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/op' AS URL ,
 ( 'No SQL Server Agent operators (emails) have been configured.  This is a free, easy way to get notified of corruption, job failures, or major outages even before monitoring systems pick it up.' ) AS Details     WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT  *
FROM    msdb.dbo.sysoperators
 WHERE   enabled = 1 )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'No Operators Configured/Enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'No Operators Configured/Enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysoperators , no enabled operator with an email address. Alerts without operators have nowhere to send notifications. Create an operator: EXEC msdb.dbo.sp_add_operator @name=N'DBA Team', @email_address=N'dba@company.com'. Configure Database Mail first (sp_configure 'Database Mail XPs').
FullStoryAction: No SQL Server Agent operators (emails) have been configured

Name: Triggers on Tables
Description: One or more user tables have DML triggers defined. Triggers fire on every affected row operation and can significantly impact DML performance. They add hidden complexity and can cause unexpected behavior when not well-documented.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.triggers WHERE parent_class = 1 AND is_disabled = 0 AND parent_id NOT IN(SELECT object_id FROM sys.objects WHERE type = 'U')) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Triggers on Tables' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Triggers on Tables' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.triggers joined to sys.objects. Triggers add hidden overhead to DML operations , they fire within the transaction, increasing lock hold duration. Multiple nested triggers can cause deadlocks. Auditing triggers (logging every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) are a common source of blocking on high-throughput tables. Review trigger logic for set-based vs cursor-based patterns.
FullStoryAction: It is important to note triggers for performance considerations

Name: Enterprise Edition Features In Use
Description: Enterprise Edition-specific features (such as partitioning, compression, or In-Memory OLTP) are in use on this instance. If the license is downgraded or the database is moved to a non-Enterprise edition, these features will no longer be accessible.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_db_persisted_sku_features) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Enterprise Edition Features In Use' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Enterprise Edition Features In Use' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: Enterprise Edition Features not In Use
NextAction: SERVERPROPERTY('EditionID') , detects Enterprise Edition features (Advanced HADR, partitioning, data compression, resource governor, online operations) in use on a non-Enterprise licence. If the server is downgraded to Standard Edition, these features stop working. Audit Enterprise feature usage before any edition downgrade.
FullStoryAction: Enterprise Edition features only work on Enterprise Edition SQL Servers. Databases with these features will fail on Standard Edition servers.

Name: Database Corruption Detected
Description: Database corruption has been detected via DBCC CHECKDB results stored in msdb or the default trace. Corruption can lead to data loss and application failures. Immediate investigation and restoration from a clean backup may be required.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT DISTINCT
    34 AS CheckID ,
    db.name ,
    1 AS Priority ,
    'Corruption' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Database Corruption Detected' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/repair' AS URL ,
    ( 'Database mirroring has automatically repaired at least one corrupt page in the last 30 days. For more information, query the DMV sys.dm_db_mirroring_auto_page_repair.' ) AS Details
    FROM (SELECT rp2.database_id, rp2.modification_time
   FROM sys.dm_db_mirroring_auto_page_repair rp2
   WHERE rp2.[database_id] not in (
   SELECT db2.[database_id]
   FROM sys.databases as db2
   WHERE db2.[state] = 1
   ) ) as rp
    INNER JOIN master.sys.databases db ON rp.database_id = db.database_id
    WHERE   rp.modification_time >= DATEADD(dd, -30, GETDATE())
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Corruption Detected' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Corruption Detected' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: Reads msdb.dbo.suspect_pages (page_error_type > 0) or sys.databases where state_desc = 'SUSPECT'. A database in SUSPECT state is inaccessible. Causes: I/O subsystem failure, hardware fault, abrupt shutdown. Recovery path: restore from backup (preferred), or DBCC CHECKDB WITH REPAIR_ALLOW_DATA_LOSS (last resort , data loss accepted).
FullStoryAction: This is only flagged if the server detects corruption in a database

Name: Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache
Description: A high percentage of plans in the procedure cache are single-use (used only once). This indicates excessive plan compilation overhead and memory waste, often caused by unparameterized ad-hoc queries or applications not using parameterized queries.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN
     EXISTS ( 
SELECT  35 AS CheckID ,
   100 AS Priority ,
   'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
   'Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache' AS Finding ,
   'https://www.brentozar.com/go/single' AS URL ,
   ( CAST(COUNT(*) AS VARCHAR(10))
     + ' query plans are taking up memory in the procedure cache. This may be wasted memory if we cache plans for queries that never get called again. This may be a good use case for SQL Server 2008''s Optimize for Ad Hoc or for Forced Parameterization.' ) AS Details
 FROM    sys.dm_exec_cached_plans AS cp
 WHERE   cp.usecounts = 1
   AND cp.objtype = 'Adhoc'
   AND EXISTS ( SELECT
         1
       FROM sys.configurations
       WHERE
         name = 'optimize for ad hoc workloads'
         AND value_in_use = 0 )
 HAVING  COUNT(*) > 1
) 
THEN 1
     ELSE 0
    END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Single-Use Plans in Procedure Cache
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_cached_plans where usecounts = 1 AND objtype = 'Adhoc'. A high count of single-use plans means the plan cache is full of non-reusable ad hoc SQL, evicting useful plans. Enable sp_configure 'optimize for ad hoc workloads' = 1 (stores plan stubs instead of full plans on first execution). Also investigate application parameterisation , ensure queries use sp_executesql or parameterised queries.
FullStoryAction: This may be a good use case for enabling the SQL Server 2008 memory optimization and parameterization features

Name: Slow Storage Reads on Drive
Description: Detects storage drives where average read latency exceeds 200 milliseconds for databases with significant read activity. High read latency directly degrades query performance and application response times. This check identifies drives that may need faster storage, load balancing, or investigation into whether the I/O subsystem is healthy.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(
    SELECT 1
    FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL, NULL) AS fs
    INNER JOIN sys.master_files AS mf
        ON fs.database_id = mf.database_id
        AND fs.[file_id] = mf.[file_id]
    WHERE (io_stall_read_ms / (1.0 + num_of_reads)) > 200
      AND num_of_reads > 100000
) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction:
Investigate the storage subsystem for the drive showing high read latency.
Consider moving database files to faster storage or redistributing I/O load across volumes.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no databases exceed 200ms average read latency. Returns FAIL when reads are slow.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Drive Reads are averaging longer than 200ms
NextAction: sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats , io_stall_read_ms / num_of_reads > threshold. Reads averaging more than 10ms indicate a storage read latency problem. Causes: overloaded SAN, insufficient read cache, or non-SSD storage for OLAP workloads. Identify affected files: SELECT DB_NAME(database_id), file_id, io_stall_read_ms/num_of_reads FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL,NULL) WHERE num_of_reads > 0 ORDER BY io_stall_read_ms/num_of_reads DESC.
FullStoryAction: When Read operations performed on a drive is averaging longer than 200ms it could point to serious performance issues on that SQL server.

Name: Slow Storage Writes on Drive
Description: SQL Server has detected slow write latency on one or more drives hosting database files. High write latency (typically >20ms) impacts transaction log writes, checkpoint performance, and overall SQL Server write throughput.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

 SELECT DISTINCT
   37 AS CheckID ,
   150 AS Priority ,
   'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
   'Slow Storage Writes on Drive '
   + UPPER(LEFT(mf.physical_name, 1)) AS Finding ,
   'https://www.brentozar.com/go/slow' AS URL ,
   'Writes are averaging longer than 100ms for at least one database on this drive.  For specific database file speeds, run the query from the information link.' AS Details
 FROM    sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL, NULL)
   AS fs
   INNER JOIN sys.master_files AS mf ON fs.database_id = mf.database_id
           AND fs.[file_id] = mf.[file_id]
 WHERE   ( io_stall_write_ms / ( 1.0
         + num_of_writes ) ) > 100
  AND num_of_writes > 100000
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Slow Storage Writes on Drive' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Slow Storage Writes on Drive' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Slow Storage reported on any Drives
NextAction: sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats , io_stall_write_ms / num_of_writes. Writes averaging > 10ms indicate storage subsystem saturation or misconfiguration. Check: (1) SAN/storage controller queue depth, (2) log drive shared with data files, (3) SQL write-ahead logging pattern vs storage write-back cache, (4) backup I/O competing with workload. Use sys.dm_io_pending_io_requests to catch active stalls.
FullStoryAction: Count of Disk drives with writes are averaging longer than 10ms

Name: Multiple TempDB Data Files Required
Description: Multiple TempDB Data Files Required - Performance (sp_Blitz CheckID 40)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

        SELECT   40 ,
            'tempdb' ,
            170 ,
            'File Configuration' ,
            'TempDB Only Has 1 Data File' ,
            'https://www.brentozar.com/go/tempdb' ,
            'TempDB is only configured with one data file.  More data files are usually required to alleviate SGAM contention.'
       WHERE (SELECT COUNT(*)
        FROM   tempdb.sys.database_files
        WHERE  type_desc = 'ROWS'
         ) = 1
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Multiple TempDB Data Files Required' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Multiple TempDB Data Files Required' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: STATS_DATE() across sys.stats shows statistics last updated beyond the staleness threshold. Stale statistics lead to incorrect cardinality estimates, causing the query optimiser to choose suboptimal join orders, serial plans where parallel would be better, and incorrect memory grants. Use sp_updatestats or Ola Hallengren's maintenance solution for statistics updates on a schedule.
FullStoryAction: More data files are usually required to alleviate SGAM contention, when present

Name: Multiple Log Files on One Drive
Description: One or more databases have multiple transaction log files on the same drive. SQL Server only uses one log file at a time (log files are sequential, not parallel), so multiple log files on the same drive provide no benefit and add management complexity.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT physical_name) AS cnt, [file_id], type_desc FROM sys.master_files WHERE [type] = 1 AND database_id > 4 GROUP BY [file_id], type_desc HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT physical_name) > 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Multiple Log Files on One Drive' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Multiple Log Files on One Drive' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Multiple Log Files on One Drive
NextAction: sys.master_files grouped by volume_mount_point , multiple log files on the same physical drive. Multiple log files for one database on the same drive give no throughput benefit (log writes are always sequential to a single active VLF). Multiple log files on the same drive just add management complexity. Drop secondary log files if they are empty: DBCC SHRINKFILE([log2], EMPTYFILE).
FullStoryAction: Database that have multiple log files on the same drive does not improve performance as log file access is sequential, not parallel.

Name: Uneven File Growth Settings in One Filegroup
Description: One or more filegroups contain data files with different auto-growth settings. Uneven file growth leads to unbalanced proportional fill, where SQL Server cannot evenly distribute I/O across files, resulting in one file becoming disproportionately larger.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT mf.file_id FROM sys.master_files mf INNER JOIN sys.filegroups fg ON mf.data_space_id = fg.data_space_id WHERE mf.database_id > 4 AND fg.type = 'FG' GROUP BY mf.file_id, mf.growth, fg.data_space_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT mf.growth) > 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Uneven File Growth Settings in One Filegroup' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Uneven File Growth Settings in One Filegroup' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Uneven File Growth Settings in One Filegroup
CheckClearSimplified: All File Growth Settings seems even
NextAction: sys.master_files grouped by volume_mount_point , data files in the same filegroup on the same drive have unequal autogrow settings. SQL's proportional fill algorithm distributes writes based on free space; unequal file sizes or growth settings cause I/O hotspots on the largest file. Pre-size files equally and set identical fixed-increment autogrow values.
FullStoryAction: This can lead to uneven file activity inside the filegroup.

Name: Queries Forcing Order Hints
Description: Queries in the plan cache are using FORCE ORDER hints, instructing the query optimizer to join tables in the order specified rather than choosing the optimal join order. This can lock in suboptimal execution plans as data distributions change.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
   SELECT  44 AS CheckID ,
  150 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Queries Forcing Order Hints' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/hints' AS URL ,
  CAST(occurrence AS VARCHAR(10))
  + ' instances of order hinting have been recorded since restart.  This means queries are bossing the SQL Server optimizer around, and if they don''t know what they''re doing, this can cause more harm than good.  This can also explain why DBA tuning efforts aren''t working.' AS Details
FROM    sys.dm_exec_query_optimizer_info
WHERE   counter = 'order hint'
  AND occurrence > 1000
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Queries Forcing Order Hints' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Queries Forcing Order Hints' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Queries Forcing specific sort order through hints
CheckClearSimplified: No Queries Forcing specific sort order through hints
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_query_stats: queries with join type forced (LOOP JOIN, HASH JOIN, MERGE JOIN hints). Forced join type hints prevent the optimizer from choosing a better join strategy as data volumes change. These are typically workarounds for parameter-sensitive plans or outdated statistics. Document which queries use join hints via sys.dm_exec_query_plan.
FullStoryAction: This might lead to unexpected performance impacts. Best to let SQL optimize on its own

Name: Queries Forcing Join Hints
Description: Queries in the plan cache are using join hints (LOOP, HASH, or MERGE), overriding the query optimizer's join selection. Hard-coded join hints can prevent the optimizer from adapting to data changes and may result in degraded performance over time.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
   SELECT  45 AS CheckID ,
    150 AS Priority ,
    'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Queries Forcing Join Hints' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/hints' AS URL ,
    CAST(occurrence AS VARCHAR(10))
    + ' instances of join hinting have been recorded since restart.  This means queries are bossing the SQL Server optimizer around, and if they don''t know what they''re doing, this can cause more harm than good.  This can also explain why DBA tuning efforts aren''t working.' AS Details
  FROM    sys.dm_exec_query_optimizer_info
  WHERE   counter = 'join hint'
    AND occurrence > 1000
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Queries Forcing Join Hints' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Queries Forcing Join Hints' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Queries are Forcing table join Hints
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_query_stats / sys.dm_exec_sql_text: queries with OPTION(FORCE ORDER) in plan XML. JOIN hints force the optimizer to use a specific join order regardless of statistics. A query that needed a join hint typically has bad statistics or a schema design issue. The hint may give good performance for one set of parameters but poor performance for others. Investigate the underlying statistics quality and index coverage.
FullStoryAction: Query hints are useful, but this might lead to performance issues

Name: Leftover Fake Indexes From Wizards
Description: Hypothetical indexes left behind by tuning wizards. Users feel this as slow page loads and timeout errors.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes WHERE name LIKE '%[_]dta[_]%' OR name LIKE '%[_]hyp[_]%') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Tune the query or configuration related to Leftover Fake Indexes From Wizards. Check execution plan and wait statistics.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Leftover Fake Indexes From Wizards' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Bad indexing burns CPU and IO you already paid for. Users experience this as slow page loads and abandoned sessions.
NextAction: sys.indexes where is_hypothetical = 1. Hypothetical indexes are leftover artifacts from the Database Engine Tuning Advisor (DTA). They consume space in sys.indexes and sys.stats, add overhead to statistics maintenance, and may confuse query plan analysis tools. Drop them: SELECT 'DROP INDEX ' + name + ' ON ' + OBJECT_NAME(object_id) FROM sys.indexes WHERE is_hypothetical = 1.
FullStoryAction: During Index Tuning Wizard or Database Tuning Advisor exercises a leftover hypothetical index can be left behind on the server.  These indexes are not actually helping performance and should be removed when present.

Name: Indexes Disabled
Description: One or more indexes have been disabled. Disabled indexes cannot be used by the query optimizer and their data is not maintained during DML operations. The index definition remains but the optimizer cannot use it until it is rebuilt.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes WHERE is_disabled = 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Indexes Disabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Indexes Disabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_exec_query_stats or Query Store top consumers by CPU, logical reads, or duration. These queries are the highest-impact targets for performance tuning. Each should be reviewed for missing indexes (sys.dm_db_missing_index_details), implicit conversions (sys.dm_exec_query_plan), and parameter sensitivity.
FullStoryAction: Disabled index are not actually helping performance and should either be enabled or removed.

Name: Foreign Keys Not Trusted
Description: One or more foreign key constraints are marked as NOT TRUSTED (WITH NOCHECK). The query optimizer cannot use untrusted constraints to simplify query plans, leading to more complex execution plans and missed optimization opportunities.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.foreign_keys WHERE is_not_trusted = 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Foreign Keys Not Trusted' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Foreign Keys Not Trusted' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.foreign_keys where is_not_trusted = 1. Untrusted foreign keys (created WITH NOCHECK or previously disabled) are not enforced for existing rows. The optimizer cannot use FK metadata to eliminate unnecessary join steps. Re-enable: ALTER TABLE [child] WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT [fk_name]. This validates all existing rows. A failed validation means orphaned rows exist , clean them before re-enabling.
FullStoryAction: This impacts SQL ability to optimize queries. It can cause some really bad performance impacts.

Name: Linked Server Configured
Description: One or more linked servers are configured on this instance. Linked servers enable distributed queries but introduce security risks, performance overhead, and dependency on external servers. Failures on the remote server can impact local queries.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
   SELECT DISTINCT
     49 AS CheckID ,
     200 AS Priority ,
     'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
     'Linked Server Configured' AS Finding ,
     'https://www.brentozar.com/go/link' AS URL ,
     +CASE WHEN l.remote_name = 'sa'
        THEN COALESCE(s.data_source, s.name, s.provider)
          + ' is configured as a linked server. Check its security configuration as it is connecting with sa, because any user who queries it will get admin-level permissions.'
        ELSE COALESCE(s.data_source, s.name, s.provider)
          + ' is configured as a linked server. Check its security configuration to make sure it isn''t connecting with SA or some other bone-headed administrative login, because any user who queries it might get admin-level permissions.'
      END AS Details
   FROM    sys.servers s
     INNER JOIN sys.linked_logins l ON s.server_id = l.server_id
   WHERE   s.is_linked = 1
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Linked Server Configured' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Linked Server Configured' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.servers where is_linked = 1. Each linked server uses an OLE DB connection and a security context (specific login or pass-through). Linked server queries are not optimised across boundaries , they pull full tables or execute remote procedures. Audit for unused linked servers and remove them.
FullStoryAction: Linked servers are a great way to interact with remote data. They can cause security problems if incorrectly configured

Name: Max Memory Set Too High
Description: Max Memory Set Too High - Performance (sp_Blitz CheckID 50)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  50 AS CheckID ,
    100 AS Priority ,
    'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Max Memory Set Too High' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/max' AS URL ,
    'SQL Server max memory is set to '
   + CAST(c.value_in_use AS VARCHAR(20))
   + ' megabytes, but the server only has '
   + CAST(( CAST(m.total_physical_memory_kb AS BIGINT) / 1024 ) AS VARCHAR(20))
   + ' megabytes.  SQL Server may drain the system dry of memory, and under certain conditions, this can cause Windows to swap to disk.' AS Details
    FROM    sys.dm_os_sys_memory m
    INNER JOIN sys.configurations c ON c.name = 'max server memory (MB)'
    WHERE   CAST(m.total_physical_memory_kb AS BIGINT) < ( CAST(c.value_in_use AS BIGINT) * 1024 )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Max Memory Set Too High' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Max Memory Set Too High' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Max Memory Set Too High on @ server(s)
NextAction: sp_configure 'max server memory' set to a value that consumes nearly all physical RAM. When SQL takes too much memory, Windows memory pressure triggers the memory notification object, causing SQL to voluntarily release buffer pool pages. This creates a feedback loop of cache thrashing. Formula: max_server_memory = TotalRAM_MB - (OS_reservation_MB + other_services_MB). Minimum OS reservation: 1 GB + (1 GB per 4 GB RAM above 16 GB).
FullStoryAction: SQL Server may drain the system dry of memory, and under certain conditions, this can cause Windows to swap to disk

Name: Max Memory Set Too Low
Description: Max Memory Set Too Low - Performance (sp_Blitz CheckID 51)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
    SELECT  51 AS CheckID ,
    1 AS Priority ,
    'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Memory Dangerously Low' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/max' AS URL ,
    'The server has ' + CAST(( CAST(m.total_physical_memory_kb AS BIGINT) / 1024 ) AS VARCHAR(20)) + ' megabytes of physical memory, but only ' + CAST(( CAST(m.available_physical_memory_kb AS BIGINT) / 1024 ) AS VARCHAR(20))
   + ' megabytes are available.  As the server runs out of memory, there is danger of swapping to disk, which will kill performance.' AS Details
    FROM    sys.dm_os_sys_memory m
    WHERE   CAST(m.available_physical_memory_kb AS BIGINT) < 262144
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Max Memory Set Too Low' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Max Memory Set Too Low' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_sys_info physical_memory_in_use_kb and max_server_memory_mb from sp_configure. If max_server_memory is below a calculated safe minimum (RAM minus OS reservation), SQL may not have enough buffer pool for the working set. Set max server memory = physical_memory_kb * 0.85 (leave ~15% for OS). Monitor via sys.dm_os_memory_clerks and DBCC MEMORYSTATUS.
FullStoryAction: If the server is throwing memory warnings this would be a likely performance constraint.

Name: Interesting Database Owner
Description: Database owner is a login with elevated privileges. Auditors flag this in the first 5 minutes of a review.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT d.name, SUSER_SNAME(d.owner_sid) AS owner FROM sys.databases d WHERE d.name NOT IN('master','model','msdb','tempdb') AND SUSER_SNAME(d.owner_sid) <> SUSER_SNAME(0x01) AND SUSER_SNAME(d.owner_sid) IS NOT NULL AND d.state = 0 AND d.is_in_standby = 0 AND d.source_database_id IS NULL) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Remediate the security finding for Interesting Database Owner. Review the technical_details section for exact T-SQL.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Interesting Database Owner' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Security gaps produce audit findings that delay compliance certification and require board-level disclosure. Each one has a dollar cost in remediation hours.

Name: Untrusted Check Constraints
Description: One or more check constraints are marked NOT TRUSTED (WITH NOCHECK). Untrusted check constraints are not enforced for existing data and the optimizer cannot use them to eliminate redundant checks, potentially leading to less efficient query plans.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.check_constraints WHERE is_not_trusted = 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check Constraint Not Trusted'' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check Constraint Not Trusted'' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: SQL Agent Job Runs at Startup
Description: SQL Agent job configured to run at SQL Server startup. Auditors flag this in the first 5 minutes of a review.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  57 AS CheckID ,
  230 AS Priority ,
  'Security' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'SQL Agent Job Runs at Startup' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/startup' AS URL ,
  ( 'Job [' + j.name
    + '] runs automatically when SQL Server Agent starts up.  Make sure you know exactly what this job is doing, because it could pose a security risk.' ) AS Details
FROM    msdb.dbo.sysschedules sched
  JOIN msdb.dbo.sysjobschedules jsched ON sched.schedule_id = jsched.schedule_id
  JOIN msdb.dbo.sysjobs j ON jsched.job_id = j.job_id
WHERE   sched.freq_type = 64
        AND sched.enabled = 1 
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Remediate the security finding for SQL Agent Job Runs at Startup. Review the technical_details section for exact T-SQL.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'SQL Agent Job Runs at Startup' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Security gaps produce audit findings that delay compliance certification and require board-level disclosure. Each one has a dollar cost in remediation hours.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
CheckClearSimplified: No SQL Agent Job Runs at SQL Start-up
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysjobs where enabled = 1 and start_step_id = 1 with schedule_id matching 'startup'. SQL Agent jobs configured to run at SQL Server startup. These run before any login-based access and with Agent's service account permissions. Review all startup jobs for legitimacy and document their purpose.
FullStoryAction: Make sure you know exactly what this job is doing, because it could pose a security risk.

Name: Alerts Configured without Follow Up
Description: SQL Server alerts are configured but have no operator notification or job response set up. Alerts without follow-up actions provide no value since nobody is notified when the alert condition is met.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  59 AS CheckID ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Alerts Configured without Follow Up' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/alert' AS URL ,
  ( 'SQL Server Agent alerts have been configured but they either do not notify anyone or else they do not take any action.  This is a free, easy way to get notified of corruption, job failures, or major outages even before monitoring systems pick it up.' ) AS Details

  WHERE EXISTS (SELECT  * FROM    msdb.dbo.sysalerts
    WHERE   enabled = 1
    AND COALESCE(has_notification, 0) = 0
    AND (job_id IS NULL OR job_id = 0x)) 
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Alerts Configured without Follow Up' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Alerts Configured without Follow Up' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Alerts do not have Follow Up Configured
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysalerts where has_notification = 0 AND message_id IS NOT NULL. An alert is configured but has no notification operator attached , it triggers silently with no action. Add an operator notification to every configured alert: EXEC msdb.dbo.sp_add_notification @alert_name=N'...', @operator_name=N'DBA Team', @notification_method=1.
FullStoryAction: Alerts are configured but they either do not notify anyone or else they do not take any action

Name: Fill Factor Changed
Description:
Suboptimal 'Fill Factor Changed' degrades query throughput and increases resource contention.
Users experience increased latency and potential timeouts during peak workloads.
Capacity planning becomes unreliable when this condition persists unchecked.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes WHERE fill_factor <> 0 AND fill_factor <> 100) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Fill Factor Changed' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Fill Factor Changed' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No indexes have Fill Factors Changed
NextAction: sys.indexes where fill_factor != 0 AND fill_factor != 80 (or similar threshold). A non-default fill factor affects every index page , too low wastes space and increases I/O; too high (100) causes frequent page splits and fragmentation. Confirm fill factor is intentionally set based on the insert pattern of each table. Document the rationale.
FullStoryAction: This can cause memory and storage performance problems but may also prevent page splits.

Name: Old Compatibility Level
Description: One or more databases are running at an older compatibility level than the SQL Server version. Older compatibility levels prevent the use of newer query optimizer improvements and features introduced in later SQL Server versions.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  62 AS CheckID ,
  [name] AS DatabaseName ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Old Compatibility Level' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/compatlevel' AS URL ,
  ( 'Database ' + [name]
    + ' is compatibility level '
    + CAST(compatibility_level AS VARCHAR(20))
    + ', which may cause unwanted results when trying to run queries that have newer T-SQL features.' ) AS Details
FROM    sys.databases
WHERE   compatibility_level <= 90
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Old Compatibility Level' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Old Compatibility Level' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No databases have old Compatibility Levels
NextAction: sys.databases where compatibility_level is more than one version behind the instance. Very old compatibility levels (e.g. 80, 90) prevent access to critical performance features. sp_Blitz flags databases still on SQL 2000 or 2005 compat mode. These databases cannot use parallel DML, filtered indexes, or modern join optimisations.
FullStoryAction: These databases cannot use optimizations on the current SQL server

Name: SQL Server Agent Jobs Starting Automatically
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 67' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE database_id > 4 AND owner_sid != 0x01) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 67' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 67' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: Reads Windows power scheme via registry or WMI. Balanced/Power Saver plans use P-states (frequency scaling) and C-states (CPU sleep), capping SQL query throughput and adding microsecond latency to CPU wake-up cycles. Set the High Performance plan: powercfg /setactive 8c5e7fda-e8bf-4a96-9a85-a6e23a8c635c. Also check the hypervisor power policy on virtual machines , the host must also use High Performance.
FullStoryAction: When multiple SQL Server Agent jobs are configured to start simultaneously it can have unexpected impacts on server resources like CPU and disk I/O

Name: DBCC CHECKDB Not Performed Recently
Description: DBCC CHECKDB Not Performed Recently - Reliability (sp_Blitz CheckID 68)
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT d.name FROM sys.databases d LEFT JOIN msdb.dbo.restorehistory rh ON d.name = rh.destination_database_name AND rh.restore_type = 'D' WHERE d.state = 0 AND d.database_id > 4 AND d.name <> 'tempdb' AND d.source_database_id IS NULL AND ISNULL(DATABASEPROPERTYEX(d.name, 'Updateability'), '') = 'READ_WRITE' AND DATEDIFF(dd, ISNULL(rh.restore_date, '1900-01-01'), GETDATE()) > 7) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'DBCC CHECKDB Not Performed Recently' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'DBCC CHECKDB Not Performed Recently' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: High VLF Count
Description: High VLF Count - Reliability (sp_Blitz CheckID 69)
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT d.name, CAST(COUNT(*) AS VARCHAR(20)) AS VLFCount FROM sys.databases d CROSS APPLY sys.dm_db_log_info(DB_ID(d.name)) li WHERE d.state = 0 AND d.name NOT IN('master','model','msdb','tempdb') AND d.source_database_id IS NULL GROUP BY d.name HAVING COUNT(*) > 999) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'High VLF Count' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'High VLF Count' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ databases with High internal VLF Counts
CheckClearSimplified: All databases have Low internal VLF Counts
NextAction: DBCC LOGINFO() , counts VLF (Virtual Log File) entries. Databases with hundreds or thousands of VLFs experience: slow database startup recovery (SQL must process each VLF), slow log backups, and high log I/O overhead. VLF proliferation is caused by excessive log file autogrow events (especially 1 MB growth increments). To fix: take a full backup, shrink the log (DBCC SHRINKFILE on the log), then grow it in one large increment.
FullStoryAction: This may be slowing down start-up, restores, and even inserts/updates/deletes

Name: @@SERVERNAME Not Set
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 70' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN
     EXISTS ( 
SELECT  70 AS CheckID ,
200 AS Priority ,
'Informational' AS FindingsGroup ,
'@@Servername Not Set' AS Finding ,
'https://www.brentozar.com/go/servername' AS URL ,
