'@@Servername variable is null. You can fix it by executing: "sp_addserver ''<LocalServerName>'', local"' AS Details
) AND @@SERVERNAME IS NULL
THEN 1
     ELSE 0
    END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 70' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 70' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.service_queues where is_poison_message_handling_on = 1. Poison message handling in Service Broker automatically disables a queue after 5 consecutive transaction rollbacks. If a queue is disabled, message processing stops silently. Monitor sys.service_queues for is_receive_enabled = 0. Fix the failing message handler before re-enabling the queue.
FullStoryAction: Where the @@Servername is different from the computer name, it may trigger certificate errors

Name: The partitioned database may have non-aligned indexes
Description: One or more partitioned databases may contain non-aligned indexes where the index partitioning does not match the table partitioning. Non-aligned indexes prevent partition elimination and can significantly reduce the performance benefits of partitioning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes i INNER JOIN sys.partition_schemes ps ON i.data_space_id = ps.data_space_id WHERE i.data_space_id <> 0 AND ps.data_space_id IS NOT NULL AND OBJECTPROPERTY(i.object_id, 'IsUserTable') = 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'The partitioned database may have non-aligned indexes' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'The partitioned database may have non-aligned indexes' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.partitions or sys.indexes joined to sys.partition_schemes , non-aligned indexes detected on a partitioned table. Non-aligned indexes cannot be maintained per-partition (partition switching is blocked). Rebuilding non-aligned indexes requires the entire table to be locked. Align all indexes to the partition scheme to enable partition-level operations.
FullStoryAction: Having non-aligned indexes on partitioned tables may cause inefficient query plans and CPU pressure

Name: Default Trace Not Enabled
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 73' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.sql_logins WHERE name = 'sa' AND is_disabled = 0) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 73' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 73' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.

Name: Stored Procedure WITH RECOMPILE
Description: One or more stored procedures are created or called WITH RECOMPILE, forcing the procedure to be recompiled on every execution. While sometimes necessary, excessive recompilation consumes CPU and can be a significant performance drain under high concurrency.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.procedures WHERE create_date IS NOT NULL AND name LIKE '%RECOMPILE%') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Stored Procedure WITH RECOMPILE' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Stored Procedure WITH RECOMPILE' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.sql_modules where definition LIKE '%WITH RECOMPILE%'. Procedures with WITH RECOMPILE recompile on every execution, adding CPU overhead. Acceptable for procedures with highly variable parameters but inappropriate for stable, high-frequency procedures. Replace WITH RECOMPILE with OPTION(OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN) or OPTION(RECOMPILE) at individual statement level where needed.
FullStoryAction: This may cause increased CPU usage due to constant recompiles of the code.

Name: Max File Size Set
Description: One or more database files have a maximum file size configured. If a database file reaches its maximum size and cannot grow, the database will go into an error state, causing application failures and potential data loss.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.master_files WHERE max_size > 0 AND max_size <= growth AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Max File Size Set' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Max File Size Set' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Max File Size Set for @ database files
CheckClearSimplified: No database files have Max File Size Set
NextAction: sys.master_files where max_size > 0 (max file size configured). A maximum file size cap silently stops the database from growing when it reaches the limit , causing 'Could not allocate space' errors that immediately impact the application. Remove max size restrictions: ALTER DATABASE [db] MODIFY FILE (NAME='...', MAXSIZE=UNLIMITED).
FullStoryAction: If this space runs out, the database will stop working even though there may be drive space available.

Name: File growth set to percent
Description: One or more database files are configured with percentage-based auto-growth. Percentage growth causes increasingly large growth events as the file gets bigger (e.g., 10% of a 1TB database = 100GB growth), causing prolonged I/O stalls.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.master_files WHERE growth > 0 AND is_percent_growth = 1 AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'File growth set to percent' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'File growth set to percent' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Database File growth set to percent for @ databases
CheckClearSimplified: No databases have Database File growth set to percent
NextAction: sys.master_files where growth > 0 and is_percent_growth = 1. Percent-based autogrow events double or grow files proportionally , a 1 GB file with 10% growth adds 100 MB, but a 100 GB file adds 10 GB. Large autogrow events cause long I/O stalls (if IFI is not enabled for data files) and VLF fragmentation in log files. Change all files to fixed-size autogrow: ALTER DATABASE [db] MODIFY FILE (NAME='...', FILEGROWTH=256MB).
FullStoryAction: This can lead to slow performance during growths

Name: Remote Admin Connections Enabled
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 83' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE is_auto_create_stats_on = 0 AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 83' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 83' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'remote admin connections' = 1. Allows the Dedicated Admin Connection (DAC) to be used from remote connections (not just localhost). Default 0 (localhost only). Enabling this is useful for remote DBA access when the server is under extreme load. Ensure it is restricted to trusted IP ranges at the firewall level.
FullStoryAction: remote admin connections. Used by DBAs to execute diagnostic functions even when the server is locked or running in an abnormal state and not responding to a SQL Server Database Engine connection.

Name: Database Mail Not Enabled
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 84' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE is_auto_update_stats_on = 0 AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 84' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 84' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ server(s) are running bad default settings for Database Mail XPs
CheckClearSimplified: Configuration changed from default for Database Mail
NextAction: sp_configure 'Database Mail XPs' = 1. Enables sp_send_dbmail and the Database Mail subsystem. Required for SQL Agent to email operator alerts. Confirm a mail profile is configured (msdb.dbo.sysmail_profile) and test with sp_send_dbmail. Without working mail, all operator alert notifications are silently lost.
FullStoryAction: Database Mail XPs. Used to enable Database Mail on this server.

Name: Elevated Permissions on a Database
Description: One or more users have elevated database roles such as db_owner or db_securityadmin in user databases. These broad permissions violate the principle of least privilege and can create security vulnerabilities.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT dbp.name FROM sys.database_principals dbp INNER JOIN sys.database_permissions p ON dbp.principal_id = p.grantee_principal_id WHERE p.class = 0 AND p.state_desc = 'GRANT' AND dbp.name NOT IN ('dbo','guest','INFORMATION_SCHEMA','sys') AND dbp.type NOT IN ('A','R') AND dbp.is_fixed_role = 0) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Elevated Permissions on a Database' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Elevated Permissions on a Database' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Users have Elevated Permissions on a Database
CheckClearSimplified: No Users have Elevated Permissions on a Database
NextAction: sys.database_permissions or sys.database_principals where elevated permissions (db_owner, CONTROL DATABASE, IMPERSONATE) are granted to accounts that should not require them. Elevated database permissions can be used for data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and bypassing row-level controls. Audit with: SELECT * FROM sys.database_principals dp JOIN sys.database_role_members drm ON dp.principal_id = drm.member_principal_id WHERE drm.role_principal_id = USER_ID('db_owner').
FullStoryAction: These users can perform tasks beyond just reading and writing data.

Name: Availability Groups has automatically repaired corruption
Description: This check identifies availability groups has automatically repaired corruption. Availability Groups has automatically repaired at least one corrupt page in the last 30 days. For more information, query the DMV sys.dm_hadr_auto_page_repair
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT DISTINCT
    89 AS CheckID ,
    db.name ,
    1 AS Priority ,
    'Corruption' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Database Corruption Detected' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/repair' AS URL ,
    ( 'Availability Groups has automatically repaired at least one corrupt page in the last 30 days. For more information, query the DMV sys.dm_hadr_auto_page_repair.' ) AS Details
    FROM    sys.dm_hadr_auto_page_repair rp
    INNER JOIN master.sys.databases db ON rp.database_id = db.database_id
    WHERE   rp.modification_time >= DATEADD(dd, -30, GETDATE())
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Availability Groups has automatically repaired corruption' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Availability Groups has automatically repaired corruption' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Database Corruption Detected - Automatically repaired AG
CheckClearSimplified: No Database Corruption Detected in Availability Groups
NextAction: sys.dm_hadr_availability_replica_states where is_local = 0 and connected_state = 0 or synchronization_health < 2 , an AG replica triggered automatic page repair. While automatic page repair is a benefit of AG, repeated repairs indicate underlying storage hardware issues. Check Windows System event log for storage error events. Replace suspect hardware.
FullStoryAction: Availability Groups allows for automatically repairing corrupt page. When this occurs the server records these events.

Name: Database Corruption Detected
Description: Additional database corruption evidence has been detected, typically from DBCC output stored in the default trace or error log. This check complements BLITZ_034 and may indicate corruption in different databases or objects.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT DISTINCT
    90 AS CheckID ,
    db.name ,
    1 AS Priority ,
    'Corruption' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Database Corruption Detected' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/repair' AS URL ,
    ( 'SQL Server has detected at least one corrupt page in the last 30 days. For more information, query the system table msdb.dbo.suspect_pages.' ) AS Details
    FROM    msdb.dbo.suspect_pages sp
    INNER JOIN master.sys.databases db ON sp.database_id = db.database_id
    WHERE   sp.last_update_date >= DATEADD(dd, -30, GETDATE())
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Corruption Detected' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Corruption Detected' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: Reads msdb.dbo.suspect_pages (page_error_type > 0) or sys.databases where state_desc = 'SUSPECT'. A database in SUSPECT state is inaccessible. Causes: I/O subsystem failure, hardware fault, abrupt shutdown. Recovery path: restore from backup (preferred), or DBCC CHECKDB WITH REPAIR_ALLOW_DATA_LOSS (last resort , data loss accepted).
FullStoryAction: This is only flagged if the server detects corruption in a database

Name: Backing Up to Same Drive Where Databases Reside
Description: Database backups are being written to the same physical drive where the database files reside. If that drive fails, both the database and its backups will be lost simultaneously, making recovery impossible.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT
 93 AS CheckID ,
 1 AS Priority ,
 'Backup' AS FindingsGroup ,
 'Backing Up to Same Drive Where Databases Reside' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/backup' AS URL ,
 CAST(COUNT(1) AS VARCHAR(50)) + ' backups done on drive '
 + UPPER(LEFT(bmf.physical_device_name, 3))
 + ' in the last two weeks, where database files also live. This represents a serious risk if that array fails.' Details
FROM msdb.dbo.backupmediafamily AS bmf
 INNER JOIN msdb.dbo.backupset AS bs ON bmf.media_set_id = bs.media_set_id
   AND bs.backup_start_date >= ( DATEADD(dd,
   -14, GETDATE()) )
 /* Filter out databases that were recently restored: */
 LEFT OUTER JOIN msdb.dbo.restorehistory rh ON bs.database_name = rh.destination_database_name AND rh.restore_date > DATEADD(dd, -14, GETDATE())
WHERE UPPER(LEFT(bmf.physical_device_name, 3)) <> 'HTT' AND
  bmf.physical_device_name NOT LIKE '\\%' AND -- GitHub Issue #2141
  -- @IsWindowsOperatingSystem = 1 AND -- GitHub Issue #1995
  UPPER(LEFT(bmf.physical_device_name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS, 3)) IN (
 SELECT DISTINCT
 UPPER(LEFT(mf.physical_name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS, 3))
 FROM sys.master_files AS mf
  WHERE mf.database_id <> 2 )
 AND rh.destination_database_name IS NULL
GROUP BY UPPER(LEFT(bmf.physical_device_name, 3))
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Backing Up to Same Drive Where Databases Reside' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Backing Up to Same Drive Where Databases Reside' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ backups done to a disk hosting databases
CheckClearSimplified: No backups done to a disk hosting databases
NextAction: sys.master_files where physical_name LIKE '\%' (UNC path) or LIKE '\%' (network share). Database files on network shares (NFS, SMB) add network latency to every I/O operation and lose write-ahead durability guarantees if the share is disconnected. Local SAN-attached or DAS storage is strongly preferred. Network file storage is only acceptable with SMB 3.0 Multichannel or certified Azure Files configurations.
FullStoryAction: Backing up to a drive where databases reside becomes a problem when a failure impacts that drive.

Name: Agent Jobs Without Failure Emails
Description: One or more SQL Server Agent jobs are configured without failure notification emails. Without failure alerts, job failures may go unnoticed, leading to undetected data issues, missed maintenance, or broken ETL pipelines.
SqlQuery:
;WITH las_job_run AS (
           SELECT  MAX(instance_id) AS instance_id, 
             job_id, COUNT_BIG(*) AS job_executions,
             SUM(CASE WHEN run_status = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS failed_executions
           FROM msdb.dbo.sysjobhistory
           WHERE step_id = 0
           GROUP BY job_id
           )
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  94 AS CheckID ,
  200 AS [Priority] ,
  'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Agent Jobs Without Failure Emails' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/alerts' AS URL ,
  'The job ' + [name]
  + ' has not been set up to notify an operator if it fails.' 
  + CASE 
   WHEN jh.run_date IS NULL OR jh.run_time IS NULL OR jh.run_status IS NULL THEN ''
   ELSE N' Executions: '+ CAST(ljr.job_executions AS VARCHAR(30)) 
    + CASE ljr.failed_executions
     WHEN 0 THEN N''
     ELSE N' ('+CAST(ljr.failed_executions AS NVARCHAR(10)) + N' failed)'
     END
    + N' - last execution started on '
    + CAST(CONVERT(DATE,CAST(jh.run_date AS NVARCHAR(8)),113) AS NVARCHAR(10)) 
    + N', at ' 
    + STUFF(STUFF(RIGHT(N'000000' + CAST(jh.run_time AS varchar(6)),6),3,0,N':'),6,0,N':')
    + N', with status "'
    + CASE jh.run_status 
      WHEN 0 THEN N'Failed'
      WHEN 1 THEN N'Succeeded'
      WHEN 2 THEN N'Retry'
      WHEN 3 THEN N'Canceled'
      WHEN 4 THEN N'In Progress'
     END +N'".'
   END AS Details
FROM    msdb.[dbo].[sysjobs] j
  LEFT JOIN las_job_run ljr 
   ON ljr.job_id = j.job_id
  LEFT JOIN msdb.[dbo].[sysjobhistory] jh
   ON ljr.job_id = jh.job_id AND ljr.instance_id = jh.instance_id
WHERE   j.enabled = 1
  AND j.notify_email_operator_id = 0
  AND j.notify_netsend_operator_id = 0
  AND j.notify_page_operator_id = 0
          AND j.category_id <> 100
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Agent Jobs Without Failure Emails' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Agent Jobs Without Failure Emails' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Agent Jobs Without Failure Emails configured
CheckClearSimplified: All Agent Jobs have Failure Emails configured
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysjobs , jobs with no failure notification operator configured. When a SQL Agent job fails silently, the DBA may not discover it until the impact is noticed (e.g. missing backup, stale index). Add an operator (msdb.dbo.sp_add_operator) and configure failure notifications on all critical jobs (backups, DBCC, index maintenance).
FullStoryAction: No email will be sent if it fails.

Name: Plan Guides Enabled
Description: One or more plan guides exist on this instance. Plan guides force specific query execution plans for targeted queries. While useful for fixing specific plan regressions, they add complexity and can mask underlying query or index issues.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.plan_guides) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Plan Guides Enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Plan Guides Enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_ring_buffers (SystemHealth) records per-minute CPU usage by SQL process. Sustained high CPU means queries are competing for compute cycles. Identify the top CPU consumers via sys.dm_exec_query_stats order by total_worker_time. Common causes: missing indexes, implicit conversions, poor cardinality estimates causing serial hash joins, or insufficient server hardware.
FullStoryAction: If there are issues getting query performance to improve, it might be due to a frozen plan

Name: No Alerts for Corruption
Description: SQL Server alerts for corruption-related errors (823, 824, 825) are not configured. Without these alerts, corruption events will not trigger notifications to DBAs, potentially allowing undetected data integrity issues to go unresolved.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  96 AS CheckID ,
200 AS Priority ,
'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
'No Alerts for Corruption' AS Finding ,
'https://www.brentozar.com/go/alert' AS URL ,
( 'SQL Server Agent alerts do not exist for errors 823, 824, and 825.  These three errors can give you notification about early hardware failure. Enabling them can prevent you a lot of heartbreak.' ) AS Details
  WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT  *
  FROM    msdb.dbo.sysalerts
  WHERE   message_id IN ( 823, 824, 825 )) 
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'No Alerts for Corruption' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'No Alerts for Corruption' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Configure corruption alerts. Corruption leads to data loss. Immediate notification of corruption is required. Take urgent action.
CheckClearSimplified: Alerts for Corruption Enabled on all servers
NextAction: msdb.dbo.sysalerts , checks whether alert 832 (Corrupt page) or 833 (I/O taking > 15 seconds) is configured. No corruption alerts means data integrity failures go undetected until they surface as application errors. Configure alerts for severity 19â€“25 and error numbers 823, 824, 825 (I/O errors) and 832 (page corruption). Use Database Mail + operator to email the DBA team.
FullStoryAction: These three errors can give you notification about early hardware failure

Name: Unusual SQL Server Edition
Description: SQL Server is running an unusual or unexpected edition (such as Express, Web, or Developer). These editions have feature and resource limitations that may not be appropriate for production workloads, including memory caps and missing enterprise features.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  97 AS CheckID ,
  100 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Unusual SQL Server Edition' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/workgroup' AS URL ,
  ( 'This server is using '
    + CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100))
    + ', which is capped at low amounts of CPU and memory.' ) AS Details
WHERE   CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%Standard%'
  AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%Enterprise%'
  AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%Data Center%'
  AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%Developer%'
  AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%Business Intelligence%'
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Unusual SQL Server Edition' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Unusual SQL Server Edition' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ server(s) have Unusual SQL Server Editions
NextAction: sys.dm_os_schedulers runnable_tasks_count and work_queue_count. A runnable_tasks_count consistently > 0 means threads are queued waiting for a scheduler slot (THREADPOOL waits). Increase max worker threads (sp_configure) or investigate why individual queries hold threads for extended durations.
FullStoryAction: Some other SQL Editions, like Web and Workgroup, are capped at low amounts of CPU and memory

Name: Alerts Disabled
Description: One or more SQL Server Agent alerts are disabled. Disabled alerts will not fire when their trigger conditions are met, potentially leaving critical errors or performance events unnoticed by the DBA team.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  98 AS CheckID ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Monitoring' AS FindingsGroup ,
  'Alerts Disabled' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/alert' AS URL ,
  ( 'The following Alert is disabled, please review and enable if desired: '
    + name ) AS Details
FROM    msdb.dbo.sysalerts
WHERE   enabled = 0
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Alerts Disabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Alerts Disabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Automatic alert notifications are off. No alerts will be sent to support teams. Take action.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_sys_info cpu_count. Informational baseline. Compare against licensed core count; SQL Server Standard Edition caps at 24 cores or 4 sockets (whichever is lower). Verify CPU topology with sys.dm_os_nodes to ensure NUMA-aware scheduling is active.
FullStoryAction: If alerting is disabled, please review and enable

Name: Infinite merge replication metadata retention period
Description: Merge replication is configured with an infinite metadata retention period. This causes the MSmerge_genhistory table in the publication database to grow without bounds, potentially consuming large amounts of disk space over time.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE is_published = 1 OR is_subscribed = 1 OR is_merge_published = 1) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Infinite merge replication metadata retention period' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Infinite merge replication metadata retention period' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats aggregate read_ios + write_ios per second. Sustained IOPS near the storage subsystem limit causes PAGEIOLATCH_SH/EX and WRITELOG waits. Identify the hottest files via io_stall_read_ms / num_of_reads and io_stall_write_ms / num_of_writes. Consider filegroup separation, SSD tiering, or read-scale AG replicas to offload read I/O.
FullStoryAction: Databases can have merge replication metadata retention period set to infinite - which can be the source of significant performance issues.

Name: Remote DAC Disabled
Description: The Remote Dedicated Administrator Connection (DAC) is disabled. The DAC allows administrators to connect to SQL Server when it is unresponsive to normal connections. Without remote DAC, a DBA must have physical or RDP access to resolve certain emergencies.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  100 AS CheckID ,
 170 AS Priority ,
 'Reliability' AS FindingGroup ,
 'Remote DAC Disabled' AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/dac' AS URL ,
 'Remote access to the Dedicated Admin Connection (DAC) is not enabled. The DAC can make remote troubleshooting much easier when SQL Server is unresponsive.'
 WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT  1
 FROM    sys.configurations
 WHERE   name = 'remote admin connections'
 AND value_in_use = 0 )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Remote DAC Disabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Remote DAC Disabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Remote Admin connections are enabled on all server(s)
CheckClearSimplified: Remote Admin connections are enabled on all servers
NextAction: Aggregates backup recency, DBCC CHECKDB last run date, and index fragmentation state. A failure here means the server is operating without fundamental DBA oversight. No backups + no integrity checks = undetected data corruption and no recovery path.
FullStoryAction: The Dedicated Admin Connection (DAC) allows Admins to connect to the SQL server during certain problem scenarios

Name: CPU Schedulers Offline
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 101' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  101 AS CheckID ,
   50 AS Priority ,
   'Performance' AS FindingGroup ,
   'CPU Schedulers Offline' AS Finding ,
   'https://www.brentozar.com/go/schedulers' AS URL ,
   'Some CPU cores are not accessible to SQL Server due to affinity masking or licensing problems.'
   WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT  *
FROM    sys.dm_os_schedulers
WHERE   is_online = 0 )
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 101' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 101' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ server(s) have CPUs that are Offline
NextAction: Cumulative MB read + written since last SQL restart, extrapolated to daily rate. High daily I/O volume is a leading indicator of future storage capacity issues. Compare against storage rated sequential throughput (MB/s). Also check for write amplification from excessive index fragmentation or log flush patterns.
FullStoryAction: Some CPU cores can become inaccessible to SQL Server due to affinity masking or licensing problems

Name: Unusual Database State
Description: One or more databases are in an unusual state (not ONLINE), such as SUSPECT, RECOVERY_PENDING, RESTORING, or OFFLINE. Non-online databases are inaccessible to applications and indicate a potential problem requiring immediate investigation.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  102 AS CheckID ,
  [name] ,
  20 AS Priority ,
  'Reliability' AS FindingGroup ,
  'Unusual Database State: ' + [state_desc] AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/repair' AS URL ,
  'This database may not be online.'
FROM    sys.databases
WHERE   state > 1
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Unusual Database State' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Unusual Database State' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.master_files , database files stored in Azure Blob Storage. Azure SQL Database Managed Instance or SQL Server data files in Azure Blob (FILESTREAM to Azure). Informational , confirm the Azure storage tier (Premium vs Standard) matches the I/O requirements and that redundancy (LRS/ZRS/GRS) meets the recovery objectives.
FullStoryAction: There are different states for databases, in some case these databases may mean the database is not online

Name: Login Can Control Server
Description: One or more SQL Server logins have been granted the CONTROL SERVER permission. CONTROL SERVER is a highly privileged permission equivalent to sysadmin for most purposes and should only be granted when absolutely necessary.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT 104 AS [CheckID] ,
 230 AS [Priority] ,
 'Security' AS [FindingsGroup] ,
 'Login Can Control Server' AS [Finding] ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/sa' AS [URL] ,
 'Login [' + pri.[name]
 + '] has the CONTROL SERVER permission - meaning they can do absolutely anything in SQL Server, including dropping databases or hiding their tracks.' AS [Details]
FROM sys.server_principals AS pri
WHERE pri.[principal_id] IN (
 SELECT p.[grantee_principal_id]
 FROM sys.server_permissions AS p
 WHERE p.[state] IN ( 'G', 'W' )
 AND p.[class] = 100
 AND p.[type] = 'CL' )
 AND pri.[name] NOT LIKE '##%##'
 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Login Can Control Server' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Login Can Control Server' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No logins have CONTROL SERVER permission
NextAction: SERVERPROPERTY('ProcessID') checked against 64-bit indicators , or @@VERSION shows 'X86'. 32-bit SQL Server cannot access more than 4 GB of memory (with AWE enabled, up to ~3.5 GB without). Modern SQL workloads require 64-bit. This is almost certainly a legacy system that needs migration.
FullStoryAction: CONTROL SERVER permissions allows a user to do absolutely anything in SQL Server, including dropping databases or hiding their tracks.

Name: Extended Stored Procedures in Master
Description:
Reliability issue in 'Extended Stored Procedures in Master' increases the probability of silent data corruption or loss.
Without proactive detection, reliability failures compound over time.
Disaster recovery planning requires validated reliability controls.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  105 AS CheckID ,
  'master' ,
  200 AS Priority ,
  'Reliability' AS FindingGroup ,
  'Extended Stored Procedures in Master' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/clr' AS URL ,
  'The [' + name
  + '] extended stored procedure is in the master database. CLR may be in use, and the master database now needs to be part of your backup/recovery planning.'
FROM    master.sys.extended_procedures
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Extended Stored Procedures in Master' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Extended Stored Procedures in Master' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Extended Stored Procedures in Master Database
CheckClearSimplified: No Extended Stored Procedures in Master Database
NextAction: sys.objects where database_id = 1 (master) and type = 'X' (extended stored procedures) or user-created objects. Extended stored procedures in master run in-process with the SQL engine , a bug in an XP can crash the SQL Server process. Audit all non-system XPs in master: SELECT * FROM master.sys.objects WHERE type='X' AND is_ms_shipped=0.
FullStoryAction: CLR may be in use, and the master database now needs to be part of your backup/recovery planning

Name: Poison Wait Detected
Description: SQL Server is experiencing poison waits - wait types associated with severe performance problems such as THREADPOOL (thread exhaustion), RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE (memory pressure), or other critical wait conditions that indicate systemic performance issues.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  107 AS CheckID ,
  50 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingGroup ,
  'Poison Wait Detected: ' + wait_type  AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/poison/#' + wait_type AS URL ,
  CONVERT(VARCHAR(10), (SUM([wait_time_ms]) / 1000) / 86400) + ':' + CONVERT(VARCHAR(20), DATEADD(s, (SUM([wait_time_ms]) / 1000), 0), 108) + ' of this wait have been recorded. This wait often indicates killer performance problems.'
FROM sys.[dm_os_wait_stats]
WHERE wait_type IN('IO_QUEUE_LIMIT', 'IO_RETRY', 'LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR', 'POOL_LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR', 'PREEMPTIVE_DEBUG', 'RESMGR_THROTTLED', 'RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE', 'RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE_QUERY_COMPILE','SE_REPL_CATCHUP_THROTTLE','SE_REPL_COMMIT_ACK','SE_REPL_COMMIT_TURN','SE_REPL_ROLLBACK_ACK','SE_REPL_SLOW_SECONDARY_THROTTLE','THREADPOOL')
GROUP BY wait_type
HAVING SUM([wait_time_ms]) > (SELECT 5000 * datediff(HH,create_date,CURRENT_TIMESTAMP) AS hours_since_startup FROM sys.databases WHERE name='tempdb')
AND SUM([wait_time_ms]) > 60000
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Poison Wait Detected' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Poison Wait Detected' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_wait_stats CXPACKET waits combined with cost threshold for parallelism = 5 (default). CXPACKET waits indicate parallel query execution with thread imbalance. The root fix is raising the cost threshold for parallelism (CTP) from 5 to 25â€“50. Also check MAXDOP: set to number of physical cores per NUMA node (not total logical processors). Monitor via sys.dm_os_waiting_tasks WHERE wait_type = 'CXPACKET'.
FullStoryAction: Some wait types will often indicates killer performance problems. We check for records of these on the SQL server since last restart.

Name: Memory Nodes Offline
Description: One or more NUMA memory nodes are offline, meaning SQL Server cannot access all physical memory regions. Offline memory nodes reduce the total memory available to SQL Server and can cause uneven memory distribution across NUMA nodes.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (


  SELECT  110 AS CheckID ,
  50 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingGroup ,
  'Memory Nodes Offline' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/schedulers' AS URL ,
  'Due to affinity masking or licensing problems, some of the memory may not be available.' 
  WHERE EXISTS (SELECT  *
FROM sys.dm_os_nodes n
INNER JOIN sys.dm_os_memory_nodes m ON n.memory_node_id = m.memory_node_id
WHERE n.node_state_desc = 'OFFLINE')
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Memory Nodes Offline' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Memory Nodes Offline' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_nodes WHERE node_state_desc = 'OFFLINE'. A NUMA node is offline, meaning some physical memory is inaccessible to SQL Server. This reduces available buffer pool below physical RAM. Causes: hardware failure, Windows NUMA topology misconfiguration, or BIOS NUMA settings. Check Windows System event log for memory module errors.
FullStoryAction: We expect all memory nodes to be online, offline nodes may point to inaccessible CPUs due to affinity masking or licensing problems.

Name: Possibly Broken Log Shipping
Description: Log shipping appears to be configured but may not be functioning correctly. The log shipping secondary may be falling behind or restore jobs may be failing, putting the secondary database out of sync with the primary.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

  SELECT  111 AS CheckID ,
    50 AS Priority ,
    'Reliability' AS FindingGroup ,
    'Possibly Broken Log Shipping'  AS Finding ,
    d.[name] ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/shipping' AS URL ,
    d.[name] + ' is in a restoring state, but has not had a backup applied in the last two days. This is a possible indication of a broken transaction log shipping setup.'
    FROM [master].sys.databases d
    INNER JOIN [master].sys.database_mirroring dm ON d.database_id = dm.database_id
     AND dm.mirroring_role IS NULL
    WHERE ( d.[state] = 1
    OR (d.[state] = 0 AND d.[is_in_standby] = 1) )
    AND NOT EXISTS(SELECT * FROM msdb.dbo.restorehistory rh
    INNER JOIN msdb.dbo.backupset bs ON rh.backup_set_id = bs.backup_set_id
    WHERE d.[name] COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS = rh.destination_database_name COLLATE SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS
    AND rh.restore_date >= DATEADD(dd, -2, GETDATE()))
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Possibly Broken Log Shipping' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Possibly Broken Log Shipping' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: Log Shipping, if present, seems to be OK
NextAction: msdb.dbo.log_shipping_secondary or sys.databases where log_reuse_wait_desc = 'LOG_BACKUP' but no recent log backup exists. Log shipping secondary restore is failing or the backup job is not running. The secondary database is falling behind, increasing RPO exposure. Check SQL Agent jobs for log_shipping_backup_log and log_shipping_restore_log job statuses.
FullStoryAction: When a database is in a restoring state, but has not had a backup applied in the last two days. This is a possible indication of a broken transaction log shipping setup.

Name: Change Tracking Enabled
Description: Change Tracking is enabled on one or more databases. Change Tracking records row-level changes for synchronization scenarios but adds overhead to DML operations and increases storage usage in the change tracking internal tables.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 112 AS CheckID,
  100 AS Priority,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup,
  'Change Tracking Enabled' AS Finding,
  d.[name],
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/tracking' AS URL,
  ( d.[name] + ' has change tracking enabled. This is not a default setting, and it has some performance overhead. It keeps track of changes to rows in tables that have change tracking turned on.' ) AS Details 
  FROM sys.change_tracking_databases AS ctd INNER JOIN sys.databases AS d ON ctd.database_id = d.database_id
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Change Tracking Enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Change Tracking Enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No databases have Change Tracking Enabled.
NextAction: sys.databases where is_change_tracking_on = 1. Change Tracking records the primary keys of changed rows (not the data itself) in internal tables. Requires an application that actively consumes the change tracking data and calls CHANGE_TRACKING_MIN_VALID_VERSION. If not actively consumed, the internal cleanup may lag, bloating the change tracking tables. Verify the consumer application is running.
FullStoryAction: Change tracking can cause performance impacts when enabled. There are specific configuration to assist with this.

Name: Full Text Indexes Not Updating
Description: One or more full-text indexes are not being updated. Stale full-text indexes return outdated search results. This may indicate that full-text change tracking is disabled or full-text crawls are failing.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.fulltext_catalogs ftc INNER JOIN sys.fulltext_indexes fti ON ftc.fulltext_catalog_id = fti.fulltext_catalog_id INNER JOIN sys.objects o ON fti.object_id = o.object_id WHERE o.is_ms_shipped = 0 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_fts_index_population WHERE database_id = DB_ID() AND status <> 0 AND catalog_id = ftc.fulltext_catalog_id)) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Full Text Indexes Not Updating' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Full Text Indexes Not Updating' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.fulltext_catalogs or sys.fulltext_indexes joined to sys.dm_fts_index_population WHERE status = 9 (failed). Full-text index population has failed or stalled. Application searches against these indexes return stale or no results. Check sys.dm_fts_index_population for error messages. Rebuild the catalog: ALTER FULLTEXT CATALOG [name] REBUILD.
FullStoryAction: Where full text index are not being crawled often the lookup information becomes stale and can point to a problem with the Full-Text service

Name: Parallelism Rocket Surger
Description: SQL Server has unusual parallelism configuration - either MAXDOP is set too high relative to the number of processors or Cost Threshold for Parallelism is set unusually low (default 5). These settings can cause excessive parallel query execution under load.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_query_plan(qs.plan_handle) qp WHERE qp.query_plan.exist('//RelOp[@Parallel="1"]') = 1 AND qs.last_execution_time > DATEADD(hh, -4, GETDATE())) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Parallelism Rocket Surger' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Parallelism Rocket Surger' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_wait_stats , CMEMTHREAD, SOS_WORK_DISPATCHER, or CXPACKET surges indicating parallelism 'rocket surgery' , excessive parallelism causing more overhead than benefit. Check: cost threshold for parallelism (should be > 25), MAXDOP (should not be 0 on NUMA systems), and Resource Governor parallelism limits. Review sys.dm_exec_requests WHERE parallel_workers_available = 0 for saturation evidence.
FullStoryAction: Advanced Developers might decide to force queries to go parallel by way of using a make_parallel function. Best practice is to let the SQL Engine make parallel query decisions.

Name: Backup Compression Default Off
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Backup Compression Default Off' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  116 AS CheckID ,
 200 AS Priority ,
 'Informational' AS FindingGroup ,
 'Backup Compression Default Off'  AS Finding ,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/backup' AS URL ,
 'Uncompressed full backups have happened recently, and backup compression is not turned on at the server level. Backup compression is included with Standard Edition. We recommend turning backup compression on by default so that ad-hoc backups will get compressed.'
 FROM sys.configurations
 WHERE configuration_id = 1579 AND CAST(value_in_use AS INT) = 0
    AND EXISTS (SELECT * FROM msdb.dbo.backupset WHERE backup_size = compressed_backup_size AND type = 'D' AND backup_finish_date >= DATEADD(DD, -14, GETDATE()))
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Backup Compression Default Off' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Backup Compression Default Off' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'backup compression default' = 0. Backup compression is available on SQL 2008 Standard and above. Without compression, backups are larger (60â€“80% larger typically), backup windows are longer, and storage costs are higher. Enable: sp_configure 'backup compression default', 1; RECONFIGURE.
FullStoryAction: Backup compression is included with SQL Server 2008R2 & newer, even in Standard Edition.

Name: Memory Pressure Affecting Queries
Description: SQL Server is experiencing memory pressure that is affecting query performance. This may manifest as frequent memory grants being denied or queued, causing queries to wait for memory before they can execute.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

 SELECT 117 AS CheckID,
 100 AS Priority,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup,
 'Memory Pressure Affecting Queries' AS Finding,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/grants' AS URL,
 CAST(SUM(forced_grant_count) AS NVARCHAR(100)) + ' forced grants reported in the DMV sys.dm_exec_query_resource_semaphores, indicating memory pressure has affected query runtimes.'
 FROM sys.dm_exec_query_resource_semaphores WHERE [forced_grant_count] IS NOT NULL 
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Memory Pressure Affecting Queries' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Memory Pressure Affecting Queries' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_wait_stats RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE and RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE_QUERY_COMPILE waits. These waits appear when queries cannot obtain their requested memory grant , they wait in a queue. Causes: max server memory too low, single query consuming excessive memory grant, or too many concurrent parallel queries. Tune max server memory to leave 10â€“15% for OS. Monitor sys.dm_exec_query_memory_grants for queued grants.
FullStoryAction: We can isolate memory pressure by looking for Resource_semaphores Waits

Name: TDE Certificate Not Backed Up Recently
Description: TDE certificate not backed up recently. Without verified backups, this instance is one disk failure from data loss.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 119 AS CheckID,
  1 AS Priority,
  'Backup' AS FindingsGroup,
  'TDE Certificate Not Backed Up Recently' AS Finding,
  db_name(dek.database_id) AS DatabaseName,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/tde' AS URL,
  'The certificate ' + c.name + ' is used to encrypt database ' + db_name(dek.database_id) + '. Last backup date: ' + COALESCE(CAST(c.pvt_key_last_backup_date AS VARCHAR(100)), 'Never') AS Details
  FROM master.sys.certificates c INNER JOIN sys.dm_database_encryption_keys dek ON c.thumbprint = dek.encryptor_thumbprint
  WHERE pvt_key_last_backup_date IS NULL OR pvt_key_last_backup_date <= DATEADD(dd, -30, GETDATE()) 

 ) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Schedule and verify backups for TDE Certificate Not Backed Up Recently. Run RESTORE VERIFYONLY monthly on every backup file.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'TDE Certificate Not Backed Up Recently' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
BusinessTranslation: Backup gaps mean unrecoverable data. The business impact of 24 hours of lost transactions typically exceeds $100K in most industries.
CheckClearSimplified: TDE Certificates, if present, are backup up
NextAction: sys.certificates WHERE expiry_date < GETDATE() + 30 (TDE certificate expiring soon) or no recent backup of the TDE certificate in the audit log. A TDE certificate that expires does NOT immediately break encryption but a future restore would require the certificate. The critical risk: if the certificate is lost (server failure, no backup), the encrypted database files are permanently inaccessible. EXEC sp_backup_database_encryption_key to back up the TDE certificate and its private key to a secure location.
FullStoryAction: When TDE Encryption is being used on a SQL server, it is important to ensure that the certificates have been Backed Up recently.

Name: Poison Wait Detected: Serializable Sleeping
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 121' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT  121 AS CheckID ,
  50 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingGroup ,
  'Poison Wait Detected: Serializable Locking'  AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/serializable' AS URL ,
  CONVERT(VARCHAR(10), (SUM([wait_time_ms]) / 1000) / 86400) + ':' + CONVERT(VARCHAR(20), DATEADD(s, (SUM([wait_time_ms]) / 1000), 0), 108) + ' of LCK_M_R% waits have been recorded. This wait often indicates killer performance problems.'
FROM sys.[dm_os_wait_stats]
WHERE wait_type IN ('LCK_M_RS_S', 'LCK_M_RS_U', 'LCK_M_RIn_NL','LCK_M_RIn_S', 'LCK_M_RIn_U','LCK_M_RIn_X', 'LCK_M_RX_S', 'LCK_M_RX_U','LCK_M_RX_X')
HAVING SUM([wait_time_ms]) > (SELECT 5000 * datediff(HH,create_date,CURRENT_TIMESTAMP) AS hours_since_startup FROM sys.databases WHERE name='tempdb')
AND SUM([wait_time_ms]) > 60000
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 121' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 121' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_wait_stats CXPACKET waits combined with cost threshold for parallelism = 5 (default). CXPACKET waits indicate parallel query execution with thread imbalance. The root fix is raising the cost threshold for parallelism (CTP) from 5 to 25â€“50. Also check MAXDOP: set to number of physical cores per NUMA node (not total logical processors). Monitor via sys.dm_os_waiting_tasks WHERE wait_type = 'CXPACKET'.
FullStoryAction: Some wait types will often indicates killer performance problems. We check for records of these on the SQL server since last restart.

Name: User-Created Statistics In Place
Description: Manually created statistics (not generated by Auto-Create Statistics) exist on one or more tables. User-created statistics require manual updates and may become stale faster than auto-managed statistics, requiring ongoing DBA attention.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.stats WHERE user_created = 1 AND auto_created = 0) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'User-Created Statistics In Place' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'User-Created Statistics In Place' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.stats where auto_created = 0 AND user_created = 1 (non-index statistics created manually). User-created statistics are updated at the same threshold as auto-created stats but may conflict with index statistics. They add overhead to statistics update jobs. Review whether manual statistics are still needed or whether an index covers the same columns.
FullStoryAction: These might actually be slowing things down, especially during stats updates.

Name: Deadlocks Happening Daily
Description: SQL Server is experiencing deadlocks at a rate of more than one per day based on the deadlock rate since the last restart. Frequent deadlocks indicate concurrency design issues in the application that need investigation and resolution.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN (CAST(p.cntr_value AS BIGINT) / NULLIF(CAST(DATEDIFF(DD, d.create_date, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP) AS DECIMAL(23,2)), 0)) > 10 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters p INNER JOIN sys.databases d ON d.name = 'tempdb' WHERE RTRIM(p.counter_name) = 'Number of Deadlocks/sec' AND RTRIM(p.instance_name) = '_Total' AND p.cntr_value > 0
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Deadlocks Happening Daily' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Deadlocks Happening Daily' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Deadlocks are occurring. These impact application behaviour and performance. Take urgent action.
NextAction: Reads system_health Extended Events session for xml_deadlock_report events within the last 24 hours. Deadlocks indicate two or more sessions hold locks the other needs. Investigation: run sys.dm_exec_query_stats to find the queries involved; review the deadlock graph XML in sys.fn_xe_file_target_read_file for the system_health session. Mitigation: consistent index access order, shorter transactions, appropriate isolation level, or RCSI.
FullStoryAction: Deadlocks can adversely affect application behaviour

Name: Plan Cache Erased Recently
Description: The procedure cache has been cleared recently (via DBCC FREEPROCCACHE or similar). A cleared plan cache causes all subsequent queries to recompile, temporarily increasing CPU usage as execution plans are rebuilt from scratch.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(qs.plan_handle) st WHERE st.text LIKE '%DBCC FREEPROCCACHE%' AND qs.last_execution_time > DATEADD(hh, -24, GETDATE())) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Plan Cache Erased Recently' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Plan Cache Erased Recently' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_os_plan_cache_stats or DBCC FREEPROCCACHE events in the default trace. The plan cache was cleared recently (DBCC FREEPROCCACHE or ALTER DATABASE SCOPED CONFIGURATION CLEAR PROCEDURE_CACHE). Clearing the plan cache causes a CPU spike as all statements recompile simultaneously on next execution , a form of self-inflicted 'recompile storm'. Identify who ran this via the default trace.
FullStoryAction: When someone runs DBCC FREEPROCCACHE, restarts the SQL Server, or the server is under horrific memory pressure the plan cache gets cleared

Name: Priority Boost Enabled
Description: SQL Server's Priority Boost option is enabled, which raises the SQL Server process priority in the Windows scheduler. This can cause the SQL Server process to starve the OS and other processes of CPU time, potentially causing system instability or crashes.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (

SELECT 126, 5, 'Reliability', 'Priority Boost Enabled', 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/priorityboost/',
        'Priority Boost sounds awesome, but it can actually cause your SQL Server to crash.'
FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'priority boost' AND (value = 1 OR value_in_use = 1)
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Priority Boost Enabled' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Priority Boost Enabled' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sp_configure 'priority boost' = 1. Priority boost raises the SQL Server process Windows scheduling priority from Normal to High. This can starve other OS processes (including the Network stack) of CPU, causing the OS to become unresponsive. Microsoft explicitly recommends against enabling priority boost. Disable: sp_configure 'priority boost', 0; RECONFIGURE.
FullStoryAction: Priority Boost sounds awesome, but it can actually cause your SQL Server to crash.

Name: Old Compatibility Level Detected
Description:
Non-compliant configuration for 'Check 128' can lead to unexpected behavior during failover or recovery.
Audit findings will flag this deviation, requiring documented exception or remediation.
Configuration drift increases operational risk and complicates disaster recovery planning.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE compatibility_level < 130 AND state = 0 AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Check 128' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Check 128' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No databases have old Compatibility Levels
NextAction: sys.databases where compatibility_level is more than one version behind the instance. Very old compatibility levels (e.g. 80, 90) prevent access to critical performance features. sp_Blitz flags databases still on SQL 2000 or 2005 compat mode. These databases cannot use parallel DML, filtered indexes, or modern join optimisations.
FullStoryAction: These databases cannot use optimizations on the current SQL server

Name: High Memory Use for In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton)
Description: In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) tables are consuming a high percentage of the SQL Server memory grant. When In-Memory OLTP usage approaches the configured memory limit, the instance can begin to reject transactions with out-of-memory errors.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_db_xtp_memory_consumers WHERE memory_consumer_type_desc = 'VARHEAP' AND allocated_bytes > 0) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'High Memory Use for In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'High Memory Use for In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: High Memory Use for In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton)
CheckClearSimplified: In-Memory OLTP memory usage, where present, seems OK
NextAction: sys.dm_os_memory_clerks WHERE type = 'MEMORYCLERK_XTP' , In-Memory OLTP memory clerk consuming excessive RAM. Memory-optimised tables hold all data in RAM permanently (no eviction to disk). If a memory-optimised table grows beyond available memory, SQL Server cannot accept inserts. Monitor memory usage: SELECT * FROM sys.dm_db_xtp_memory_consumers ORDER BY allocated_bytes DESC.
FullStoryAction: Microsoft recommends having 2X your Hekaton table space available in memory just for Hekaton, with a max of 250GB of in-memory data regardless of your server memory capacity

Name: In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) In Use
Description: In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) tables or natively compiled stored procedures are in use on this instance. This is informational to highlight the presence of this feature, which has specific backup, HA, and monitoring requirements.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_db_xtp_memory_consumers) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) In Use' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) In Use' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.tables where is_memory_optimized = 1. In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) tables are present. Informational , confirms the feature is in use. Key management items: (1) MEMORY_OPTIMIZED_DATA filegroup must be on reliable fast storage, (2) checkpoint pairs accumulate until the merge process runs, (3) natively compiled procedures have limited T-SQL surface area.
FullStoryAction: Performance - In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) allows certain tables to reside In-Memory. This can greatly improve OLTP performance.

Name: In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) Transaction Errors
Description: In-Memory OLTP is generating transaction errors, such as write conflicts or out-of-memory conditions. These errors indicate that In-Memory OLTP workloads are exceeding configured capacity or encountering concurrency issues.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.dm_db_xtp_transactions) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) Transaction Errors' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'In-Memory OLTP (Hekaton) Transaction Errors' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
NextAction: sys.dm_db_xtp_transactions WHERE transaction_id > 0 AND result = 'ABORTED_DUE_TO_CONFLICT'. In-Memory OLTP optimistic concurrency causes transaction aborts when write-write conflicts occur. High abort rates indicate two sessions frequently modifying the same rows. Review the application's transaction patterns , use SNAPSHOT isolation consistently in the application layer.
FullStoryAction: In-Memory OLTP Transactions can cause problems. These issues are tracked in the SQL engine and can be queried to find more information.

Name: Database Files on Network File Shares
Description: One or more database files reside on network file shares (UNC paths). Network file shares introduce latency, are susceptible to network disruptions, and require specific SQL Server service account permissions, creating availability risks.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN
     EXISTS (
SELECT DISTINCT 148 AS CheckID ,
         d.[name] AS DatabaseName ,
         170 AS Priority ,
         'Reliability' AS FindingsGroup ,
         'Database Files on Network File Shares' AS Finding ,
         'https://www.brentozar.com/go/nas' AS URL ,
         ( 'Files for this database are on: ' + LEFT(mf.physical_name, 30)) AS Details
 FROM    sys.databases d
   INNER JOIN sys.master_files mf ON d.database_id = mf.database_id
 WHERE mf.physical_name LIKE '\\%'  
     )
        THEN 1
     ELSE 0
    END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Database Files on Network File Shares' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Database Files on Network File Shares' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Database Files on Network File Shares
CheckClearSimplified: All Database Files on normal locations
NextAction: sys.master_files WHERE physical_name LIKE '\%' (UNC network paths). Database files on network file shares introduce network latency into every SQL I/O operation. If the network path is unavailable, the database goes offline. Only certified file share configurations (SMB 3.0 with WSFC-aware NAS) are supported. Use direct-attached or SAN storage whenever possible.
FullStoryAction: Although it is possible to run SQL Databases with the database Files on Network File Share it is not a supportable configuration.

Name: Database Files Stored in Azure
Description: Detects database files that are stored directly on Azure blob storage via a URL path. While Azure blob storage is a supported database file location in SQL Server, it may introduce latency and reliability concerns compared to locally attached managed disks or premium storage. This check surfaces databases using URL-based storage so you can verify it aligns with your performance and DR requirements.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(
    SELECT 1
    FROM sys.databases d
    INNER JOIN sys.master_files mf ON d.database_id = mf.database_id
    WHERE mf.physical_name LIKE 'http://%'
) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction:
Verify that Azure blob storage meets the performance requirements for database files.
Ensure network connectivity and redundancy measures are in place for Azure-based files.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no database files are stored on Azure blob via URL. Returns FAIL when URL-based files exist.
CheckClearSimplified: All Database Files on normal locations
NextAction: sys.master_files WHERE physical_name LIKE 'https:%'. Database files stored in Azure Blob Storage (remote storage). SQL Server 2012+ supports storing .mdf/.ldf in Azure Blob for specific cloud scenarios. Latency is higher than local SSD. Ensure the Azure Premium storage tier is used for data and log files. Monitor io_stall via sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats.
FullStoryAction: Although it is possible to run SQL Databases with the database Files on Azure blob storage, it is not a supportable configuration.

Name: Errors Logged Recently in the Default Trace
Description: SQL Server's default trace has recorded errors or significant events recently. The default trace captures events such as login failures, configuration changes, and database growth events that may indicate underlying issues.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.fn_trace_gettable((SELECT TOP 1 path FROM sys.traces WHERE is_default = 1), DEFAULT) WHERE EventClass IN(55, 56, 57, 58, 59, 60) AND StartTime > DATEADD(dd, -7, GETDATE())) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Errors Logged Recently in the Default Trace' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Errors Logged Recently in the Default Trace' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ Errors found in the SQL Trace Log
CheckClearSimplified: No Errors found in the SQL Trace Log
NextAction: Reads sys.dm_os_ring_buffers WHERE ring_buffer_type = 'RING_BUFFER_EXCEPTION' and the SQL Server error log via xp_readerrorlog for severity >= 17 errors. Recent high-severity errors (errors 17â€“25) indicate resource exhaustion or internal failures. Investigate via SELECT * FROM sys.dm_os_ring_buffers WHERE ring_buffer_type = 'RING_BUFFER_EXCEPTION'.
FullStoryAction: The Errors Logged by in the SQL server Logs needs to be monitored.

Name: File Growths Slow
Description: Detects database file autogrowth events that took longer than 15 seconds to complete. Slow autogrowth events can cause application timeouts and transaction failures because SQL Server pauses all write activity to the affected file until the growth completes. Frequent or slow file growths indicate that files should be larger or use instant file initialization to speed up growth operations.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(
    SELECT 1
    FROM sys.traces t
    CROSS APPLY sys.fn_trace_gettable(t.[path], t.is_rollover) ft
    WHERE t.is_default = 1
      AND ft.EventClass IN (92, 93)
      AND ft.Duration > 15000000
      AND ft.StartTime > DATEADD(dd, -30, GETDATE())
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction:
Pre-size database files to an appropriate size to minimize autogrowth events.
Enable Instant File Initialization (IFI) to speed up data file growth operations.
Set autogrowth to a fixed MB amount rather than a percentage.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no slow file growth events are detected. Returns FAIL when growths exceed 15 seconds.
NextAction: sys.dm_io_pending_io_requests or sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats , autogrow events taking more than the expected duration. Slow autogrow events block the session that triggered them (data files without IFI, all log files). Prevent autogrow in production: pre-size files and set autogrow only as a safety net with a fixed large increment. Monitor with sys.dm_io_pending_io_requests during off-hours expansion.
FullStoryAction: When database file growths takes more than 15 seconds each it is best to consider setting file autogrowth to a smaller increments

Name: 32-bit SQL Server Installed
Description: SQL Server is running as a 32-bit process. 32-bit SQL Server has a maximum addressable memory limit of approximately 4GB (with PAE extensions), which is severely limiting for any production workload and prevents use of modern hardware capabilities.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
SELECT  154 AS CheckID ,
  10 AS Priority ,
  'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
  '32-bit SQL Server Installed' AS Finding ,
  'https://www.brentozar.com/go/32bit' AS URL ,
  ( 'This server uses the 32-bit x86 binaries for SQL Server instead of the 64-bit x64 binaries. The amount of memory available for query workspace and execution plans is heavily limited.' ) AS Details
WHERE   CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('edition') AS VARCHAR(100)) NOT LIKE '%64%'
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding '32-bit SQL Server Installed' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of '32-bit SQL Server Installed' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: All SQL servers have the 64-bit x64 binaries
NextAction: @@VERSION contains 'X86' or SERVERPROPERTY('ProcessID') resolves to a 32-bit process. 32-bit SQL Server is a legacy concern. Maximum addressable memory is 4 GB (2 GB user space without /3GB switch). All modern SQL Server versions are 64-bit. This system needs immediate migration.
FullStoryAction: The amount of memory available for query workspace and execution plans is impacted by the binary architecture of the SQL server

Name: Dangerous Build of SQL Server (Security)
Description: SQL Server is running on a build that has known security vulnerabilities. Microsoft has released patches or cumulative updates that address security issues in this build. Applying the latest CU is recommended to protect against known exploits.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.databases WHERE compatibility_level < 130 AND state = 0) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Dangerous Build of SQL Server (Security)' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Dangerous Build of SQL Server (Security)' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: @ server(s) have a Dangerous Build of SQL Server (Security)
NextAction: @@VERSION compared against a list of builds with known critical security CVEs. A build with a critical security vulnerability is flagged. Check Microsoft Security Bulletins for the specific CVE numbers. Apply the relevant security patch (GDR release) or full CU immediately.
FullStoryAction: Security. There are dangerous known bugs for certain SQL versions

Name: File growth set to 1MB
Description: One or more database files are configured to grow in 1MB increments. For any database larger than a few gigabytes, 1MB growth events are extremely small, causing frequent growth events that each stall I/O until the file system allocates the new space.
SqlQuery: SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM sys.master_files WHERE growth = 1 AND is_percent_growth = 0 AND database_id > 4) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'File growth set to 1MB' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'File growth set to 1MB' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckTriggeredSimplified: Database File growth set to 1MB for @ databases
CheckClearSimplified: No databases have Database File growth set to 1MB
NextAction: sys.master_files where growth = 1024 KB (1 MB autogrow). 1 MB autogrow creates a new VLF for every megabyte of log growth, resulting in thousands of VLFs over time (DBCC LOGINFO). High VLF count causes slow database startup recovery and log backup overhead. Correct: ALTER DATABASE [db] MODIFY FILE (NAME='...', FILEGROWTH=512MB) for log files. Then cycle log to reclaim VLFs.
FullStoryAction: This can lead to slow performance during growths

Name: Memory Dangerously Low in NUMA Nodes
Description: This check identifies memory dangerously low in numa nodes'. This check identifies memory dangerously low in numa nodes'. Check 159 (sp_Blitz CheckID 159, Priority 100)
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE 
WHEN EXISTS (
    SELECT DISTINCT 159 AS CheckID ,
    1 AS Priority ,
    'Performance' AS FindingsGroup ,
    'Memory Dangerously Low in NUMA Nodes' AS Finding ,
    'https://www.brentozar.com/go/max' AS URL ,
    'At least one NUMA node is reporting THREAD_RESOURCES_LOW in sys.dm_os_nodes and can no longer create threads.' AS Details
    FROM    sys.dm_os_nodes m
    WHERE   node_state_desc LIKE '%THREAD_RESOURCES_LOW%'
) THEN 1
 ELSE 0
END
RecommendedAction: Investigate the finding 'Memory Dangerously Low in NUMA Nodes'' and address the root cause. Consult the recommendations in technical_details for specific remediation steps.
ExpectedResult: Returns PASS when no instances of 'Memory Dangerously Low in NUMA Nodes'' are detected. Returns FAIL or WARN when the condition exists and requires attention.
CheckClearSimplified: No Numa nodes reporting thread issues
NextAction: sys.dm_os_nodes WHERE memory_node_id > 0 and online_scheduler_count = 0 or memory_utilization_percentage > 95. A NUMA node has critically low available memory. When one NUMA node is memory-starved, local allocation fails and remote NUMA allocation occurs , increasing memory access latency significantly. Investigate memory pressure on the specific NUMA node via sys.dm_os_memory_nodes.
FullStoryAction: When a NUMA node is reporting THREAD_RESOURCES_LOW , no more threads can be created

Name: Many Plans for One Query
Description: One or more queries have an unusually high number of execution plans cached. This indicates query plan instability, often caused by parameter sniffing issues or varying SET options between connections leading to excessive plan compilation overhead.
SqlQuery:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (SELECT * FROM sys.all_columns WHERE name = 'query_hash')
     AND EXISTS (
SELECT TOP 1 160 AS CheckID,
 100 AS Priority,
 'Performance' AS FindingsGroup,
 'Many Plans for One Query' AS Finding,
 'https://www.brentozar.com/go/parameterization' AS URL,
 CAST(COUNT(DISTINCT plan_handle) AS NVARCHAR(50)) + ' plans are present for a single query in the plan cache - meaning we probably have parameterization issues.' AS Details
 FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs
    CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_plan_attributes(qs.plan_handle) pa
    WHERE pa.attribute = 'dbid'
    GROUP BY qs.query_hash, pa.value
